title: Protocol for evaluating ChatGPT in biomedical association generation and verification using a RAG-enabled, cross-model majority voting workflow
comment: Main Manuscript and Supplementary Information. Both are equally important
summary: We present a protocol to evaluate ChatGPT's ability to generate disease-centric biomedical associations. It outlines how we generate the associations, validate the biological entities using biomedical ontologies, and verify associations using literature. The protocol includes a self-consistency strategy to assess generative reliability across ChatGPT models. To address ontology exact-match limitations, we provide a use case performing semantic verification through a workflow enabled by Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) powered by open-source large language models (LLMs). This enables LLMs to establish truth over content generated by other LLMs and expose hallucination.

AI.tldr: Protocol to test how well ChatGPT and other LLMs generate and verify disease-centric biomedical associations, using ontologies, literature checks, self-consistency, and RAG-based semantic verification to detect hallucinations.
AI.motivation: LLMs like ChatGPT can propose connections between diseases, genes, drugs, and other biomedical entities, but their accuracy, reliability, and tendency to hallucinate are not well-characterized. Traditional ontology-based validation requires exact matches and misses semantically close but non-identical terms. There is a need for a systematic, reproducible way to generate, validate, and cross-check LLM-derived biomedical associations and to leverage other LLMs plus external knowledge sources to detect errors.
AI.method: The authors design a protocol with several components: (1) prompting ChatGPT to generate disease-centric biomedical associations (e.g., disease–gene, disease–drug); (2) validating named entities by mapping them to established biomedical ontologies; (3) verifying associations against the published literature; (4) applying a self-consistency strategy that compares outputs across different ChatGPT models or multiple runs to evaluate generative reliability; and (5) demonstrating a RAG-based workflow, using open-source LLMs that query external knowledge bases and documents to perform semantic rather than exact-string verification of entities and associations, thereby identifying hallucinated content in the original LLM output.
AI.result: The protocol formalizes how to systematically evaluate LLM-generated biomedical associations and illustrates that combining ontology mapping, literature verification, and self-consistency checks can reveal discrepancies and hallucinations. The RAG-based semantic verification use case shows that open-source LLMs, supported by external knowledge retrieval, can effectively assess and sometimes correct associations produced by other LLMs, overcoming limitations of exact-match ontology checks.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a structured evaluation framework for disease-centric biomedical associations generated by ChatGPT and other LLMs. By integrating ontology-based validation, literature-backed verification, self-consistency analysis, and RAG-enabled semantic checking, the protocol offers a practical way to quantify reliability and uncover hallucinations. It also highlights the potential of using one LLM, augmented with external knowledge, to independently audit and validate content produced by another LLM in biomedical research workflows.

title: Exploring Autonomous Agentic Data Engineering for Model Specialization
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong performance on general tasks, while often struggling to adapt to specialized domains without high-quality domain-specific data. Existing LLM-based data curation methods primarily rely on human-designed workflows, leaving it unexamined whether LLMs can autonomously execute an end-to-end data engineering pipeline for model specialization. We formalize \textbf{Autonomous Agentic Data Engineering}, a novel task designed to evaluate LLMs as autonomous data engineers that drive model specialization through end-to-end data curation. We frame data as an optimizable component and study agents that plan, generate, and iteratively optimize training data across multiple domains, guided by post-training performance improvement. Experiments show that autonomous LLM data engineers yield substantial gains, as GPT-5.2 constructs a training curriculum that improves a student model by \textbf{57.29\%}, entirely through iterative, agent-driven data adaptation. By illuminating both potential and bottlenecks, our study establishes autonomous data engineering as a measurable capability and charts a path toward agent-driven model specialization\footnote{Code will be released at https://github.com/zjunlp/DataAgent.}.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and evaluates autonomous LLM-based agents that can perform end-to-end data engineering to specialize models without relying on human-designed data curation workflows.
AI.motivation: While LLMs perform well on general tasks, they struggle in specialized domains without high-quality domain-specific data. Current LLM-based data curation approaches depend heavily on human-designed workflows and do not test whether LLMs can independently manage the full data engineering pipeline. The authors want to understand if LLMs themselves can autonomously plan, generate, and refine training data to specialize models across domains.
AI.method: The authors formalize a new task called Autonomous Agentic Data Engineering, in which LLMs act as autonomous data engineers. They design agent systems that treat data as an optimizable component and allow the agent to iteratively plan, generate, and optimize training datasets across multiple domains. The agents’ decisions are guided by observing post-training performance improvements of a student model, effectively creating a feedback loop where the LLM refines the training curriculum over iterations.
AI.result: Experiments show that the autonomous LLM data engineer (based on GPT-5.2) can construct a training curriculum that substantially improves the performance of a student model. Specifically, the paper reports a 57.29% performance gain achieved purely through iterative, agent-driven data adaptation, without manual human workflow design for the data pipeline.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that LLMs can act as autonomous data engineers capable of end-to-end data curation for model specialization, achieving large performance gains. It identifies both the potential and limitations of this paradigm, and proposes autonomous data engineering as a measurable capability for LLMs, outlining a path toward more fully agent-driven model specialization in the future.

title: Domain Adaptation and Reasoning Frameworks in Language Models: A Controlled Experiment with Historical Cosmology
summary: We investigate how domain adaptation reshapes explanatory behavior in language models using historical cosmology as a controlled setting. In Phase 1, we train a small language model from scratch on a pre-Copernican corpus from which explicit heliocentric references were removed, and evaluate whether Earth-motion or heliocentric continuations nevertheless emerge. In Phase 2, we fine-tune a larger pretrained model using QLoRA on the same corpus in order to study how adaptation modifies explanatory framing and cosmological stance. Model outputs are evaluated using an LLM-as-judge framework that labels both cosmological stance (geocentric, heliocentric, or ambiguous) and explanatory frame (premodern versus modern). In the constrained setting of Phase 1, the smaller models occasionally generate local Earth-motion continuations, but these remain globally unstable and insufficient to support coherent cosmological reasoning. In Phase 2, fine-tuning induces a large and statistically significant shift toward premodern explanatory framing, while the conditional cosmological stance distributions remain comparatively stable within those frames. As a result, increases in geocentric outputs arise primarily from redistribution over explanatory regimes rather than from direct modification of stance. These results suggest that domain adaptation may primarily reshape the linguistic frameworks from which continuations are generated, with changes in stance emerging secondarily from those shifts.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how domain adaptation changes the explanatory style and cosmological stance of language models using historical cosmology as a controlled testbed.
AI.motivation: To understand whether fine-tuning language models on domain-specific, historically-situated texts mainly alters factual stances (e.g., geocentric vs. heliocentric) or primarily reshapes the explanatory frameworks and linguistic style they use. Historical cosmology offers a controlled setting where premodern vs. modern explanatory regimes and geocentric vs. heliocentric stances can be disentangled.
AI.method: Phase 1: Train a small language model from scratch on a pre-Copernican corpus that has explicit heliocentric references removed; evaluate if the model still produces Earth-motion or heliocentric continuations. Phase 2: Fine-tune a larger pretrained model using QLoRA on the same corpus to study how domain adaptation affects explanatory framing (premodern vs. modern) and cosmological stance (geocentric, heliocentric, ambiguous). Use an LLM-as-judge evaluation pipeline to label model outputs for stance and explanatory frame, and analyze distributions and shifts due to training/fine-tuning.
AI.result: In Phase 1, small models occasionally generate local mentions of Earth’s motion, but these are unstable and do not support a coherent heliocentric cosmology. In Phase 2, fine-tuning produces a pronounced, statistically significant shift toward premodern explanatory framing, while the conditional distributions over cosmological stance within each explanatory frame remain relatively stable. The observed increase in geocentric outputs is primarily due to more generations falling into premodern explanatory regimes, not because the stance itself is directly changed within a given regime.
AI.conclusion: Domain adaptation primarily reshapes the explanatory and linguistic frameworks through which language models generate text, rather than directly overwriting underlying cosmological stance. Changes in explicit stance (e.g., more geocentric answers) emerge largely as a secondary effect of shifting the explanatory regime (premodern vs. modern) that the model employs when producing continuations.

title: Cross-Lingual Steering for Figurative Language Generation
summary: Multilingual large language models can generate figurative language, but whether the internal signals driving this behavior are language-specific or reusable across languages is unclear. Using activation steering as a probe, we estimate a direction for a figurative category from figurative--literal activation differences in one language and apply it during generation. Across five figurative categories, six languages, and four multilingual LLMs, these directions steer reliably within their own language, most robustly for metaphor and simile. More importantly, they transfer across languages: a direction learned in one increases the target behavior when applied to another, with German among the most receptive targets. Going further, directions assembled from other languages can match or even surpass a target language's own native direction, while removing this shared component weakens native steering. Together, these results provide direct evidence of a reusable but target-dependent cross-lingual signal for figurative generation.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether the internal mechanisms that enable multilingual LLMs to generate figurative language are language-specific or shared across languages, using activation steering to test cross-lingual reusability of figurative-language signals.
AI.motivation: While multilingual LLMs can produce figurative language (e.g., metaphors, similes) in many languages, it is unclear if the representations underlying this ability are separate for each language or if there is a common, reusable component. Understanding this matters for interpretability of multilingual models and for improving controllable generation of figurative language in low-resource or less-studied languages.
AI.method: The authors use activation steering as a probing method. For each figurative category and language, they compute an activation direction based on the difference between figurative and literal activations inside the model. They then apply this direction during text generation to see if it increases or decreases figurative language. They run experiments on five figurative categories, six languages, and four multilingual LLMs, testing both within-language steering and cross-language transfer, including compositional directions built from multiple source languages and ablation of shared components.
AI.result: Within a given language, learned directions reliably steer generation toward the desired figurative category, with the strongest and most consistent effects for metaphor and simile. Crucially, directions learned in one language successfully transfer to others: applying a direction extracted from a source language boosts figurative behavior in a different target language, with some languages such as German being especially responsive. Furthermore, combining directions from multiple source languages can equal or outperform a direction learned directly in the target language, and subtracting this shared cross-lingual component degrades the target language’s own steering performance.
AI.conclusion: The findings show that multilingual LLMs contain a reusable, cross-lingual signal that underlies figurative language generation, though its expression still depends on properties of each target language. This provides direct evidence that at least part of figurative-language circuitry is shared across languages rather than fully language-specific, and it demonstrates that activation steering can exploit this shared structure for controllable, cross-lingual figurative generation.

title: PhyDrawGen: Physically Grounded Diagram Generation from Natural Language
comment: 9 figures, 7 tables. Under review at EMNLP 2026
summary: Generating physics diagrams from text requires strict adherence to physical laws. While current generative models produce visually plausible outputs, they systematically hallucinate force vectors, ignore conservation laws, and violate geometric constraints. We present PhyDrawGen, a neuro-symbolic pipeline that decouples semantic scene understanding from physical constraint satisfaction. First, a large language model extracts a typed scene graph from the problem text. A deterministic solver then converts this graph into a Planar Straight-Line Graph (PSLG), encoding force balance, optical paths, and field topologies as exact geometric primitives. Finally, a fine-tuned Qwen-VL model implements a visually grounded propose-verify loop to iteratively correct any constraint violations. Evaluated on a benchmark of 1,449 problems spanning mechanics, optics, and electromagnetism, PhyDrawGen significantly outperforms GPT-5-image, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 3 Pro, demonstrating robust physical accuracy even on unusual-object problems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PhyDrawGen, a neuro-symbolic system for generating physics diagrams from text that rigorously enforces physical laws, outperforming existing image generative models on physical accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing generative models can draw physics diagrams that look plausible but frequently break real physical laws: they hallucinate or misplace forces, ignore conservation, and violate geometric or field constraints. There is a need for a diagram generation system that is both visually coherent and strictly physically correct, especially for education and assessment settings.
AI.method: The authors propose PhyDrawGen, a three-stage neuro-symbolic pipeline. (1) A large language model parses the textual physics problem into a typed scene graph encoding entities and relations. (2) A deterministic solver transforms this scene graph into a Planar Straight-Line Graph (PSLG) where mechanics, optics, and electromagnetism constraints (force balance, optical paths, field topology) are represented as exact geometric primitives and enforced symbolically. (3) A fine-tuned Qwen-VL vision-language model runs a propose-verify loop to render the final diagram and iteratively correct any remaining violations of physical constraints.
AI.result: On a benchmark of 1,449 physics problems across mechanics, optics, and electromagnetism, PhyDrawGen is empirically compared with strong image-generation baselines such as GPT-5-image, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 3 Pro. It achieves substantially higher physical correctness, including for problems involving unusual or less common objects, indicating superior robustness and generalization in enforcing physics laws.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling semantic understanding from symbolic physical reasoning and combining them via a neuro-symbolic pipeline enables significantly more physically accurate diagram generation from text. PhyDrawGen shows that enforcing explicit physical constraints and using an iterative propose-verify loop yields diagrams that are both visually plausible and faithful to underlying physics, surpassing leading generative models and providing a promising direction for educational and scientific diagram synthesis.

title: Can LLM Teams Play What? Where? When?
summary: Large language models (LLMs) remain limited on tasks requiring indirect reasoning, cultural knowledge, and coordinated hypothesis testing. We investigate whether team-based interaction improves LLM performance in What? Where? When? (ChGK), a quiz game designed to reward collective reasoning. We introduce three team strategies: Voting, Silent Team (the captain observes final answers), and Talkative Team (the captain observes both answers and rationales). To minimize data leakage, we evaluate these strategies on a dataset consisting of 572 ChGK questions released in 2025. Using six recent large-scale open models, we show that team-based strategies outperform single-model baselines, yielding gains of up to 20 percentage points in accuracy. The best team achieves 44.23% accuracy, and approaches human team performance on questions with available human statistics. Analysis of inter-model diversity reveals that disagreement strongly predicts lower accuracy, but explanatory communication substantially mitigates performance drops. We further examine captain behavior and find no evidence of self-preference bias; access to peer rationales improves captain judgments. Overall, LLM teams function primarily as answer selection and error-filtering mechanisms rather than generators of novel solutions. Our findings highlight the importance of interaction and suggest adaptive strategies as a promising direction for multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether organizing multiple LLMs into ‘teams’ can improve performance on a complex quiz game requiring reasoning and cultural knowledge, and finds that team strategies significantly outperform single models.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with indirect reasoning, cultural context, and hypothesis testing. Human teams often excel at such tasks via collaboration and debate. The paper is motivated by the question of whether similar team-based interaction patterns can boost LLM performance, especially on a demanding quiz game (What? Where? When?) that rewards collective reasoning.
AI.method: The authors construct a benchmark of 572 What? Where? When? (ChGK) questions released in 2025 to avoid training leakage. They evaluate six recent large open-source LLMs under several configurations: single-model baselines and three multi-model ‘team’ strategies—(1) Voting (independent answers aggregated), (2) Silent Team (a captain model sees only the final answers of peers), and (3) Talkative Team (the captain also sees peers’ rationales). They compare accuracies, analyze inter-model disagreement, study how access to explanations affects the captain’s decisions, and inspect whether the captain exhibits self-preference bias.
AI.result: Team-based strategies deliver up to 20 percentage point accuracy improvements over single-model setups. The best-performing team reaches 44.23% accuracy, approaching human team performance on questions where human statistics are available. Disagreement among models correlates with lower accuracy, but when the captain can read peers’ rationales (Talkative Team), the negative impact of disagreement is reduced. Captains show no self-preference bias, and their access to peer rationales improves their judgment quality.
AI.conclusion: LLM teams enhance performance primarily by selecting among and filtering candidate answers rather than by creating fundamentally new solutions. Structured interaction, especially with shared explanations, is beneficial, and adaptive multi-agent strategies are a promising avenue for improving LLM reasoning on complex, culturally grounded tasks.

title: Physically Viable World Models: A Case for Query-Conditioned Embodied AI
comment: 21 pages; Adam J. Thorpe and Stepan Tretiakov contributed equally
summary: World models for embodied AI must be physically viable: constructed to answer intervention queries by representing the physical structure governing action outcomes, rather than merely predicting future observations. Existing observation-predictive world models can produce visually plausible but physically wrong rollouts. This failure is structural; distinct physical systems can look identical yet diverge under intervention. We expose this problem with controlled benchmarks that fix the visible scene while varying latent physics. We show that such models may recommend infeasible actions, mispredict interaction outcomes, or certify unsafe behavior. We argue that embodied AI requires world models that identify the simplest physical abstraction sufficient to answer an intervention query. Such a model comprises modular components, including environment representation, latent state and parameter estimation, action specification, interventional dynamics, and query-level response. An autonomous orchestrator should identify the relevant abstraction and compose compatible learned and structured components per query. When closed-form physics is unavailable, uncertain, or costly, the transition model may be analytic, simulated, learned, or hybrid, but it must preserve the structure that determines interventional outcomes. This decomposition makes the model interpretable, its components verifiable, and its outputs auditable against the query. It also provides a design principle for new world models and a feasibility test for existing ones: the right abstraction is not the most detailed model of the world, but the simplest model that preserves the distinctions relevant to the query. We demonstrate this approach on queries that existing systems fail to answer correctly, and outline how an orchestrator can dynamically assemble and adapt physically viable models for planning, control, and verification.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that world models for embodied AI must be designed to answer intervention queries by capturing underlying physical structure, not just predicting future observations, and proposes a modular, abstraction-focused framework plus benchmarks and demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Current world models in embodied AI mostly focus on predicting future observations (e.g., images) and can produce visually plausible but physically incorrect rollouts. Because different physical systems can produce identical observations yet respond differently to interventions, such models can fail badly in planning and control: they may suggest infeasible actions, mispredict interactions, or claim unsafe behaviors are safe. There is a need for models that explicitly respect physical structure so they can correctly support counterfactual and interventional reasoning required for safe and effective embodied AI.
AI.method: The paper introduces controlled benchmarks where the visible scene is held fixed but underlying latent physics vary, exposing that purely observation-predictive models cannot distinguish these cases and thus fail on intervention queries. It then proposes a design framework for "physically viable" world models built around the notion of choosing the simplest physical abstraction that still suffices to answer a given intervention query. The framework decomposes a world model into modular components: environment representation, latent state and parameter estimation, action specification, interventional dynamics, and query-level response. An autonomous orchestrator dynamically selects the appropriate abstraction and composes compatible learned, simulated, analytic, or hybrid transition models depending on the query and the availability of closed-form physics, while preserving the structure that governs interventional outcomes.
AI.result: Empirically, the paper demonstrates scenarios and queries where standard observation-predictive world models fail—recommending impossible actions, mispredicting interactions, or mis-certifying safety—while the proposed structured, abstraction-aware models succeed. The benchmarks illustrate how fixing the observed scene while varying latent physics breaks naive predictive models, and the orchestrator-based approach correctly adapts the model components to align with the intervention being queried. These results validate that enforcing physical structure and query-relevant abstraction improves reliability for planning, control, and verification tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that for embodied AI, it is not enough for world models to generate realistic predictions; they must be physically viable, meaning they preserve the structural distinctions that matter for interventions. The right abstraction is defined as the simplest model that still captures all physical differences relevant to the query, rather than the most detailed possible model. A modular decomposition plus an autonomous orchestrator yields interpretable, verifiable, and auditable world models, and provides both a design guideline for future models and a feasibility test for assessing existing ones on their ability to support interventional reasoning.

title: Knowledge Graph-Enhanced Zero-Shot Topic Classification: A Multi-Strategy Comparative Study
comment: 15 pages, 1 figure, ACL format. This paper proposes a KG-augmented zero-shot multi-label topic classification framework and evaluates multiple strategies
summary: Multi-label topic classification without labeled training data is a challenging task, specially when documents contain complex relational information. We present a zero-shot multi-label topic classification framework and systematically investigate how per-article knowledge graph augmentation affects its performance. The base framework classifies topics in documents without labeled training data and has four variants: article-only classification, keyword-enhanced classification, and self-consistency decoding variants of both. Then, we augment each base variant with per article knowledge graph. This graph is extracted from the input document through a pipeline similar to KGGen based on subject-predicate-object triples. We test all eight methods, four base and four graph augmented on fifteen LLMs and eight multi-label datasets across different domains. For the base framework, keyword-enhanced classification (AK) is the best performing method, and six out of fifteen LLMs surpass the sentence-encoder baseline. Graph augmentation has positive and negative impacts on small and large models, respectively. This shows that larger models already contain enough relational information from pretraining. Furthermore, the self-consistency decoding variant does not show performance improvements in any experiment while increasing computation costs about fivefold.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and evaluates a zero-shot, multi-label topic classification framework that uses large language models, keyword prompts, self-consistency decoding, and per-article knowledge graph augmentation, showing that keyword prompting helps, knowledge graphs help small models but hurt large ones, and self-consistency adds cost without gains.
AI.motivation: Multi-label topic classification typically requires labeled training data, which is costly to obtain, and becomes harder when documents contain complex relational information that existing approaches struggle to exploit. With the rise of large language models and automatic knowledge graph construction, there is a need to understand how to perform zero-shot multi-label topic classification and whether augmenting LLM prompts with structured relational knowledge can improve performance across models and domains.
AI.method: The authors design a zero-shot multi-label topic classification framework with four base variants that operate without any labeled training data: (1) article-only classification using the raw document as input to LLMs, (2) keyword-enhanced classification that supplements the document with topic-related keywords, and (3–4) self-consistency decoding versions of both, which generate multiple predictions and aggregate them. They then augment each base variant with a per-article knowledge graph constructed from subject–predicate–object triples extracted via a KGGen-like pipeline, injecting this structured graph into the LLM’s input. This yields eight methods in total, which they systematically test on fifteen different LLMs and eight multi-label datasets from diverse domains, comparing against a sentence-encoder baseline and measuring the impact of keyword prompting, graph augmentation, and self-consistency decoding.
AI.result: Across the eight datasets and fifteen LLMs, the keyword-enhanced classification (AK) is the strongest among the base zero-shot methods, and for six out of fifteen LLMs, it outperforms a sentence-encoder baseline. Knowledge graph augmentation yields mixed effects: it improves performance for smaller LLMs but degrades performance for larger ones, indicating that large models already encode substantial relational knowledge from pretraining and may be overloaded or distracted by additional structured input. The self-consistency decoding variants fail to produce performance gains in any experiment, despite increasing computation cost by roughly a factor of five.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot multi-label topic classification with LLMs can be effectively improved by simple keyword-enhanced prompting, which can even surpass sentence-encoder baselines for many models, while more involved strategies such as self-consistency decoding offer no benefit relative to their computational cost. Per-article knowledge graph augmentation is beneficial primarily for smaller LLMs that lack rich relational knowledge, but can harm the performance of larger models that already encode such information, suggesting that future work should carefully tailor graph augmentation to model capacity rather than assuming it is universally helpful.

title: Transforming and Encoding FTS for SAT Solving: What Helps, What Hurts (Extended Version)
summary:
Factored tasks are a classical planning representation that extends SAS+ with limited forms of disjunctive preconditions, conditional effects, and angelic nondeterminism. This allows for a more compact representation of tasks than traditional formalisms such as STRIPS or SAS+, and supports a wide range of task transformations. However, existing planning approaches for factored tasks have been limited to heuristic search methods.
  In this work, we investigate how to encode factored tasks in SAT. We propose several ways to encode the tasks, focusing on different strategies for translating the factored transition relation into propositional logic. We also analyze how to exploit parallelism at various levels in this setting and study the impact of common task transformations on the performance of SAT-based planners.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to translate factored classical planning tasks into SAT, proposes several SAT encodings of the factored transition relation, and empirically analyzes their performance and interaction with task transformations and parallelism.
AI.motivation: Factored tasks extend SAS+ with disjunctive preconditions, conditional effects, and angelic nondeterminism, providing a more compact and flexible representation than traditional STRIPS/SAS+ and enabling powerful task transformations. However, current planners for this representation mainly rely on heuristic search, leaving unexplored how to leverage the power of modern SAT solvers for factored tasks. The authors aim to close this gap and understand how to effectively use SAT-based planning in this richer formalism.
AI.method: The authors design several SAT encodings for factored tasks, each based on a different strategy to translate the factored transition relation into propositional formulas. They systematically vary how operator applicability, effects, and nondeterministic/angelic choices are captured. They further consider multiple forms of parallelism (e.g., action-level or step-level parallel execution) and examine how common task transformations used in planning (such as factoring, normalization, or compilation of complex constructs) affect the resulting SAT instances. An empirical evaluation compares the performance of the resulting SAT-based planners under these different encodings and configurations.
AI.result: The study identifies concrete SAT encodings that work well for factored tasks and clarifies which translation strategies yield better performance in practice. It shows how different choices in encoding the transition relation and exploiting parallelism significantly influence SAT solver efficiency. The experiments also reveal how standard task transformations can either help or hinder SAT-based planning, depending on the encoding and parallelism scheme used.
AI.conclusion: SAT-based planning can be effectively extended to factored tasks, but performance is highly sensitive to how the transition relation is encoded and how parallelism and task transformations are handled. Carefully designed encodings allow SAT planners to exploit the compactness and expressiveness of factored tasks, broadening the applicability of SAT-based techniques beyond traditional STRIPS/SAS+ formulations and providing guidance for future planner implementations and compilation strategies.

title: Your Multimodal Speech Model Says I Have a Face for Radio
summary: As large neural models have become better at language tasks, researchers are increasingly building multi- and omnimodal models that handle more modalities of data. One example is the expansion of speech recognition models to audio-visual data for noise mitigation and multimodal subtitling. While performance and bias have been studied extensively in the single-modality regime, it is unknown how new modalities affect this, even though they produce biases in humans. We therefore propose the first bias evaluation of multimodal speech recognition, where we create videos pairing different faces with the same audio, and measure changes in speech transcription accuracy. We find large quality-of-service differences across mWhisper-Flamingo and Gemini models, with drops of up to 4.05 word error rate points, across self-declared gender, ethnicity, and their intersection. Our findings point to a priority for developers to evaluate, fix, and communicate such limitations, as providing more signals through additional modalities is not necessarily better, and may even lead to biased outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates bias in multimodal (audio-visual) speech recognition systems by pairing the same audio with different faces and measuring transcription accuracy differences, finding notable quality-of-service disparities across demographic groups.
AI.motivation: With the rise of multimodal models that combine audio and visual data, it is unclear how adding modalities impacts model bias, even though humans exhibit modality-induced biases. Existing bias evaluations focus mostly on single-modality (e.g., audio-only) systems, leaving a gap in understanding whether and how visual information, such as faces, changes speech recognition performance across demographic groups.
AI.method: The authors construct a dataset of videos in which identical audio clips are paired with different faces. Using this setup, they systematically evaluate multiple multimodal speech recognition models (e.g., mWhisper-Flamingo and Gemini) by comparing word error rates on the same audio when different demographic attributes—self-declared gender, ethnicity, and their intersection—are shown visually. They then quantify differences in transcription accuracy as a function of the visual demographic cues.
AI.result: The experiments reveal substantial differences in quality of service across demographic groups, with word error rate changes of up to 4.05 points depending on the face shown, despite the audio content being unchanged. These disparities arise along self-declared gender, ethnicity, and intersectional categories, and they are observed across evaluated multimodal models such as mWhisper-Flamingo and Gemini.
AI.conclusion: Adding visual modalities to speech recognition does not uniformly improve performance and can introduce or exacerbate demographic biases in transcription quality. Developers of multimodal systems must prioritize systematic bias evaluation, mitigation, and transparent communication of such limitations, as more input signals are not inherently beneficial and may lead to unfair outcomes for certain groups.

title: Procedural Generation of First Person Shooter Maps using Map-Elites
summary: We investigate the application of MAP-Elites (a well-known quality diversity algorithm) to design levels for First-Person Shooter (FPS) games. We consider two well-known map representations (All-Black and Grid-Graph) and introduce two novel representations (Point-Line and Spatial-Layout) that improve the characterization of FPS maps. We define a series of metrics to describe maps' topological properties (which solely depend on maps' layout), and emergent properties (which must be evaluated through actual gameplay). We perform an in-depth analysis to identify the most suitable features to guide MAP-Elites illumination process. We apply MAP-Elites with Sliding Boundaries (MESB) to evolve populations of FPS maps. Our results show that the new representations can generate maps with higher diversity and quality than the representations previously used for evolving FPS maps.

AI.tldr: The paper applies the MAP-Elites quality-diversity algorithm to automatically generate diverse, high-quality FPS game levels using improved map representations and guided by both structural and gameplay-based metrics.
AI.motivation: Traditional procedural generation of FPS maps often focuses on optimizing a single objective (e.g., win balance or path length), which can limit variety, player experience, and designer control. Existing map representations for evolutionary design of FPS levels (like All-Black and Grid-Graph) have limitations in how well they capture the spatial and tactical characteristics important for gameplay. There is a need for methods that can systematically explore a wide range of level designs, discovering many high-performing but qualitatively different maps, while using representations and metrics that better reflect what makes an FPS map interesting and playable.
AI.method: The authors employ MAP-Elites, a quality-diversity evolutionary algorithm, augmented with Sliding Boundaries (MESB), to search over the space of FPS maps. They compare two existing level representations (All-Black and Grid-Graph) with two new representations they introduce (Point-Line and Spatial-Layout) that aim to encode spatial structure and layout of FPS maps more expressively. They define metrics that fall into two groups: (1) topological properties depending only on the map layout (e.g., connectivity, corridors, chokepoints) and (2) emergent properties derived from simulated gameplay (e.g., movement patterns, combat encounters, balance). An analysis is conducted to determine which features are most effective as behaviour descriptors (dimensions) in the MAP-Elites feature space. Using MESB, they evolve populations of maps and examine the diversity and quality of solutions produced by each representation.
AI.result: The experiments show that the two proposed map representations (Point-Line and Spatial-Layout) enable MAP-Elites to discover a broader set of distinct FPS maps than the older All-Black and Grid-Graph encodings. In addition to increased diversity, the new representations consistently yield maps with higher quality according to the defined structural and gameplay-based metrics. The analysis of the feature space further indicates which topological and emergent metrics are most informative for guiding the illumination process, improving the coverage and usefulness of the generated map repertoires.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that carefully designed map representations, combined with appropriate topological and emergent behaviour descriptors, substantially enhance the effectiveness of MAP-Elites for FPS level generation. The proposed Point-Line and Spatial-Layout encodings lead to more diverse and higher-quality maps than previous representations, demonstrating the value of richer spatial abstractions for evolutionary design. The work suggests that quality-diversity approaches like MESB, when coupled with domain-specific features, are a powerful tool for exploring and populating the design space of FPS game levels, and it opens avenues for further refinement of representations and metrics in procedural content generation.

title: When English Rewrites Local Knowledge: Global Narrative Dominance in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are widely used as cross-lingual knowledge interfaces. However, culturally grounded questions often reflect globally dominant narratives rather than local contexts. We study this failure mode as \textit{global narrative dominance} in Bangla, a low-resource cultural context. We introduce \texttt{CulturalNB}, a dataset of 717 manually curated Bengali cultural instances with parallel Bangla--English question--answer pairs and supporting evidence, metadata, and sociocultural annotations. Using question-only and evidence-based prompting, we evaluate nine state-of-the-art LLMs with human and two independent LLM judges across metrics for cross-lingual consistency, language anchoring, global substitution, institutional bias, and epistemic perspective coverage. Results show that questions asked in English systematically increase global substitution and institutional framing while reducing local perspective coverage. Local evidence improves factual consistency and perspective coverage, but does not eliminate language-induced epistemic shifts. These findings suggest that cultural failures in LLMs are not only missing-knowledge errors but also failures of grounding and narrative prioritization.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how large language models (LLMs) privileged global (often Western or institutional) narratives when answering culturally grounded questions about Bangladeshi/Bengali culture, especially when queries are in English rather than Bangla.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as cross-lingual knowledge tools, but when dealing with culture-specific questions, they may default to globally dominant perspectives instead of local viewpoints. This is especially critical in low-resource cultural and linguistic settings like Bangla, where local knowledge is underrepresented. The authors aim to systematically characterize and measure this bias, termed global narrative dominance, and understand how language choice and evidence affect model behavior.
AI.method: The authors construct CulturalNB, a curated dataset of 717 Bengali cultural instances, each with parallel Bangla–English QA pairs, supporting evidence, metadata, and sociocultural annotations. They evaluate nine state-of-the-art LLMs using two prompting setups: (1) question-only and (2) evidence-based prompts that include local supporting texts. Human annotators and two independent LLM judges score model outputs along multiple dimensions: cross-lingual consistency between Bangla and English responses, degree of language anchoring (alignment to the query language), frequency of global narrative substitution, institutional bias in framing, and coverage of different epistemic perspectives. Comparative analyses across languages, prompts, and models reveal systematic patterns.
AI.result: When questions are posed in English, LLMs more often replace local Bengali cultural perspectives with global or dominant narratives, and they frame answers more in terms of institutional viewpoints, while covering fewer local perspectives. Providing local evidence in prompts improves factual correctness and enriches perspective coverage but does not fully prevent the epistemic shift induced by using English, meaning that language alone still steers models toward globalized narratives. These effects are robust across the nine tested models and across evaluation metrics from both human and LLM judges.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that cultural failures of LLMs go beyond simple gaps in factual knowledge: they reflect deeper issues of grounding (what evidence and context models rely on) and narrative prioritization (which cultural perspectives are centered). English queries tend to push models toward global and institutional narratives at the expense of local Bangla viewpoints, and even explicit local evidence cannot fully counteract this tendency. Addressing such global narrative dominance will require methods that more fundamentally align LLM reasoning and generation with diverse, locally grounded epistemic perspectives rather than just expanding knowledge coverage.

title: Uncertainty-Aware and Temporally Regulated Expert Advice in Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Driving
comment: Accepted in The IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC) September 15-18, 2026 -- Naples, Italy
summary: Exploration in reinforcement learning for autonomous driving is inherently unsafe: agents must experience novel behaviors to learn, yet exploration can lead to collisions or off-road driving. We propose an uncertainty-aware framework that leverages expert advice to guide exploration while avoiding long-term dependence. Advice is triggered when epistemic or aleatoric uncertainty exceeds adaptive thresholds derived from rolling buffers, ensuring advice evolves with the agent's confidence. A commitment-cooldown strategy with a stochastic early-stop heuristic regulates the duration and frequency of guidance, exposing the agent to coherent maneuvers without exhausting the advice budget. Expert and agent experiences are combined in a shared replay buffer within an off-policy implicit quantile network (IQN) backbone, enabling efficient reuse of expert trajectories. Experiments in CARLA show that our method outperforms the IQN baseline, improving success by 5-7% and reducing failures, demonstrating that risk-sensitive uncertainty coupled with regulated expert integration enables safer and more efficient exploration for sensor-based RL policy learning in unsignalized intersection navigation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an uncertainty-aware, expert‑guided exploration framework for reinforcement learning in autonomous driving that improves safety and performance at unsignalized intersections.
AI.motivation: Exploration in reinforcement learning for autonomous driving is dangerous because learning agents must try unfamiliar actions that can cause collisions or unsafe maneuvers. Existing methods either explore unsafely or rely too heavily on expert demonstrations, which can limit autonomy and adaptability. The authors aim to enable safe yet sufficiently rich exploration for sensor-based RL policies in complex driving scenarios like unsignalized intersections.
AI.method: They introduce an uncertainty-aware framework that requests expert advice only when needed. The agent estimates epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty and compares them against adaptive thresholds obtained from rolling buffers, so that the criteria for advice evolve with the agent’s growing confidence. When uncertainty is high, the expert temporarily takes control following a commitment–cooldown strategy: the agent commits to expert guidance for a short period and then enforces a cooldown before more advice can be requested, with a stochastic early-stop heuristic to end guidance sooner when appropriate. Expert and agent transitions are stored together in a shared replay buffer and learned via an off-policy Implicit Quantile Network (IQN), allowing efficient use of expert trajectories while still training a risk-sensitive value distribution.
AI.result: In experiments conducted in the CARLA simulator on unsignalized intersection navigation tasks, the proposed method outperforms a standard IQN baseline without expert integration. It yields a 5–7% increase in overall success rate and a reduction in failure cases such as collisions or going off-road, demonstrating more efficient and safer exploration during training.
AI.conclusion: Coupling risk-sensitive uncertainty estimation with regulated, on-demand expert intervention enables safer and more efficient exploration for reinforcement learning in autonomous driving. By adaptively triggering expert advice based on evolving uncertainty thresholds and carefully controlling its duration and frequency, the method reduces dangerous behaviors while avoiding long-term dependence on the expert, leading to better performance in challenging intersection navigation tasks.

title: Configurable Reward Model for Balanced Safety Alignment
summary: Aligning large language models (LLMs) to heterogeneous and rapidly evolving safety requirements remains a critical challenge. Existing instruction-tuned LLMs and standalone safety classifiers often fail to generalize to new safety configurations, motivating the need for Reward Models (RMs) that are explicitly configurable to changing specifications. We introduce the Configurable Safety Reward Model (CSRM), which is jointly optimized for calibrated safety compliance and reward modeling. Our approach is supported by configuration-targeted data augmentation that enforces instruction adherence while preserving relative severity structure. The resulting RM is sensitive to fine-grained safety configurations and conversational nuances, substantially improving generalization to previously unseen safety configurations. CSRM achieves state-of-the-art performance on recent configurable safety benchmarks, including CoSApien (94.6% F1) and DynaBench (75.8% F1), without requiring additional human annotation. When used for downstream safety alignment, CSRM yields LLMs with a significantly improved helpfulness-safety tradeoff compared to existing baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Configurable Safety Reward Models (CSRM) to better align large language models with changing and fine‑grained safety requirements, achieving state-of-the-art performance on configurable safety benchmarks and improving the helpfulness–safety tradeoff without extra human annotation.
AI.motivation: Existing instruction-tuned LLMs and standalone safety classifiers struggle to generalize to heterogeneous, rapidly evolving, and fine-grained safety requirements. They typically cannot be easily reconfigured to new safety policies, and existing reward models are not explicitly designed to be configurable. There is a need for a reward modeling approach that can adapt to new safety configurations while maintaining calibrated safety assessment and supporting downstream alignment.
AI.method: The authors propose the Configurable Safety Reward Model (CSRM), a reward model that is explicitly conditioned on safety configurations. CSRM is jointly optimized for (1) calibrated safety compliance judgment and (2) reward modeling of assistant responses. The training pipeline uses configuration-targeted data augmentation designed to: (a) enforce adherence to the specified safety configuration and (b) preserve the relative severity structure across examples. This makes the reward model sensitive to subtle policy differences and conversational context. CSRM is then evaluated as both a standalone safety assessor and as a reward model for RL/feedback-based safety alignment.
AI.result: CSRM is able to generalize to previously unseen safety configurations and capture fine-grained, configuration-specific safety nuances better than baselines. On configurable safety benchmarks, it achieves state-of-the-art results, including 94.6% F1 on the CoSApien benchmark and 75.8% F1 on DynaBench, without using any additional human-labeled data beyond what is already available. In downstream experiments where CSRM is used to align LLMs, the resulting models show a substantially improved tradeoff between being helpful and complying with safety constraints compared with existing methods and standard safety classifiers.
AI.conclusion: Configurable Safety Reward Models provide an effective way to align LLMs with dynamic and fine-grained safety policies. By conditioning the reward model on explicit safety configurations and using targeted data augmentation, CSRM achieves better generalization to novel configurations and improved benchmark performance, all without extra human annotation. When used for safety alignment, CSRM yields LLMs that are both safer and more helpful, suggesting that configurable reward modeling is a promising direction for scalable and adaptable safety alignment of large language models.

title: Harness Updating Is Not Harness Benefit: Disentangling Evolution Capabilities in Self-Evolving LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents are increasingly deployed as systems built around editable external harnesses, including prompts, skills, memories and tools, that shape task execution without changing model parameters. Harness self-evolution adapts such agents by updating these harnesses from execution evidence. Yet it remains unclear whether a model's base capability in task-solving predicts its capabilities in harness self-evolution: which models produce useful harness updates, and which actually benefit from them? We analyze two harness self-evolution capabilities: (i) harness-updating, the capability to produce useful persistent harness updates from execution evidence; (ii) harness-benefit, the capability to benefit from updated harnesses during task solving. Our analysis reveals two findings. First, harness-updating is flat in base capability: models from different capability tiers produce harness updates that lead to surprisingly similar gains; even Qwen3.5-9B's updates yield gains comparable to those of Claude Opus~4.6. Second, harness-benefit is non-monotonic in base capability: weak-tier models benefit little from updated harnesses, mid-tier models benefit most, and strong-tier models benefit less than mid-tier. We trace low gains at the weak tier to two failure modes: weak-tier models may fail to activate relevant harness artifacts, or activate them but fail to follow them faithfully. These findings suggest investing capability budget in the task-solving agent rather than the evolver, and targeting harness invocation and long-horizon instruction following in agent training. Our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/A-EVO-Lab/a-evolve/tree/release/harness-evolution.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how large language model (LLM) agents that use editable external components (“harnesses” like prompts, tools, and memories) can automatically improve themselves, and which kinds of models are good at both generating and using such improvements.
AI.motivation: LLM agents today are often adapted not by fine-tuning weights, but by editing external harnesses such as system prompts, skills, tools, and memories. Some agents can automatically update these harnesses from their own experience (self-evolution). However, it is not well understood how a model’s base problem-solving capability relates to: (1) its ability to generate good harness updates, and (2) its ability to benefit from those updates later. Clarifying this relationship can guide where to invest model capacity and how to design training for self-improving agents.
AI.method: The authors define and empirically analyze two distinct capabilities for LLM agents with self-evolving harnesses: (i) harness-updating – the ability to turn execution traces into useful, persistent harness modifications; and (ii) harness-benefit – the ability to obtain better task performance when using these updated harnesses. They compare models of different capability tiers (weak, mid, strong, including Qwen3.5-9B and Claude Opus 4.6) on the quality of their harness updates and on how much they benefit from those updates, identifying patterns and failure modes in each tier.
AI.result: They find that harness-updating is largely flat across base capabilities: models of very different strengths generate harness updates that yield surprisingly similar performance gains, and even a relatively small model like Qwen3.5-9B can produce updates as useful as those from a top-tier model like Claude Opus 4.6. In contrast, harness-benefit is non-monotonic: weak models gain little from improved harnesses, mid-tier models get the largest performance boosts, and very strong models benefit less than mid-tier ones. For weak-tier models, they diagnose two key failures: not activating relevant harness components at the right time, and not reliably following the guidance even when it is activated.
AI.conclusion: Self-evolution in LLM agents depends differently on base capability for generating versus using harness updates. Since even smaller models can produce competitive harness improvements, it is often more effective to allocate capability to the task-solving agent than to a separate, more powerful evolver. Training should focus on better harness invocation mechanisms and stronger long-horizon instruction-following so that agents, particularly weaker ones, can actually exploit the improved harnesses. The work also releases code to facilitate further research on harness evolution.

title: CanLegalRAGBench: Evaluating Retrieval-Augmented Generation on Canadian Case Law
summary: RAG-based legal assistants have been growing in popularity, but LLM hallucinations remain a key issue and potentially undermines justice. While benchmarks have been developed to evaluate progress, many rely on synthetic queries rather than realistic legal scenarios. Moreover, Canadian law remains underrepresented in existing evaluations. To address this gap, we introduce CanLegalRAGBench, a Canadian legal QA benchmark based on realistic queries and expert-annotated answers grounded in case law. Our evaluation shows that retrieval performance is sensitive to design choices and that open-source embedding models are competitive with closed source models. However, it also reveals the limitation of automatic evaluations that penalize systems for retrieving alternative relevant documents. We also find that generated answers often diverge from gold responses, either with hallucinations or by producing overly detailed or irrelevant content, with 8-29% of claims not being supported by the retrieved documents. We hope this benchmark will help drive continued progress in addressing limitations of legal RAG systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces CanLegalRAGBench, a realistic Canadian legal QA benchmark to evaluate and improve RAG-based legal assistants, highlighting retrieval sensitivity, competitiveness of open-source embeddings, and limitations of current automatic evaluation and generation quality.
AI.motivation: RAG-based legal assistants suffer from hallucinations that can undermine justice, and existing benchmarks often use synthetic queries and underrepresent Canadian law. There is a need for a realistic, jurisdiction-specific benchmark with expert-annotated answers grounded in actual case law to better assess and improve legal RAG systems.
AI.method: The authors construct CanLegalRAGBench, a Canadian legal QA benchmark derived from realistic legal queries with expert-annotated answers linked to supporting case law documents. They then evaluate different RAG configurations, including various retrieval design choices and both open-source and closed-source embedding models, and compare automatic evaluation metrics with human-grounded relevance and faithfulness assessments.
AI.result: Their experiments show that retrieval quality is highly sensitive to system design choices, and that open-source embedding models can perform competitively with proprietary ones. Automatic evaluation metrics sometimes misjudge systems, especially when they retrieve alternative but still relevant documents. In generated answers, 8–29% of factual claims are not supported by the retrieved documents, indicating notable hallucination and irrelevance issues.
AI.conclusion: CanLegalRAGBench fills an important gap for evaluating RAG-based legal assistants in the Canadian context by using realistic queries and expert-grounded answers. The findings stress the need for careful retrieval design, more nuanced evaluation methods that can handle multiple relevant sources, and improved generation techniques to reduce hallucinations and over- or off-target verbosity in legal advice. The benchmark is intended as a foundation for ongoing research to make legal RAG systems more reliable and trustworthy.

title: EHRBench: An Automated and Reliable EHR-based Benchmark for Clinical Decision Making with LLMs
comment: Proceedings of the 32nd ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2026), Datasets and Benchmarks Track, Oral
summary: Clinical decision-making (CDM) is central to real-world clinical workflows, where clinicians infer diagnoses, select treatments, or anticipate future health outcomes under incomplete evidence. LLMs are increasingly used to support these decisions due to strong language capabilities, broad biomedical knowledge, and efficiency, yet the reliability of LLMs on real-world clinical decision tasks remains insufficiently understood. To evaluate CDM models, especially LLM-based models, an ideal and practical medical decision benchmark should be constructed via an automated yet reliable pipeline to ensure both scale and quality. Moreover, the grounding of a CDM benchmark in real patient EHRs can better support evaluation on practical CDM tasks that require substantive biomedical knowledge and clinical inference. To fill the gaps, we introduce EHRBench, an automated and reliable EHR-grounded benchmark for evaluating LLM-based clinical decision-making at scale. To ensure scalability and reliability, EHRBench is constructed through an EHR-LLM-KB(knowledge-base) interaction pipeline. For efficiency, we use a specialized LLM to automatically convert encounter-level EHR trajectories into structured templates and deterministically instantiate the templates into QA items. In parallel, we apply systematic KB-based verification and enrichment to filter hallucinated or ambiguous relations and to improve reliability. Using this pipeline, we construct nearly 1M (960,067) QA items spanning three core inference-required clinical decision tasks: diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis. We benchmark more than 30 representative LLMs on EHRBench and provide detailed analyses of performance and robustness. The results show consistent capability trends across settings, further validating the reliability of EHRBench and highlighting actionable gaps toward clinically reliable LLM systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EHRBench, a large-scale, EHR-grounded benchmark to evaluate LLM-based clinical decision-making models using an automated, verified pipeline.
AI.motivation: Clinical decision-making is central to healthcare but LLM reliability for real-world clinical tasks is unclear. Existing benchmarks are often small, manually built, or not tightly grounded in real EHR data, limiting their realism, scale, and trustworthiness. There is a need for an automated yet reliable way to construct a large benchmark that reflects real clinical workflows and can robustly evaluate LLMs on diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis tasks.
AI.method: The authors design an EHR-LLM-KB interaction pipeline that takes real encounter-level EHR trajectories and uses a specialized LLM to convert them into structured templates. These templates are then deterministically instantiated into QA items. In parallel, a knowledge-base (KB) driven verification and enrichment step filters hallucinated or ambiguous relations and augments items to improve reliability. This process scales automatically and maintains quality through KB checks.
AI.result: Using this pipeline, the authors build EHRBench, consisting of about 960k QA items that cover three core clinical decision-making tasks: diagnosis, treatment selection, and prognosis. They evaluate over 30 representative LLMs on this benchmark and conduct detailed analyses of their performance and robustness, obtaining consistent capability trends across different settings.
AI.conclusion: EHRBench provides a scalable, reliable, EHR-grounded benchmark for assessing LLM-based clinical decision-making. The consistent performance patterns of models across tasks and settings support the benchmark’s validity, while the observed performance gaps highlight remaining challenges and directions for improving LLMs toward clinically reliable systems.

title: Linear Ensembles Wash Away Watermarks: On the Fragility of Distributional Perturbations in LLMs
summary: Watermarking embeds statistical signatures in AI-generated text for detection and attribution. We reveal a fundamental vulnerability: when users access multiple models (today's reality), watermarks trivially fail. Watermarks perturb output distributions away from the original, and in competitive markets, these perturbations are typically independent across providers. We theoretically prove that averaging output probability distributions recovers the unwatermarked distribution with up to a second-order error term. Empirically, simply averaging 3-5 models cancels out these perturbations. We introduce WASH (Watermark Attenuation via Statistical Hybridisation), which solves practical challenges in ensemble generation: vocabulary misalignment and tokenisation differences across heterogeneous models. Experiments across six watermarking schemes and three LLMs show that averaging across 3 models suppresses detection z-scores from 5-300 to below 2 (below the detection threshold of 4) and reduces TPR at 5% FPR to below 50%, while improving quality by 27.5% and running 6 times faster than the best baseline on the long sequence generation. Our results suggest that robust AI-text detection via watermarking requires either accepting this fundamental vulnerability or unprecedented coordination among model providers.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that AI text watermarks become ineffective when outputs from multiple different models are combined, and proposes a practical method (WASH) to do this combination while handling model differences.
AI.motivation: AI text watermarks are proposed for provenance and detection of machine-generated content, but current schemes largely assume text comes from a single watermarked model. In reality, users can access several independent models and combine or ensemble their outputs. The authors are motivated to understand whether watermarking remains robust in this multi-model setting and whether an adversary can easily remove watermarks by exploiting model diversity.
AI.method: The authors provide a theoretical analysis showing that if each model’s watermark independently perturbs its output distribution, then averaging probability distributions across models approximately recovers the original, unwatermarked distribution up to a second-order error. They then design WASH (Watermark Attenuation via Statistical Hybridisation), a practical framework that ensembles heterogeneous models by averaging their token probability distributions while addressing vocabulary mismatches and tokenisation differences. They test WASH on multiple watermarking schemes and LLMs, measuring watermark detection scores, true positive rates, text quality, and efficiency.
AI.result: Theoretical results show that independent watermark perturbations cancel when averaged. Empirically, averaging outputs from as few as 3–5 models drastically reduces watermark detectability. Using WASH across six watermarking schemes and three LLMs, averaging across 3 models lowers watermark detection z-scores from highly detectable ranges (5–300) to below 2, under common detection thresholds, and drops true positive rates at 5% false positive rate to below 50%. Simultaneously, WASH improves generation quality by about 27.5% and is roughly 6× faster than the strongest baseline for long sequence generation.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current watermark-based AI text detection is fundamentally vulnerable in realistic multi-model environments, because simple ensemble averaging can largely erase watermark signals while even improving output quality. Mitigating this would require either accepting this inherent vulnerability or achieving strong, cooperative alignment of watermarking schemes across different model providers, which may be impractical in competitive markets.

title: Structure-Induced Information for Rerooting Levin Tree Search
summary: Subgoal-based policy tree search, which uses a policy to guide search, is effective for complex single-agent deterministic problems but often relies on explicit subgoal generation that can incur substantial overhead and hinders scalability. In this paper, we overcome these limitations by using a learned ``rerooter'' through the recently-introduced $\sqrt{\text{LTS}}$ algorithm. A rerooter implicitly decomposes the problem into soft subtasks. While previous work focused on the formal guarantees for given or handcrafted rerooters, in this work we propose three rerooter designs: (i) a clustering-based rerooter that exploits global state-space structure, (ii) a heuristic-based rerooter that leverages learned cost-to-go estimates, and (iii) a hybrid that combines both signals. Our framework avoids having to explicitly reconstruct and reason over generated subgoals, thereby enabling scalable allocation of search effort with significantly lower computational overhead. Empirically, our rerooting-based methods scale to complex environments where subgoal-based policy tree search fails, and achieve state-of-the-art online training efficiency on the domains tested.

AI.tldr: The paper improves subgoal-based policy tree search by replacing explicit subgoal generation with learned “rerooters” within the √LTS algorithm, yielding more scalable and efficient search.
AI.motivation: Traditional subgoal-based policy tree search methods work well in complex deterministic single-agent settings but suffer from high computational overhead and poor scalability because they depend on explicitly generating and reasoning about subgoals. There is a need for methods that retain the benefits of guided search via subgoals while avoiding the cost and complexity of explicit subgoal construction, especially in large or complex environments.
AI.method: The authors build on the √LTS algorithm and introduce learned rerooters that implicitly decompose problems into soft subtasks without explicit subgoal enumeration. They design three rerooter variants: (i) a clustering-based rerooter that uses global structure of the state space, (ii) a heuristic-based rerooter that uses learned cost-to-go estimates to guide rerooting decisions, and (iii) a hybrid rerooter that combines clustering and heuristic cost signals. These rerooters are integrated into a subgoal-based policy tree search framework to allocate search effort efficiently and reduce computational overhead.
AI.result: Experiments show that the rerooting-based methods scale to more complex environments than those handled by traditional subgoal-based policy tree search, where the latter fails due to computational or scalability limits. Additionally, the proposed methods achieve state-of-the-art online training efficiency on the tested domains, indicating both better scalability and improved practical performance.
AI.conclusion: Implicit subtask decomposition via learned rerooters within the √LTS framework can replace explicit subgoal generation in policy tree search, leading to significantly lower computational overhead and better scalability. The proposed rerooter designs enable more efficient allocation of search effort and achieve strong empirical performance, suggesting rerooting as a promising direction for solving complex deterministic single-agent problems.

title: Auditing LLM Benchmarks with Item Response Theory
summary: LLM benchmark labels are frozen at release and silently propagated into downstream benchmarks, errors and all. We introduce an Item Response Theory-based indicator that surfaces likely mislabels at 95% precision in the top 200 examples across seven preference and multiple-choice benchmarks using responses from 114 models, outperforming a supervised classifier. We trace these errors to mechanical labeling heuristics, upstream annotation mistakes inherited unchanged from source datasets, and fundamentally ambiguous items without a defensible single label. The same model fit reveals that reward models specialize in stylistic preference rather than factual knowledge, and identifies one frontier reward model that agrees with detected mislabels at 78% accuracy versus 38% for its peers, consistent with benchmark contamination or benchmark-specific over-optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper develops an Item Response Theory (IRT)-based method to detect likely mislabels in LLM benchmarks, showing many labels are wrong or ambiguous and that some reward models may be overfitted or contaminated by these benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLM benchmarks are widely reused and their labels are treated as ground truth, yet they are frozen at release and errors propagate silently into downstream evaluations. This undermines the reliability of measuring model progress and training reward models. There is a need for a systematic, scalable way to detect labeling errors and ambiguities in existing benchmarks using model behavior itself, rather than assuming benchmark labels are always correct.
AI.method: The authors collect responses from 114 language models on seven preference and multiple-choice benchmarks. They fit an Item Response Theory (IRT) model that jointly models item characteristics and model abilities based on the response patterns. From this fit, they derive an indicator for label quality: items that are consistently answered against the benchmark label by strong models are flagged as likely mislabels. They evaluate this indicator’s precision at surfacing mislabels and compare it against a supervised classifier baseline.
AI.result: The IRT-based indicator achieves 95% precision in identifying mislabels among the top 200 flagged examples across multiple benchmarks, outperforming a supervised classifier. The analysis reveals several sources of label issues: artifacts from mechanical labeling heuristics, uncorrected upstream annotation mistakes inherited from original datasets, and inherently ambiguous items that lack a single defensible label. The fitted IRT model also shows that existing reward models are more attuned to stylistic preferences than factual correctness, and it identifies one advanced reward model that aligns with the discovered mislabels far more often (78% vs. 38% for others), suggesting possible benchmark contamination or over-optimization on those benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark labels for LLM evaluation are not reliably correct, and their errors propagate through the ecosystem. An IRT-based analysis of model responses can efficiently surface high-precision candidate mislabels and expose structural problems in how benchmarks and reward models are constructed. The findings warn that some reward models may be overfitted or contaminated by the very benchmarks they are evaluated on and that future benchmark design should incorporate procedures for ongoing label validation, especially for ambiguous or mechanically labeled items.

title: Healthcare Mechanisms from Policy-as-Code Search under Strategic Provider Response
summary: Healthcare mechanisms are inseparable from the strategic provider response they induce: existing healthcare AI benchmarks hold this response fixed and so cannot evaluate mechanisms by the equilibrium they produce. We recast hospital mechanism design as program synthesis for language models: typed, inspectable rule programs are executed and scored by Medi-Sim, a multi-agent simulator with five strategic provider channels (coding, selection, delay, effort, triage). An incentive sweep recovers classical health-economics findings as adjacent regimes -- up-coding and low-complexity-patient selection under profit pressure, and Goodhart-style drift where measured performance becomes anti-correlated with true outcomes -- and a single audit lever exposes pressure migration: closing the coding channel more than doubles low-complexity selection. LLM-guided evolutionary code search over the same rule-program space then synthesizes an inspectable mixed-objective program that eliminates up-coding, halves rejection, and retains most of the profit-oriented baseline's funds.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Medi-Sim, a multi-agent simulation framework that models how hospitals strategically respond to different payment and evaluation rules, and uses program-synthesized, language-model-generated mechanisms to find rule systems that improve incentives while preserving revenue.
AI.motivation: Existing healthcare AI and policy benchmarks typically assume providers behave passively, holding their response fixed when evaluating algorithms or payment rules. In reality, hospitals strategically adapt along several channels (coding, patient selection, timing, effort, triage), and these behavioral responses determine real-world outcomes and unintended consequences like gaming, up-coding, and cherry-picking patients. The authors aim to create a framework that evaluates healthcare mechanisms in terms of the equilibrium behavior they induce, not just their static performance, and then to use that framework to design better, inspectable incentive rules.
AI.method: The authors reframe hospital mechanism design as a program synthesis problem for language models. They define typed, human-inspectable rule programs that specify how hospitals are paid or evaluated. These programs are executed in Medi-Sim, a multi-agent simulator that models five strategic provider channels: coding intensity, patient selection, care delay, treatment effort, and triage behavior. By sweeping incentive parameters, they explore how different mechanisms induce different strategic behaviors and outcomes.
