They then use LLM-guided evolutionary code search over the same rule-program space to automatically synthesize new mixed-objective mechanisms, with the LLM iteratively proposing and refining rule programs that perform well under simulation on multiple objectives (e.g., reduce gaming, maintain access, preserve funds).
AI.result: Incentive sweeps in Medi-Sim reproduce several classical findings from health economics: stronger profit pressure induces up-coding and selection of low-complexity patients, and performance metrics can become Goodharted so that measured performance is negatively correlated with true clinical outcomes. They also show a pressure-migration effect: tightening one channel (e.g., via an audit that reduces coding manipulation) causes strategic behavior to shift to another channel (substantially increased selection of low-complexity patients, more than doubling it). Using LLM-guided evolutionary search, they discover an inspectable mixed-objective mechanism that successfully eliminates up-coding, cuts patient rejection rates roughly in half, and still preserves most of the financial performance (funds) of a profit-focused baseline payment rule.
AI.conclusion: Modeling provider incentives as strategic, multi-channel behavior in simulation allows more realistic evaluation of healthcare AI and payment mechanisms. The proposed Medi-Sim framework, combined with program-synthesis via language models, can both reproduce known economic phenomena and automatically design new, interpretable incentive rules that mitigate gaming and access problems while preserving much of the financial viability. This approach suggests a path toward data-driven, inspectable mechanism design in healthcare where equilibria under strategic provider response are central to evaluation.

title: MAVEN: Improving Generalization in Agentic Tool Calling
summary: Generalization across agentic tool-calling environments remains a central challenge for reliable agentic reasoning systems. Although large language models achieve strong results on individual benchmarks, their ability to compose reasoning strategies, preserve intermediate states, and coordinate tools across domains remains underexplored. We present MAVEN (Modular Agentic Verification and Execution Network), a lightweight symbolic reasoning scaffold for structured decomposition, adaptive tool orchestration, and intermediate verification. We evaluate MAVEN across established tool-calling benchmarks, including BFCL v3, TauBench, Tau2Bench, AceBench, and introduce MAVEN-Bench, a stress-test benchmark for multi-step mathematical and physical reasoning with explicit verification and adversarial task composition. MAVEN-Bench exposes a substantial gap between partial reasoning quality and end-to-end task success; in direct MAVEN-Bench runs, MAVEN improves its GPT-OSS-120b base model from 48% to 71% accuracy without additional training. It also remains competitive with frontier proprietary baselines while using an open-weight backbone with an estimated cost ratio of roughly 1/10, suggesting that lightweight verification-centered scaffolds can strengthen compositional reasoning and motivate more process-aware evaluation of agents in the wild.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MAVEN, a symbolic reasoning framework that improves large language model performance in complex, tool-based agentic environments, significantly boosting accuracy on challenging multi-step reasoning benchmarks at lower cost.
AI.motivation: While large language models perform well on isolated benchmarks, their reliability in real-world, agentic settings is limited. In such settings, models must decompose tasks, maintain intermediate states, and coordinate multiple tools across domains. Existing work underexplores how to systematically support these capabilities and evaluate them in stress-test conditions. The authors aim to close the gap between partial reasoning quality and true end-to-end task success, and to do so in a way that does not rely on ever-larger proprietary models.
AI.method: The authors propose MAVEN (Modular Agentic Verification and Execution Network), a lightweight symbolic reasoning scaffold around an LLM. MAVEN provides: (1) structured task decomposition, breaking problems into modular subtasks; (2) adaptive tool orchestration, deciding which tools to call and in what sequence; and (3) intermediate verification, checking partial results before proceeding. They then evaluate MAVEN on several established tool-calling benchmarks (BFCL v3, TauBench, Tau2Bench, AceBench) and introduce a new benchmark, MAVEN-Bench, specifically designed as a stress test for multi-step mathematical and physical reasoning with explicit verification and adversarially composed tasks.
AI.result: On MAVEN-Bench, the framework reveals a notable discrepancy between local reasoning steps that look good and overall task success. By adding MAVEN around the GPT-OSS-120b open model, end-to-end accuracy improves from 48% to 71% without any additional model training. MAVEN’s performance remains competitive with stronger proprietary frontier systems while running on an open-weight backbone, and the overall estimated cost is about one-tenth that of those proprietary baselines.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight, verification-centered symbolic scaffolds such as MAVEN can substantially enhance compositional and tool-based reasoning for LLM agents without retraining the base model. The work argues that progress in agentic systems should emphasize process-aware evaluation—focusing on decomposition, verification, and end-to-end success under adversarial, multi-step conditions—rather than only on raw model size or single-shot benchmark scores.

title: Generalistic or Specific Embeddings, Which is Better? An Empirical Study on Search for Clinical Coding in Non-English Languages
summary: Sentence-embedding models for semantic search are overwhelmingly developed and evaluated on English corpora. When applied to clinical retrieval in other languages -- particularly retrieval of ICD-10-CM / CIE-10 codes -- recall degrades in ways often masked by aggregate benchmarks. We study whether large generative language models can serve as data factories to close this gap. We build a two-stage retriever (bi-encoder followed by cross-encoder reranker), fine-tuned from a Spanish biomedical encoder (PlanTL-GOB-ES/bsc-bio-ehr-es) on Gemini-generated synthetic data covering English, Spanish, Catalan, Italian, Portuguese and French, and evaluate against BioBERT-ST and the un-tuned Spanish encoder. The bi-encoder alone matches BioBERT-ST on MRR (0.876 vs. 0.866) and overtakes it on R@3 (0.650 vs. 0.626) and R@5 (0.804 vs. 0.790) without English biomedical pretraining. Adding a cross-encoder reranker lifts aggregate R@5 to 0.822 and dominates on four of five languages (+0.017 Spanish, +0.033 Catalan, +0.018 French, +0.037 Portuguese) at the cost of a small English regression. The trade-off is clinically acceptable: Portuguese reaches R@5 = 0.829 vs. BioBERT-ST's 0.714. Contributions: an open recipe for building domain-specific medical retrievers from LLM-generated data; quantification of the learning gain (MRR 0.755 to 0.876, +15.9% with ~19,500 synthetic pairs); and a characterisation of where gains concentrate by language and rank.

AI.tldr: They build and evaluate a multilingual clinical code retriever using synthetic training data generated by a large language model.
AI.motivation: Most sentence-embedding models for semantic search are built and tested on English, so their recall drops when used for clinical retrieval (e.g., ICD-10-CM / CIE-10 codes) in other languages. This degradation is often hidden by aggregate benchmarks, and there is a need to improve multilingual clinical retrieval without relying on large real labeled datasets.
AI.method: They use a large generative language model as a data factory to create about 19,500 synthetic query–code pairs across six languages (English, Spanish, Catalan, Italian, Portuguese, French). Starting from a Spanish biomedical encoder (PlanTL-GOB-ES/bsc-bio-ehr-es), they fine-tune a two-stage retrieval system: a bi-encoder for initial retrieval and a cross-encoder for reranking. They compare against BioBERT-ST and the untuned Spanish encoder using metrics like MRR and recall at ranks 3 and 5, analyzing performance by language and rank.
AI.result: The fine-tuned bi-encoder alone matches BioBERT-ST on MRR (0.876 vs. 0.866) and surpasses it on R@3 (0.650 vs. 0.626) and R@5 (0.804 vs. 0.790), even without English biomedical pretraining. Adding the cross-encoder reranker increases aggregate R@5 to 0.822 and yields clear gains in four of five non-English languages (Spanish +0.017, Catalan +0.033, French +0.018, Portuguese +0.037), while causing only a modest performance drop in English. Portuguese, for example, improves to R@5 = 0.829 compared to BioBERT-ST's 0.714. They observe an overall MRR gain from 0.755 to 0.876 (+15.9%) attributable to the synthetic data.
AI.conclusion: LLM-generated synthetic data can effectively train strong multilingual, domain-specific clinical retrievers, significantly improving retrieval of ICD-10-related codes in several Romance languages with an acceptable trade-off in English performance. Their open recipe demonstrates that relatively modest amounts of synthetic data can close much of the gap between monolingual English-focused models and practical multilingual clinical needs, especially at clinically relevant top ranks.

title: Generating Graph-like Rules for Knowledge Graph Reasoning via Diffusion Models
summary: Logical rules constitute a cornerstone of knowledge graph (KG) reasoning, valued for their interpretability and ability to model relational patterns. However, existing rule mining methods predominantly focus on simple chain-like rules and therefore neglect the richer relational information encoded in graph-like structures, such as cycles and branches. This limitation is further exacerbated by computational bottlenecks caused by the combinatorial explosion of the search space, which is especially challenging for graph-like rules. Meanwhile, generative approaches such as diffusion models, despite their success in other domains, can not be directly applied to rule mining because their training objectives are not aligned with the goal of learning high-quality rules, and non-differentiable KG rule quality metrics cannot directly guide model optimization. To address these limitations, we propose GRiD, a framework that reformulates graph-like rule discovery as a discrete generative process conditioned on the target relation. GRiD employs a two-phase training strategy. First, supervised pre-training enables GRiD to capture structural priors from subgraphs sampled from the KG meta-graph. Subsequently, reinforcement learning is applied to fine-tune GRiD through policy gradient optimization guided directly by non-differentiable rule-quality metrics. Experiments on six benchmark datasets show that GRiD achieves competitive performance on KG completion tasks. Ablation studies confirm the efficiency and robustness of GRiD and further show that graph-like rules complement chain-like rules in KG completion. Our codes and datasets are available in https://github.com/Haoxiang-Cheng/GRiD

AI.tldr: They propose GRiD, a generative + reinforcement learning framework to mine complex graph-like logical rules for knowledge graphs, improving KG completion over baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing KG rule mining methods mostly learn simple chain-like rules and ignore richer graph-like structures (cycles, branches) due to huge combinatorial search space and computational bottlenecks. Generative models like diffusion work well elsewhere but don’t align with non-differentiable rule quality metrics used in KG reasoning, so they can’t directly optimize for good rules. The authors want a method that can efficiently discover expressive graph-structured rules while being directly guided by rule-quality measures.
AI.method: They reformulate graph-like rule discovery as a discrete conditional generative process over rules given a target relation. The proposed framework GRiD uses two-stage training: (1) supervised pre-training on subgraphs sampled from a KG meta-graph to learn structural priors and generate plausible graph-like rule structures; (2) reinforcement learning fine-tuning with policy gradients, where the reward is based on non-differentiable KG rule-quality metrics so that the generator is directly optimized for rule usefulness. GRiD can produce rules including cycles and branches, beyond simple chains.
AI.result: On six benchmark datasets, GRiD attains competitive or better performance for knowledge graph completion compared with existing rule mining and reasoning approaches. Ablation studies show that each component (pre-training, RL fine-tuning, graph-like structure modeling) contributes to performance, and that generated graph-like rules provide complementary predictive signal to traditional chain-like rules.
AI.conclusion: GRiD is an effective framework for mining expressive graph-like logical rules in knowledge graphs by casting rule discovery as a discrete generative modeling task with RL-based optimization on rule quality. It overcomes search-space and differentiability limitations in prior work, improves KG completion performance, and demonstrates that graph-like rules usefully complement chain-like rules. Code and data are released for reproducibility.

title: Refining Word-Based Grammatical Error Annotation for L2 Korean
summary: Korean grammatical error correction (K-GEC) presents a structural mismatch between word-based evaluation and the morpheme-level locus of many learner errors. Postpositions and verbal endings are bound to lexical hosts, but they encode grammatical relations that must be represented in correction and evaluation. This paper refines word-based grammatical error annotation for L2 Korean by addressing three connected problems in existing resources: surface target realization, Korean-specific edit annotation, and single-reference evaluation. We reconstruct target sentences from the National Institute of Korean Language (NIKL) L2 corpus under morphologically constrained realization rules and convert its morpheme-level annotations into word-level \texttt{m2} edits. We then define a Korean ERRANT-style annotation scheme that preserves the MRU core while distinguishing functional morpheme errors, spelling errors, word boundary errors, and word order errors. We also augment the KoLLA corpus with an additional reference correction, yielding a multi-reference evaluation setting for Korean GEC. Empirical validation shows that the refined NIKL targets yield lower perplexity, the converted \texttt{m2} files achieve higher agreement with source-target edit representations, and the refined resources improve KoBART-based correction under the same model setting. Multi-reference KoLLA evaluation further reduces the penalty imposed on valid corrections that diverge from a single reference, especially for neural and prompted GEC systems. These results show that Korean GEC evaluation depends not only on correction models, but also on reference data and edit annotations that reflect Korean morphology, spacing, and correction variability.

AI.tldr: They refine Korean grammatical error correction resources and evaluation to align with Korean morphology, and show that this improves model training and fairer evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing Korean GEC resources are misaligned with the morpheme-level nature of many Korean learner errors. Word-based evaluation ignores the fact that key grammatical markers (postpositions, verbal endings) are bound morphemes, leading to structural mismatch between how errors occur and how they are annotated and evaluated. Current corpora also have issues with how target sentences are realized on the surface, how Korean-specific edits are labeled, and they mostly rely on a single reference correction, which unfairly penalizes valid alternative corrections.
AI.method: (1) Reconstruct target sentences from the NIKL L2 corpus using morphologically constrained realization rules so that morpheme-level annotations correspond consistently to surface words. (2) Convert the original morpheme-level annotations into standard word-level m2 edits. (3) Design a Korean-specific ERRANT-style annotation scheme that keeps the minimal-revision-unit core but adds categories for functional morpheme errors, spelling, word boundary, and word order errors. (4) Add an extra human reference correction to the KoLLA corpus to enable multi-reference evaluation. (5) Empirically compare perplexity of targets, agreement between edit representations, and performance of KoBART-based GEC systems under the old vs refined resources, and under single- vs multi-reference evaluation, including neural and prompted systems.
AI.result: The reconstructed NIKL target sentences produce lower language-model perplexity, implying more consistent, well-formed targets. The converted m2 files show higher agreement with source–target edit representations, indicating better alignment between annotations and actual corrections. Using the refined resources improves the performance of KoBART-based GEC systems under the same model configuration. Multi-reference evaluation on KoLLA reduces penalties for acceptable corrections that differ from the original reference, particularly benefiting neural and prompted GEC models whose outputs are more diverse.
AI.conclusion: Reliable Korean GEC evaluation requires not only strong correction models but also linguistically appropriate reference data and edit schemes tailored to Korean morphology and spacing. By reconstructing targets, redefining edit types, and moving to multi-reference evaluation, the paper shows measurable gains in both model performance and fairness of evaluation, underscoring that evaluation infrastructure must reflect the structural properties and correction variability of Korean.

title: Learning Agent-Compatible Context Management for Long-Horizon Tasks
summary: LLM agents increasingly face long-horizon tasks such as web search and deep research in real-world applications, where accumulated context can cause long-context degradation and reasoning failures. Prior work mitigates this through context management with agent-side context control or fixed strategies such as summarization, which require training the agent itself for adaptation - making it impractical for closed-source agents and ignoring that different agents may require different strategies. We introduce Adaptive Context Management (AdaCoM), which trains an external LLM to manage the context of a frozen agent through flexible modification actions and end-to-end reinforcement learning. Across diverse agents on web search and deep research benchmarks, AdaCoM substantially improves performance by preserving task constraints and progress while pruning stale content. The learned strategies reveal a Fidelity-Reliability Trade-off: agents with higher vanilla ReAct performance benefit from higher-fidelity context preservation, whereas lower-performing agents require more aggressive compression to stay within a reliable reasoning regime. Transfer experiments show that AdaCoM generalizes most effectively across agents with similar capability (measured by vanilla ReAct performance), suggesting a practical path toward reusable context managers for agent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AdaCoM, an external LLM-based context manager that uses reinforcement learning to adaptively manage long-horizon context for frozen LLM agents, improving their performance on web search and deep research tasks.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle with long-horizon tasks because accumulated context leads to long-context degradation and reasoning failures. Existing context management techniques either require modifying or retraining the agent itself, which is impractical for closed-source agents, or rely on fixed heuristics like summarization that don’t adapt to different agents’ needs. The authors aim to create a general, trainable context manager that can work with frozen, possibly closed-source agents and tailor its strategy to the agent’s capabilities.
AI.method: The authors introduce Adaptive Context Management (AdaCoM), an external LLM trained to manage another frozen LLM agent’s context. AdaCoM can flexibly modify the context (e.g., prune, compress, or preserve information) and is trained with end-to-end reinforcement learning on web search and deep research tasks. Different agents are kept frozen while AdaCoM learns policies that balance preserving task-relevant constraints and progress with pruning stale or harmful content. The study also analyzes learned strategies and performs transfer experiments across agents of varying capabilities.
AI.result: Across multiple LLM agents and benchmarks for web search and deep research, AdaCoM significantly improves task performance compared to baseline context strategies. It successfully preserves important task constraints and progress while discarding stale context that could cause reasoning failures. The analysis uncovers a Fidelity-Reliability Trade-off: higher-performing agents benefit from higher-fidelity context preservation, while weaker agents perform better when their context is more aggressively compressed to maintain reliable reasoning. Transfer experiments show that an AdaCoM trained on one agent tends to generalize best to agents with similar vanilla ReAct performance.
AI.conclusion: AdaCoM demonstrates that an external, RL-trained LLM can effectively manage the context of frozen LLM agents, boosting performance on long-horizon tasks without modifying the underlying agents. The discovered Fidelity-Reliability Trade-off indicates that optimal context strategies depend on the agent’s inherent capability, and that context managers are most reusable among similarly capable agents. This provides a practical route to build reusable, general context management modules for real-world LLM agent systems, including closed-source models.

title: Generating and Refining Dynamic Evaluation Rubrics for LLM-as-a-Judge
summary: LLM-as-a-Judge is a scalable alternative to human evaluation, yet existing rubric-based methods rely on human-annotated data such as reference answers or expert-crafted rubrics. We propose to automatically generate fine-grained evaluation rubrics without any human annotation. Our training-free method generates rubrics at dataset-specific and instance-specific granularities, achieving performance competitive with existing methods across four benchmarks. We further present a method that iteratively fine-tunes a rubric generator model via meta-judge reward signals. The fine-tuned generator outperforms all existing baselines in both pairwise and pointwise evaluation. Notably, a fine-tuned 14B rubric generator outperforms a much larger proprietary model at rubric generation, showing the effectiveness of our fine-tuning strategy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces automatic, fine-grained rubric generation for LLM-as-a-Judge, removing the need for human-annotated rubrics or references, and shows these methods match or surpass existing baselines, even against larger proprietary models.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-as-a-Judge approaches require human-created artifacts like reference answers or hand-crafted rubrics, which are costly, limit scalability, and may be dataset-specific. The authors want a way to evaluate model outputs automatically and reliably without relying on such human supervision, enabling broader, cheaper, and more adaptable evaluation.
AI.method: They propose a training-free approach that uses LLMs to automatically generate fine-grained rubrics at both dataset-specific and instance-specific levels. These rubrics guide subsequent judgments of model outputs. In addition, they introduce an iterative fine-tuning framework for a rubric generator model, where meta-judge reward signals—feedback from evaluations of rubric usefulness or quality—are used to update the generator. This produces a specialized rubric generator that better captures evaluation criteria over time.
AI.result: The training-free rubric generation approach performs competitively with state-of-the-art rubric-based LLM-as-a-Judge methods on four evaluation benchmarks. The iteratively fine-tuned rubric generator further improves performance, outperforming all existing baselines in both pairwise (comparing two answers) and pointwise (scoring a single answer) settings. A 14B-parameter fine-tuned rubric generator even surpasses a much larger proprietary model at the task of rubric generation.
AI.conclusion: Automatic rubric generation, both training-free and fine-tuned, can replace human-authored rubrics and reference answers in LLM-as-a-Judge setups while maintaining or improving evaluation quality. The meta-judge-guided fine-tuning strategy is particularly effective, enabling a relatively smaller open model to outperform larger proprietary systems on rubric generation, suggesting a practical and scalable path toward high-quality automatic evaluation frameworks.

title: PReMISE: Policy Rubrics as Measurement Specifications for LLM Judges
summary: LLM judges are increasingly used to evaluate open-ended responses, but their scores depend strongly on the rubrics that condition them. A vague rubric asking for a response to be ``helpful and factual'' can reward polished answers that invent facts or violate user intent. We treat reusable rubrics as measurement specifications: changing the rubric changes the response quality measurement induced by a fixed judge. We introduce PReMISE, a framework that, given pairwise human-preference data, (i) discovers a policy-level rubric set, and (ii) audits any rubric set under LLM-judge use along four axes: structural adequacy, reliability, preference fit, and adversarial robustness. Across rubric sources no raw source is simultaneously reliable, preference-predictive, and adversarially robust; and high inter-rater agreement does not imply low exploitability. PReMISE is the only rubric source to score non-trivially on applicability, specificity, and effective dimensionality simultaneously. We contribute two audit-targeted repair operations: preference-rank selection raises judge accuracy on paired responses from $65.0\%$ to $68.6\%$, competitive with the strongest rubric-discovery baselines and leading on two of three judges in our cross-judge sweep; reliability-constrained refinement reduces the rate at which exploit responses receive high scores from $46.4\%$ to $36.0\%$ with little change in inter-judge agreement ($α{=}.531\to.519$).

AI.tldr: The paper presents PReMISE, a framework for designing and auditing reusable rubrics for LLM-based evaluation, using human preference data to improve reliability, preference alignment, and robustness to adversarial exploits.
AI.motivation: LLM judges are widely used to score open-ended responses, but their evaluations are highly sensitive to the rubrics used, especially vague ones like “helpful and factual,” which can reward plausible but incorrect or misaligned answers. There is no principled, measurement-oriented way to design and audit such rubrics, and existing rubric sources tend to trade off reliability, alignment with human preferences, and robustness to gaming. The authors aim to treat rubrics as formal measurement specifications and to systematically discover and repair them so that LLM-based evaluation better reflects human preferences and is harder to exploit.
AI.method: The authors formalize reusable rubrics as measurement specifications for LLM judges and propose PReMISE, a framework that, given pairwise human preference data over responses, (1) discovers a set of policy-level rubrics and (2) audits any rubric set used with LLM judges along four axes: structural adequacy (e.g., dimensionality, applicability, specificity), reliability (e.g., inter-rater agreement), preference fit (how well rubric-based judgments predict human preferences), and adversarial robustness (susceptibility to exploitative responses). They also introduce two audit-guided repair operations: (a) preference-rank selection, which chooses rubric elements that best predict human preference rankings, and (b) reliability-constrained refinement, which adjusts rubrics to reduce exploitability while maintaining inter-judge reliability.
AI.result: No existing rubric source examined is simultaneously reliable, predictive of human preferences, and robust to adversarially crafted responses; moreover, high inter-rater agreement does not guarantee low exploitability. PReMISE-generated rubrics achieve non-trivial scores on applicability, specificity, and effective dimensionality at once, outperforming raw alternatives on structural adequacy. Preference-rank selection improves judge accuracy on human-preference pairs from 65.0% to 68.6%, matching or beating strong rubric-discovery baselines and performing best on two of three LLM judges. Reliability-constrained refinement lowers the proportion of exploit responses that receive high scores from 46.4% to 36.0%, with only a small drop in inter-judge agreement (Krippendorff’s alpha from 0.531 to 0.519).
AI.conclusion: Treating rubrics as formal measurement specifications and learning/auditing them via PReMISE yields more structurally adequate, preference-aligned, and robust evaluation schemes for LLM judges than existing rubric sources. Systematic audit and targeted repair operations can simultaneously improve preference-prediction accuracy and reduce exploitable failure modes without substantially sacrificing reliability, highlighting the need for principled rubric design whenever LLMs are used as evaluators.

title: Probing the Prompt KV Cache: Where It Becomes Dispensable
summary: Prior KV cache compression schemes empirically demonstrate that the prompt cache is partially redundant during decoding, dropping or summarising entries with little accuracy loss. We ask when and what kind of redundancy: at which layers, after how many decoding steps, and in what form can the prompt span KV cache be replaced without breaking the task. A controlled splice intervention swept over layer cutoff and decoding steps shows this redundancy is about form (chat template scaffolding) rather than content. Replacing the upper layer prompt span KV cache with KV cache from a chat template scaffold whose user content is a neutral filler recovers near clean accuracy, while zeroing the same slots collapses accuracy. The dissociation replicates across the Qwen3, Gemma 3, and Llama 3 families on multiple datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper studies when and where the prompt KV cache in LLMs is redundant, showing that high-layer KV states for the prompt can be replaced with those from a neutral chat scaffold without hurting accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing KV cache compression methods show that parts of the prompt cache can be removed or summarized with little loss, but it is unclear what type of information is redundant, at which layers, and after how many decoding steps. The authors aim to characterize this redundancy more precisely to better understand and exploit it.
AI.method: They use a controlled splice intervention on the KV cache: for different layers and decoding steps, they replace the prompt-span KV entries with ones coming from a different prompt—specifically, a chat template scaffold where the user content is replaced by neutral filler—or with zeroed-out entries. They systematically sweep over layer cutoffs and generation steps, measuring performance on multiple datasets and models (Qwen3, Gemma 3, Llama 3).
AI.result: Replacing the upper-layer prompt KV cache with KV values produced by a chat template that has neutral filler user content preserves accuracy close to the unperturbed model, whereas simply zeroing those same KV slots causes a sharp drop in accuracy. This pattern holds across several LLM families and datasets, indicating a robust phenomenon.
AI.conclusion: Redundancy in the prompt KV cache primarily concerns the form or structural scaffold of the conversation (chat template) rather than the specific user content. High-layer prompt KV states can be substituted with structurally similar but semantically neutral scaffolds without significantly harming performance, clarifying what aspects of the prompt cache are critical vs. redundant and informing more targeted KV compression schemes.

title: Planner-Centric Reinforcement Learning for Deep Research with Structure-Aware Reward
summary: Deep research tasks require LLMs to plan what to investigate, retrieve evidence, and synthesize long-form answers across multiple branches of inquiry. Existing training paradigms either rely on short-form verifiable QA as a proxy or optimize monolithic long trajectories, which makes planning and execution difficult to disentangle and yields weak credit assignment for the planning process. We propose DecomposeR, a planner-centric deep research framework that represents research plans as typed directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), allowing planning to be made explicit, structured, and rewardable. We train a Qwen3-8B model in two stages: planner reinforcement learning (RL) first learns graph structure and query decomposition to improve research planning, and answerer reinforcement learning (RL) then learns branch-level execution and final synthesis conditioned on the learned plan. By assigning rewards to explicit planner tokens and structured components rather than to a flat trajectory, DecomposeR enables finer-grained optimization of planning while reducing the ambiguity of end-to-end training. Experiments show that DecomposeR-8B improves over strong comparable open baselines by 5.1-8.0 points on popular long-form benchmarks due to improved planning and answering capabilities.

AI.tldr: They introduce DecomposeR, a planner-centric framework where research plans are explicit DAGs; they separately train a planner and an answerer with RL to improve deep research performance of an 8B LLM on long-form tasks.
AI.motivation: Deep research tasks (multi-step, multi-branch inquiry with retrieval and long-form synthesis) need explicit planning plus evidence gathering. Existing training uses short QA proxies or monolithic long trajectories, which confound planning and execution and give poor credit assignment to the planning part. The authors want a framework where planning itself is explicit, structured, and directly trainable/rewardable.
AI.method: Represent a research plan as a typed directed acyclic graph (DAG) encoding sub-questions, dependencies, and retrieval actions. Use a Qwen3-8B model split conceptually into planner and answerer. Stage 1: planner RL optimizes the generation of the DAG structure and query decomposition with rewards on planner tokens and structured DAG components. Stage 2: answerer RL trains branch-level execution (retrieval and reasoning per node) and final synthesis, conditioned on the pre-learned plan. This two-stage RL setup separates planning from execution and enables more precise credit assignment.
AI.result: DecomposeR-8B, the resulting model, outperforms strong open baselines by 5.1–8.0 points on popular long-form evaluation benchmarks, indicating better deep-research planning and answer quality.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, DAG-based planning combined with planner-centric RL and a separate answerer RL stage yields more effective deep research behavior in LLMs than monolithic training. Rewarding structured planner outputs improves credit assignment and leads to measurable gains on long-form benchmarks.

title: Speculative Decoding Across Languages
comment: 10 pages, 11 figures, submitted to ACL ARR May 2026
summary:
Speculative decoding has become a crucial component of large language model (LLM) inference, enabling faster generation by drafting multiple tokens and verifying them in parallel. However, small draft models tend to suffer from disproportionately poor multilingual capabilities. Thus, when generating text in a non-English language, speculative decoding is far less effective.
  We compare three strategies to improve speculative decoding efficiency for eleven languages: finetuning the draft model on task-specific data (translation); finetuning the draft model on unlabeled monolingual corpora; and training simple n-gram draft models on the same monolingual corpora. We evaluate efficiency on translation (from English into the target language) and the held-out task of story generation. We find that while task-specific distillation can significantly improve efficiency, distilled models generalize poorly to a new task. Meanwhile, n-gram draft models, despite lower acceptance rates, consistently provide large speed-ups due to much faster draft generation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to make speculative decoding efficient for non-English generation in LLMs, comparing multilingual fine-tuning and simple n-gram draft models.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference but works poorly for non-English text because small draft models lack strong multilingual ability, reducing its effectiveness. The authors want to understand how to restore or improve efficiency across multiple languages.
AI.method: They evaluate three strategies across eleven target languages: (1) finetuning the draft model on task-specific parallel data for translation; (2) finetuning the draft model on unlabeled monolingual corpora; and (3) training lightweight n-gram draft models on the same monolingual data. They then measure speculative decoding efficiency on a translation task (English → target language) and on a held-out story generation task, focusing on acceptance rates, speed-ups, and generalization across tasks.
AI.result: Task-specific finetuning (distillation) of the draft model can greatly improve speculative decoding efficiency on the translation task but does not transfer well to the unseen story generation task. In contrast, n-gram draft models, despite lower token acceptance rates, achieve robust and substantial speed-ups overall due to their very fast draft generation process.
AI.conclusion: Improving speculative decoding for multilingual LLMs is not just about boosting acceptance rates with a more capable neural draft model. Simpler n-gram draft models, trained on monolingual data, can be more effective in practice because their faster draft generation compensates for lower acceptance, and they generalize better across tasks than task-specific distilled draft models.

title: SLAT: Segment-Level Adaptive Trimming for Efficient CoT Reasoning
summary: Recent advances in Large Reasoning Models have significantly improved chain-of-thought (CoT) capabilities via reinforcement learning (RL). However, generated reasoning chains frequently suffer from structural redundancy (i.e., \emph{overthinking}), incurring high computational overhead without improving answer correctness. Existing mitigation strategies typically rely on token-uniform length penalties, which provide coarse, segment-agnostic pressure toward shorter outputs and can inadvertently suppress useful reasoning alongside redundancy. To address this, we demonstrate that inefficiency concentrates in high-probability segments with low marginal utility. We derive a theoretical characterization of segment suboptimality under the correctness-length trade-off objective and propose \textsc{SLAT} (Segment-Level Adaptive Trimming), an RL framework that selectively suppresses redundant segments based on this criterion. Empirical results on standard benchmarks indicate that \textsc{SLAT} establishes a superior accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier, reducing reasoning length by $50\%$ relative to uncompressed baselines while maintaining competitive accuracy. Overall, our results suggest that theoretically grounded, segment-aware trimming is a promising direction for efficient CoT reasoning in large language models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SLAT, a segment-level adaptive trimming RL framework to reduce redundant reasoning in large reasoning models while preserving accuracy, achieving ~50% shorter chain-of-thought with comparable correctness.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought reasoning in large reasoning models has improved with RL, but generated chains often contain redundant, low-utility segments (overthinking) that waste computation without improving correctness. Existing methods use uniform length penalties that push all parts of the output to be shorter, potentially harming useful reasoning. A more precise way is needed to identify and remove only inefficient parts of the reasoning process.
AI.method: The authors theoretically study where inefficiency arises in chain-of-thought outputs and show it tends to concentrate in high-probability segments that add little marginal utility for correctness. Based on this, they define a notion of segment suboptimality under a correctness–length trade-off objective. They then develop SLAT (Segment-Level Adaptive Trimming), an RL-based training framework that operates at the segment level, assigning penalties and selectively suppressing or trimming segments deemed redundant according to their suboptimality, instead of applying a uniform penalty across all tokens.
AI.result: On standard chain-of-thought reasoning benchmarks, SLAT improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off compared with prior approaches. The method reduces the average reasoning length by about 50% relative to uncompressed baselines, while keeping answer accuracy competitive, thereby establishing a better Pareto frontier of performance versus computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Segment-aware, theoretically principled trimming of chain-of-thought outputs is more effective than uniform length penalties for improving efficiency in large reasoning models. By focusing on suppressing only redundant, low-marginal-utility segments, SLAT substantially cuts reasoning length without sacrificing much accuracy, indicating that adaptive segment-level control is a promising direction for efficient CoT reasoning systems.

title: AI for Monitoring and Classifying Data Used in Research Literature
summary: While platforms like Google Scholar and Semantic Scholar track citations for academic papers, no comparable infrastructure exists for monitoring dataset usage in research literature, leaving the landscape of data use largely opaque. Addressing this gap is critical for transparency, reproducibility, and monitoring of impact, yet progress is hindered by inconsistent citation practices, scarce labeled data, and ambiguous references to datasets in the wild. Traditional NLP approaches struggle with these challenges, motivating the shift toward more adaptive, semantically rich models. Building on prior work using LLMs for data mention detection and synthetic data for bootstrapping training, this paper presents an updated methodology for scalable dataset monitoring. We introduce a multitask GLiNER-based framework that jointly performs dataset mention extraction, relation identification, and usage-context classification. To address label scarcity, the pipeline leverages synthetic data generation to produce training examples and LLM-based revalidation to filter incorrect mentions and enforce labeling consistency, together improving reliability, coverage, and output consistency across the training pipeline. This work advances the development of open-source tools for monitoring data use in research literature, contributing to the broader goal of generalizable, unconstrained dataset citation tracking.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an automated, scalable system to detect and analyze how datasets are used and cited across research papers, using a multitask GLiNER-based model plus synthetic data and LLM validation.
AI.motivation: Unlike academic papers, datasets lack standardized, trackable citation infrastructure, making it hard to see how data is reused, to ensure transparency and reproducibility, or to measure dataset impact. Existing NLP methods struggle with messy, inconsistent, and ambiguous real-world dataset mentions, so a more robust, semantically aware approach is needed.
AI.method: The authors design a multitask framework based on GLiNER that jointly performs (1) extraction of dataset mentions from text, (2) identification of relations between mentions and their attributes, and (3) classification of the context of dataset usage. To overcome label scarcity, they generate synthetic training data and then apply an LLM-based revalidation step to remove incorrect mentions and harmonize labels, improving the quality and consistency of the training corpus.
AI.result: The resulting pipeline can more reliably detect dataset mentions, characterize how datasets are used, and maintain consistent labeling compared to traditional approaches, enabling scalable monitoring of data use across research literature. It improves reliability, coverage, and output consistency throughout the training and inference pipeline, though detailed metrics are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: By combining a multitask GLiNER architecture with synthetic data generation and LLM-based label validation, the paper advances open-source infrastructure for tracking how datasets are cited and used in research. This contributes toward a generalizable, large-scale system for transparent, unconstrained monitoring of dataset usage in the scientific literature.

title: COMPASS: Cognitive MCTS-Guided Process Alignment for Safe Search Agents
summary: LLM-powered search agents enable multi-step reasoning and tool use. However, these capabilities introduce retrieval-induced safety degradation, as harmful intents may decompose into seemingly innocuous sub-queries that lead to unsafe outcomes. Existing alignment methods struggle to capture sparse safety signals and fail to supervise diverse violations across multi-step interactions. We propose COMPASS, a Cognitive MCTS-Guided Process Alignment framework designed to achieve robust safety alignment throughout the agent workflow while preserving general utility. COMPASS integrates cognitive tree exploration (CTE) to efficiently synthesize stealthy attack trajectories, and introspective step-wise alignment (ISA) to isolate risky intermediate actions for fine-grained process supervision. Empirical results show that COMPASS achieves a favorable safety-utility trade-off while requiring substantially less training data.

AI.tldr: Introduces COMPASS, a framework to keep LLM-based search agents safe across multi-step tool-use workflows while preserving their usefulness.
AI.motivation: LLM search agents can break a harmful user goal into harmless-looking sub-queries, so traditional safety checks at the input or final output miss these hidden unsafe trajectories. Existing alignment methods don’t capture sparse safety signals or the many ways multi-step interactions can go wrong.
AI.method: Proposes COMPASS, which has two main components: (1) Cognitive Tree Exploration (CTE), a Monte Carlo Tree Search–style method to actively explore and synthesize stealthy, multi-step attack trajectories; and (2) Introspective Step-wise Alignment (ISA), which inspects each intermediate action in an agent’s reasoning/tool-use chain to detect and supervise risky steps at a fine-grained level. These are combined into a process alignment framework that trains the agent to remain safe across the entire workflow rather than only at endpoints.
AI.result: Experiments show that COMPASS reduces unsafe behaviors in LLM-powered search agents while keeping their general performance high, and it reaches this safety–utility balance using significantly less alignment training data than baselines.
AI.conclusion: By using search-based exploration of stealthy attacks and per-step introspective supervision, COMPASS provides a more data-efficient and robust way to align multi-step LLM agents, preventing retrieval-induced safety degradation without heavily sacrificing utility.

title: ImmigrationQA: A Source-Grounded Dataset and Small-Model Adaptation for U.S. Immigration Law
comment: 12 pages, 4 tables. Dataset (17,058 QA pairs), fine-tuned model, and code are publicly released
summary: U.S. immigration law spans thousands of pages of official policy, federal regulations, and procedural guidance that change frequently and carry high stakes for petitioners who lack legal representation. We describe the construction of ImmigrationQA, a source-grounded question-answering dataset of 17,058 pairs across 13 immigration subdomains, and the fine-tuning of a Llama 3.2 3B Instruct model on that dataset using parameter-efficient LoRA. The corpus was assembled from 11 primary and secondary sources -- including the USCIS Policy Manual, 8 CFR, BIA precedent decisions, and community Q&A -- yielding 10,056 validated canonical documents and 18,308 text chunks. Structured QA pairs were generated from these chunks using Claude Sonnet 4.6 via five mode-specific prompts, with 22 pairs rejected for insufficient source-span overlap. The fine-tuned model was evaluated against a held-out split of 993 pairs using LLM-as-judge scoring on a 101-example stratified sample. The fine-tuned model scored a mean of 1.08/3.0 (16.8% fully correct; 101-example stratified eval) versus the Llama 3 8B base model at 0.85/3.0 (4% fully correct), a relative improvement of 27% in mean score; a zero-shot Claude Sonnet baseline scored 1.52/3.0 (25% fully correct). The fine-tuned model shows concentrated improvement in procedural subdomains (travel documents, adjustment of status, nonimmigrant visas) while remaining weak on complex legal reasoning and time-sensitive statistics. The full pipeline ran for approximately $29 in cloud compute. All artifacts -- dataset, model, code, and prompt templates -- are publicly released. The system is not a substitute for legal counsel and does not reflect regulatory changes after the corpus crawl date.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ImmigrationQA, a new source-grounded QA dataset for U.S. immigration law, and a small Llama model fine-tuned on it.
AI.motivation: U.S. immigration law is complex, frequently changing, and difficult to navigate, especially for unrepresented petitioners. Existing models and resources are not tailored to this domain or grounded tightly in authoritative sources, which creates risks in high-stakes scenarios. The authors aim to build a domain-specific, source-grounded QA resource and model to improve accessibility while retaining traceability to official materials.
AI.method: The authors construct a corpus from 11 authoritative and secondary immigration law sources (e.g., USCIS Policy Manual, 8 CFR, BIA decisions, community Q&A). They process it into 10,056 validated documents and 18,308 text chunks. Using Claude Sonnet 4.6 with five tailored prompting modes, they generate structured, source-grounded QA pairs, filtering out pairs with insufficient overlap to the source text, yielding 17,058 QA pairs in 13 subdomains. They then fine-tune a Llama 3.2 3B Instruct model with LoRA on this dataset and evaluate it on a held-out test split (993 pairs) via LLM-as-judge scoring on a 101-example stratified sample, comparing performance to the Llama 3 8B base model and a zero-shot Claude baseline. They also track costs and release all artifacts.
AI.result: The final dataset contains 17,058 QA pairs across 13 immigration subdomains derived from 10,056 canonical documents and 18,308 chunks, after rejecting some QA pairs for weak source alignment. The LoRA-fine-tuned Llama 3.2 3B model achieves a mean LLM-judge score of 1.08/3.0 with 16.8% fully correct answers on the stratified test sample, outperforming the Llama 3 8B base model (0.85/3.0, 4% fully correct), representing a 27% relative improvement in mean score. However, the fine-tuned model still underperforms a zero-shot Claude Sonnet baseline (1.52/3.0, 25% fully correct). Performance gains are strongest in procedural subdomains like travel documents, adjustment of status, and nonimmigrant visas, with persistent weaknesses in complex legal reasoning and time-sensitive numerical/statistical questions. The full pipeline cost about $29 in cloud compute.
AI.conclusion: A small open LLM can be effectively specialized to U.S. immigration law using a modestly sized, source-grounded QA dataset at low computational cost, yielding meaningful performance gains over its base version in procedural questions. Nevertheless, the fine-tuned model does not yet match stronger proprietary systems, especially on nuanced legal reasoning and up-to-date statistics. The authors release all dataset, model, code, and prompts to support further research, while emphasizing that the system is not a replacement for professional legal advice and may become outdated as regulations change.

title: Distilling LLM Feedback for Lean Theorem Proving
summary: Post-training for reasoning models typically combines supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards, most commonly with GRPO. However, this algorithm suffers from sparse rewards, limited exploration, and mode collapse. Building upon recent works on self-distillation, we propose Feedback Distillation, a training method where the model is trained to match, at the token level, its own distribution conditioned on privileged feedback produced by a language model. Feedback Distillation offers token-level supervision and can inject external knowledge. Evaluating our method for Lean4 theorem-proving, we find that Feedback Distillation maintains greater diversity in generated trajectories than GRPO, yielding higher policy entropy and better pass@k scaling. The two methods are complementary: initializing GRPO from a Feedback Distillation checkpoint outperforms either method alone. All in all, our results suggest a promising avenue to improve post-training for complex reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Feedback Distillation, a token-level post-training method for reasoning models that uses privileged feedback from a language model, improving diversity, entropy, and pass@k in Lean4 theorem proving, and complements GRPO.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training methods for reasoning models rely heavily on GRPO-style reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards, which suffers from sparse rewards, limited exploration, and mode collapse. There is a need for a training approach that provides denser supervision, better exploration, and can leverage external knowledge while still improving complex reasoning performance.
AI.method: The authors propose Feedback Distillation, a self-distillation-based training approach where a reasoning model is trained, at the token level, to match its own output distribution when conditioned on privileged feedback generated by a language model. This feedback can encode additional information or guidance, effectively turning it into token-level supervision and a mechanism to inject external knowledge. They compare this method against GRPO and also study a combined approach where GRPO is initialized from a Feedback Distillation checkpoint.
AI.result: On the Lean4 theorem-proving benchmark, Feedback Distillation generates more diverse solution trajectories than GRPO, as indicated by higher policy entropy and better pass@k scaling. Furthermore, initializing GRPO training from a model trained with Feedback Distillation leads to performance that surpasses using either GRPO or Feedback Distillation alone.
AI.conclusion: Feedback Distillation is an effective complement to GRPO for post-training reasoning models, addressing some of GRPO's weaknesses such as sparse rewards and mode collapse by providing token-level supervision and enabling external knowledge injection. The combined use of Feedback Distillation and GRPO yields better theorem-proving performance, suggesting that feedback-conditioned self-distillation is a promising direction for improving complex reasoning post-training.

title: Semantic Motion Anchors: Bridging Motion and Meaning in Co-Speech Gestures
summary: Learning a shared representation between spoken text and gesture is central to co-speech gesture retrieval, synthesis, and understanding, but remains challenging for semantically meaningful gestures whose communicative intent is not captured by motion alone. Direct contrastive alignment between transcripts and continuous motion embeddings often overemphasizes low-level kinematics and misses the symbolic content of semantic gestures. We propose semantic motion anchors, natural-language abstractions of gesture motion capturing physical form and communicative intent. Our method discretizes 3D gestures into body-hand motion primitives, verbalizes them into structured descriptions, and grounds them in the transcript to provide auxiliary contrastive supervision. On BEAT2, our method improves text-to-gesture R@1 by 8.2% over a direct text-motion baseline and outperforms prior retrieval approaches on text to gesture and gesture to text retrieval directions. Beyond aggregate retrieval metrics, semantic motion anchor supervision helps retrieve gestures that are semantically meaningful for the spoken query, rather than defaulting to generic motion patterns. A downstream retrieval-augmented gesture generation study showed that users significantly preferred gestures retrieved by our approach over a retrieval-augmented generation baseline, demonstrating that semantically grounded retrieval translates to gestures that better convey communicative intent in downstream generation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces semantic motion anchors—natural-language descriptions of gesture primitives—to better align spoken text and 3D co-speech gestures, improving semantic retrieval and generation quality.
AI.motivation: Existing text–gesture alignment methods rely on direct contrastive learning between transcripts and continuous motion embeddings, which tend to capture low-level kinematics rather than higher-level symbolic or semantic meaning of gestures. This leads to retrieval and synthesis systems that favor generic motion and fail to reflect the communicative intent present in semantically meaningful gestures. There is a need for a representation and supervision signal that bridges physical gesture form and linguistic meaning more explicitly.
AI.method: The authors propose semantic motion anchors, which are natural-language abstractions of gesture motion that encode both physical form and communicative intent. Their pipeline discretizes continuous 3D co-speech gestures into body-hand motion primitives, converts these primitives into structured verbalized descriptions, and grounds these descriptions in the spoken transcript. These anchors are then used to provide auxiliary contrastive supervision alongside the usual text–motion alignment objective, effectively inserting an interpretable, discrete semantic layer between raw motion and language. The approach is evaluated on the BEAT2 dataset for retrieval and in a retrieval-augmented generation setup.
AI.result: On the BEAT2 benchmark, the proposed method improves text-to-gesture retrieval performance, achieving an 8.2% gain in R@1 over a direct text–motion contrastive baseline and outperforming prior retrieval approaches for both text-to-gesture and gesture-to-text directions. Qualitative and retrieval analyses indicate that the model trained with semantic motion anchors more often retrieves gestures that are semantically aligned with the spoken content rather than defaulting to generic kinematic patterns. In a user study of retrieval-augmented gesture generation, participants significantly preferred gestures retrieved using the proposed method over those from a competitive baseline, suggesting better perceived communicative adequacy.
AI.conclusion: Semantic motion anchors—discrete, verbalized descriptions of gesture primitives—provide effective auxiliary supervision for learning a shared representation between speech transcripts and co-speech gestures. By explicitly modeling both gesture form and communicative intent, the method improves semantic quality in retrieval and leads to more communicatively appropriate gestures in downstream generation tasks. This demonstrates the value of inserting an interpretable, language-like intermediate representation in multimodal alignment between speech and gesture.

title: UniScale: Adaptive Unified Inference Scaling via Online Joint Optimization of Model Routing and Test-Time Scaling
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: In real-world deployments of large language models (LLMs), balancing inference quality and computational cost has become a central challenge. Existing approaches tackle this trade-off along two largely independent dimensions: model routing, which switches among models of different scales to match request complexity, and test-time scaling (TTS), which adjusts inference-time compute within a fixed model for fine-grained control. However, this decoupled design introduces inherent limitations. Model routing yields coarse-grained, discrete performance changes due to the sparse set of model scales, while single-model TTS often encounters capacity ceilings and exhibits diminishing returns as compute increases. Moreover, treating the two mechanisms separately restricts adaptability in dynamic inference environments. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Unified Inference Scaling (UIS), which unifies model routing and TTS in a single optimization space. Building on this formulation, we propose UniScale, an online framework that models adaptive UIS as a contextual multi-armed bandit problem and learns inference policies via LinUCB. The framework incorporates efficiency-aware learning and cost modeling to ensure stable and scalable optimization over high-dimensional action spaces. Evaluation shows that UniScale effectively exploits the synergy in the UIS space to deliver a fine-grained and consistently better quality-cost trade-off across diverse, dynamic inference scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Unified Inference Scaling (UIS) and an online framework, UniScale, to jointly optimize model routing and test-time scaling for large language models, achieving finer and more efficient quality–cost trade-offs.
AI.motivation: In practice, running LLMs is expensive, and current methods manage the quality–cost trade-off in two separate ways: (1) model routing between small and large models, which is coarse-grained, and (2) test-time scaling within a single model, which hits capacity limits and gives diminishing returns. Treating these dimensions independently limits flexibility and efficiency, especially in dynamic, real-world settings where request difficulty and cost constraints vary over time.
AI.method: The authors define Unified Inference Scaling (UIS), a joint optimization space that treats both model selection (routing across scales) and test-time compute choices as a single decision problem. On top of this, they design UniScale, an online framework that casts adaptive UIS as a contextual multi-armed bandit using LinUCB. UniScale learns policies that map request features (context) to UIS actions (choices of model and compute level), and incorporates explicit cost models and efficiency-aware learning to remain stable and scalable in high-dimensional action spaces.
AI.result: Experiments across varied, dynamic inference scenarios show that UniScale takes advantage of the combined UIS space to achieve more fine-grained and consistently better quality–cost trade-offs than baselines that only do model routing or only test-time scaling. It maintains strong performance while adapting to changing conditions and constraints.
AI.conclusion: Unifying model routing and test-time scaling into a single UIS optimization framework overcomes the granularity, capacity, and adaptability limits of treating them separately. The proposed UniScale system demonstrates that bandit-based, online learning in this unified space can systematically yield superior, more flexible quality–compute trade-offs for LLM inference in realistic, dynamic deployment settings.

title: The Architecture of Errors: From Universal Impossibility to Patch-Local LLM Reliability
summary: Universal LLM reliability is not a finite-library problem: across all possible tasks, tools, schemas, knowledge sources, and evaluator expectations, new intervention-distinguishable failure modes can appear without bound, so no finite intervention dictionary can guarantee bounded residual error for every such mode. But deployed systems do not operate over the whole universe. They operate inside operationally bounded patches (legal review, medical RAG, code repair, customer-support agents, contract extraction) with recurring tasks, schemas, tools, and evaluator expectations. Within such patches, empirical evidence suggests failures are sparse, repetitive, and concentrated in a small recurring catalogue, so reliability becomes a local catalogue-discovery and intervention-coverage problem rather than an exponential token-length problem. We formalize this transition with two propositions and one corollary. Proposition 1 is the worst-case-mode-wise negative result: no finite intervention dictionary covers every distinguishable failure mode of an unbounded domain. Corollary 1 is the inverse-discovery implication: the logarithmic upper bound on mode discovery cannot accommodate linearly more distinct tail modes without exponentially more observed hard-failure events. Proposition 2 is the positive patch-local result: under log active-mode exposure and head-heavy coverage, a sufficient per-hard-decision intervention budget grows polylogarithmically in sequence length and becomes domain-constant once the patch catalogue saturates. The framework relocates rather than dissolves long-context difficulty: where the number of hard decisions itself grows with task length, reliability remains hard; the contribution is to identify the on-axis intervention rather than to make those regimes easy.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that while large language models (LLMs) cannot be made universally reliable with any finite set of interventions across all possible domains, in practical, bounded application domains (“patches”), failures are sparse and repetitive, so reliability can be managed by discovering and covering a finite catalogue of local failure modes.
AI.motivation: Current discussions of LLM reliability often implicitly treat the problem as universal: trying to handle every possible failure mode across all tasks, tools, and contexts. This makes reliability appear intractable because new, distinguishable failures can always appear in an unbounded domain. However, real deployed systems operate in narrower, well-defined domains (like legal review or medical RAG) where tasks and expectations repeat. The authors are motivated to formalize why reliability is more tractable in such bounded “patches,” and to clarify how intervention budgets should scale with sequence length and domain complexity.
AI.method: The authors provide a formal, theoretical framework using propositions and a corollary. They model failure modes, intervention dictionaries, and task domains, distinguishing between an unbounded global domain and bounded operational patches. Proposition 1 proves a worst-case impossibility result: no finite set of interventions can cover all distinguishable failure modes in an unbounded domain. Corollary 1 then quantifies the difficulty of discovering many distinct tail failure modes, showing it requires exponentially more observed hard failures if discovery is bounded logarithmically. Proposition 2 presents a constructive, positive result for bounded patches, showing that under assumptions of sparse active modes and head-heavy coverage, the required intervention budget per hard decision grows only polylogarithmically with sequence length and eventually becomes constant once the local failure catalogue saturates.
AI.result: The theoretical results are: (1) a negative universality result (Proposition 1) that rules out any finite intervention dictionary guaranteeing bounded residual error over all possible tasks and failure modes; (2) a corollary (Corollary 1) that shows that discovering linearly more distinct tail failure modes would require exponentially more observed hard failures if mode discovery scales only logarithmically; and (3) a positive, patch-local result (Proposition 2) that in bounded domains with sparse, repetitive failures, the per-hard-decision intervention budget grows only polylogarithmically with sequence length and plateaus at a domain-specific constant once the local failure catalogue is saturated.
AI.conclusion: LLM reliability is not solvable as a universal finite-library problem, but within realistically bounded operational domains, it becomes a tractable catalogue-discovery and intervention-coverage problem. Long-context difficulty is not eliminated but reframed: reliability remains challenging when the number of genuinely hard decisions scales with context length, yet the appropriate focus is on identifying and covering the operative failure modes within a given patch. Thus, practical reliability engineering should prioritize local catalogue building and patch-specific interventions rather than seeking a single universal intervention library.

title: BilliardPhys-Bench: Benchmarking Physical Reasoning and Visual Dynamics of Multimodal LLMs
summary: Current multimodal models handle static image recognition well, but intuitive physical reasoning remains a weakness. Predicting how objects will move and interact from a single image is still difficult for these systems. We present BilliardPhys-Bench, a benchmark for physical reasoning in synthetic billiards environments. Its procedural engine generates randomized scenarios with friction and elastic collisions. The benchmark tests three abilities: (1) predicting ball-to-ball collisions, (2) reasoning about wall bounces, and (3) estimating final ball positions after motion stops. We evaluate recent MLLMs from the GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Qwen families. Performance drops as simulation time increases and scene geometry grows more complex. We also observe a consistent failure mode we call "stasis bias": when the correct physical outcome is harder to infer, models tend to predict no interaction. These findings show where current MLLMs break down on visual dynamics and point toward the need for better physical inductive biases in multimodal architectures.

AI.tldr: Introduces BilliardPhys-Bench, a synthetic billiards benchmark to test MLLMs’ intuitive physical reasoning about object motion and interactions from a single image.
AI.motivation: Multimodal large language models are strong at static image understanding but weak at intuitive physical reasoning, especially predicting future object motions and interactions. There is a lack of focused benchmarks that isolate and stress-test this ability in controlled environments, making it hard to diagnose where current models fail at visual dynamics.
AI.method: The authors design BilliardPhys-Bench, a synthetic billiards environment driven by a procedural physics engine that randomizes table layouts, ball positions, friction, and elastic collisions. They define three task types: (1) predicting whether and how balls collide, (2) reasoning about trajectories involving wall bounces, and (3) estimating final ball positions once motion ceases. They then evaluate a set of recent multimodal LLMs (from GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Qwen families) on these tasks under varying simulation durations and geometric complexity.
AI.result: Across models and families, performance systematically degrades as the simulated time horizon grows and as the spatial layout becomes more complex. A recurring, model-agnostic error pattern emerges: when scenes require harder inference about dynamics, models disproportionately default to predicting that no collisions or interactions occur, which the authors term a “stasis bias.”
AI.conclusion: Current multimodal LLMs lack robust intuitive physics for dynamic visual scenes, especially over longer horizons and complex geometries, and they exhibit a tendency to assume no interactions when reasoning is difficult. BilliardPhys-Bench exposes these weaknesses and suggests that future multimodal architectures need stronger, explicitly encoded physical inductive biases to handle visual dynamics more reliably.

title: COFT: Counterfactual-Conformal Decoding for Fair Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can reveal and amplify societal biases during chain-of-thought (CoT) generation. We present COFT (Chain of Fair Thought), a training-free decoding method that applies token-level fairness control at decode time, with distribution-free marginal validity guarantees (under exchangeability) for any frozen causal language model. COFT operates in three stages. First, it creates a masked counterfactual prompt by replacing sensitive spans with neutral tokens. Second, it compares the factual and masked logit distributions through lightweight logit fusion to attenuate attribute-driven biases. Third, it uses dual-branch split-conformal calibration to certify per-step candidate token sets at a user-chosen risk level. We evaluate COFT across six models and multiple bias benchmarks. Our method reduces standard bias metrics by 30-55% (median 38%) while preserving task utility and language quality. Reasoning accuracies remain unchanged within run-to-run noise margins. The computational overhead is modest, equivalent to one additional cached forward pass (<=11%). COFT offers a clear, auditable path to safer CoT generation with significant bias reduction, negligible utility loss, and no requirement for retraining, auxiliary classifiers, or weight access.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces COFT, a decoding-time method to reduce societal bias in chain-of-thought outputs from large language models without retraining, auxiliary models, or access to weights.
AI.motivation: LLMs often exhibit and even amplify societal biases, especially in their step-by-step chain-of-thought reasoning. Existing debiasing approaches typically need retraining, fine-tuning, or extra classifiers, can hurt task performance, and rarely give formal guarantees. The authors are motivated to create a lightweight, plug-and-play decoding strategy that can control token-level bias, maintain model utility, and provide statistical guarantees on fairness-related interventions.
AI.method: COFT (Chain of Fair Thought) is a training-free decoding procedure with three main stages applied to any frozen causal LLM. (1) Prompt masking: It constructs a counterfactual prompt by replacing sensitive spans (e.g., demographic attributes) with neutral tokens, yielding a masked version of the original input. (2) Logit fusion: It compares the model’s token logit distributions under the original and masked prompts and performs a lightweight fusion to down-weight differences attributable to sensitive attributes, thereby attenuating those biases at each decoding step. (3) Split-conformal calibration: Using a dual-branch split-conformal prediction framework, it certifies sets of candidate next tokens at each step, controlling the risk level selected by the user, and giving distribution-free marginal validity guarantees under an exchangeability assumption. This is all done at inference time, with an extra cached forward pass and no parameter updates.
AI.result: Across six different LLMs and multiple bias-oriented benchmarks, COFT reduces bias metrics by 30–55% (median 38%) while maintaining language quality and task performance, including chain-of-thought reasoning accuracy, which stays within normal run-to-run variance. The computational overhead is limited (about one extra cached forward pass, ≤11% overhead).
AI.conclusion: COFT demonstrates that substantial reductions in CoT-related societal biases can be achieved through a purely decoding-time intervention that is model-agnostic and requires no retraining, no access to internal weights, and no auxiliary classifiers. The approach offers an interpretable, auditable mechanism for token-level fairness control with formal distribution-free guarantees, while preserving task utility and imposing only minor computational cost, making it a practical path toward safer chain-of-thought generation in deployed LLMs.

title: A Persona-Based Evaluation Framework for Pluralistic Alignment in Generative AI
summary: Current alignment paradigms for generative artificial intelligence rely predominantly on monolithic benchmarking frameworks that reduce the plurality of human judgment to aggregated statistical baselines, thereby obscuring cultural, demographic, and contextual variability in evaluation. We introduce a state-space constrained emulation framework for AI evaluation that replaces singular assessment functions with a structured manifold of synthetic cognitive profiles representing diverse human perspectives. We show that modern generative architectures can instantiate and maintain these evaluative personas with high consistency, enabling a form of pluralistic, perspective-dependent benchmarking that more closely reflects real-world consensus variability. However, we further analyze the stability of these simulated evaluators under sequential inference and stochastic prompt perturbations, revealing systematic degradation in persona coherence that manifests as state-space drift and semantic inconsistency. These findings suggest that static alignment constraints are insufficient for sustaining robust evaluative behavior over time. Instead, we argue for the necessity of embedding dynamic, viability-driven regulatory mechanisms within generative systems to preserve coherent cognitive emulation. By framing persona-based evaluation as a structured dynamical system over latent representation manifolds, this study provides a foundation for more adaptive, human-aligned, and context-sensitive approaches to AI evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques current one-size-fits-all AI alignment benchmarks and proposes a framework where AI models emulate multiple, diverse human-like evaluative personas to better capture variability in human judgment.
AI.motivation: Existing alignment and benchmarking approaches compress diverse human values and judgments into single aggregated scores, masking cultural and contextual differences and limiting how well AI systems can be aligned with real-world pluralistic perspectives.
AI.method: The authors design a state-space constrained emulation framework in which generative models are prompted or structured to adopt distinct synthetic cognitive profiles (personas). They then evaluate how consistently models can maintain these personas across queries, sequential interactions, and under stochastic prompt perturbations, analyzing behavior as trajectories over a latent state manifold.
AI.result: They find that modern generative models can initially instantiate and follow specified evaluative personas with high consistency, enabling perspective-dependent benchmarking. However, under longer sequences and noisy or varied prompts, persona coherence degrades: model responses drift in latent state-space and exhibit semantic inconsistencies relative to the target profile.
AI.conclusion: Static, one-shot alignment or persona constraints are inadequate for long-term, reliable evaluative emulation.
