MASA has two stages: (1) a hierarchical skill evolution process that iteratively rewrites both general and task-specific skills using hill-climbing and a UCB-guided tree search, driven by environment feedback and profiles of each model’s capabilities; (2) a compact, model-conditioned skill rewriter trained on these evolution trajectories so that, at test time, it can perform the adaptation in one forward pass without costly search.
AI.result: Across three interactive environments and four different LLM backbones, MASA yields the best overall performance, achieving improvements of up to 25.8 points over the strongest existing baseline. The trained rewriter also transfers to new tasks and environments without needing additional search and still outperforms a significantly larger teacher LLM while incurring much lower inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Skill libraries for LLM agents should not be treated as model-agnostic: their effectiveness is strongly backbone-dependent. By explicitly aligning and rewriting skills to each model via MASA, one can substantially boost interactive task performance and obtain a reusable, model-conditioned rewriter that generalizes to new tasks while remaining efficient.

title: HypoAgent: An Agentic Framework for Interactive Abductive Hypothesis Generation over Knowledge Graphs
summary: Abductive reasoning over knowledge graphs aims to generate logical hypotheses that explain observed entities or facts. Existing controllable hypothesis generation methods allow users to guide this process with explicit conditions, but they remain limited in interactive settings: they struggle to ground evolving natural-language intents across multi-turn dialogues and provide little fine-grained diagnosis when generated hypotheses fail. To address these limitations, we propose HypoAgent, an Agentic framework for interactive abductive Hypothesis Generation over knowledge graphs. HypoAgent integrates three agents: an Intent Recognition Agent that grounds user utterances and dialogue history into executable KG conditions, a Hypothesis Generation Agent that performs controllable hypothesis generation according to the extracted user intention, and a Root Cause Analysis Agent that diagnoses unreliable hypothesis fragments and leverages KG neighborhood probing to identify supported refinements. Experiments on commonsense and biomedical domain-specific knowledge graphs demonstrate that HypoAgent achieves state-of-the-art semantic similarity under single-turn, multi-turn, and unconditional settings. Our code is available at https://github.com/HKUST-KnowComp/HypoAgent.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HypoAgent, a multi-agent framework that interactively generates and refines hypotheses over knowledge graphs by understanding user intent, producing controlled abductive explanations, and diagnosing/fixing weak hypothesis parts using KG evidence.
AI.motivation: Abductive reasoning over knowledge graphs is useful for explaining observed facts with logical hypotheses, but current controllable hypothesis generation methods are not well suited for interactive, dialogue-based use. They cannot robustly map evolving natural language queries across multiple turns into precise KG-level constraints and offer limited insight or repair mechanisms when the generated hypotheses are unreliable or partially incorrect. This creates a gap for user-centric, iterative hypothesis exploration and debugging, especially in domains like commonsense and biomedicine.
AI.method: The authors propose HypoAgent, an agentic framework composed of three specialized agents operating over a knowledge graph. (1) An Intent Recognition Agent interprets user utterances and dialogue history, translating them into explicit, executable KG conditions that reflect the user’s current constraints and goals. (2) A Hypothesis Generation Agent performs controllable abductive hypothesis generation conditioned on these KG constraints, producing candidate explanations on the knowledge graph. (3) A Root Cause Analysis Agent inspects generated hypotheses to identify unreliable fragments, probes the local KG neighborhood for supporting evidence, and recommends refined or alternative hypothesis components. The system is evaluated on commonsense and biomedical KGs in single-turn, multi-turn, and unconditional hypothesis generation settings.
AI.result: On benchmarks built from commonsense and biomedical knowledge graphs, HypoAgent attains state-of-the-art performance in terms of semantic similarity between generated and reference hypotheses across different interaction regimes: single-turn, multi-turn dialogue, and unconditional generation. The framework is empirically shown to better align with user-specified conditions and produce more semantically faithful hypotheses than existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that modeling abductive hypothesis generation as an agentic, interactive process over knowledge graphs—through explicit intent grounding, controlled generation, and root-cause-driven refinement—substantially improves the quality and robustness of generated hypotheses. HypoAgent enables more effective, user-guided exploration and debugging of KG-based explanations and establishes a new state of the art on both commonsense and biomedical tasks, suggesting broad applicability of multi-agent designs for interactive reasoning over structured knowledge.

title: MosaicLeaks:Privacy Risks in Querying-in-the-Open for Deep Research Agents
summary: Deep research agents increasingly combine private local documents with external tools like web retrieval, creating a privacy risk: an agent's external queries may leak sensitive information from its local context. This risk is amplified by the mosaic effect, where individual queries may appear harmless but become revealing in aggregate. We introduce MosaicLeaks, a benchmark of 1,001 multi-hop deep research tasks that chain private enterprise documents and a public web corpus, forcing agents to make external queries that depend on local information. We evaluate leakage with an adversary LLM that observes only the agent's external queries and attempts to infer private information at three levels: the agent's research intent, answers to specific private questions and verifiable claims about the enterprise documents. We find that models across families and sizes frequently leak at all three levels, that zero-shot privacy prompting reduces but does not eliminate leakage and that reinforcement learning for task performance alone worsens leakage. To address this, we propose Privacy-Aware Deep Research (PA-DR), an RL framework that combines situational rewards for task success with a learned privacy classifier to provide dense credit assignment over both per-query and mosaic-level leakage. Training Qwen3-4B-Instruct with PA-DR improves accuracy from 48.7% to 58.7% and reduces answer and full-information leakage from 34.0% to 9.9%.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MosaicLeaks, a benchmark to measure privacy leakage in deep research agents that mix private documents with web tools, and proposes a reinforcement learning framework (PA-DR) that improves task performance while significantly reducing information leakage.
AI.motivation: Deep research agents increasingly operate over sensitive local enterprise data while using external tools like web search. Their tool queries may inadvertently reveal private information, especially through the mosaic effect, where multiple individually-benign queries jointly expose sensitive data. Existing evaluations largely ignore this privacy dimension; the authors aim to systematically characterize and mitigate such leakage in realistic multi-hop research settings.
AI.method: The authors build MosaicLeaks, a benchmark of 1,001 multi-hop research tasks requiring agents to reason over both private enterprise documents and public web data, forcing external queries that depend on private context. They define three privacy leakage levels (research intent, specific private answers, verifiable claims) and use an LLM adversary that only sees the agent’s external queries to infer private info. They then propose Privacy-Aware Deep Research (PA-DR), a reinforcement learning framework that augments task-reward with signals from a learned privacy classifier to penalize both per-query and aggregate (mosaic-level) leakage, and train Qwen3-4B-Instruct under this scheme.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple model families and sizes shows substantial privacy leakage at all defined levels when agents perform MosaicLeaks tasks. Simple privacy prompts help but do not prevent leakage, and standard RL aimed only at improving task success actually increases leakage. In contrast, training Qwen3-4B-Instruct with PA-DR boosts task accuracy from 48.7% to 58.7% while reducing answer-level and full-information leakage from 34.0% to 9.9%.
AI.conclusion: MosaicLeaks reveals that current deep research agents systematically leak private information via their external queries, and that naive approaches like privacy prompting or performance-only RL are insufficient. Integrating explicit privacy objectives through PA-DR enables agents to achieve higher task performance and substantially lower leakage, demonstrating that privacy-aware reinforcement learning is a promising direction for deploying deep research systems on sensitive enterprise data.

title: FAM-Bench: A Multimodal Benchmark for Condition-Aware Food-as-Medicine Reasoning
summary: Food-as-Medicine requires models to reason beyond what a dish is or what nutrition it contains: they must decide whether a concrete food choice is appropriate for a specific health condition. Existing food AI benchmarks primarily evaluate dish recognition, recipe understanding, nutrient estimation, or general nutrition question answering, leaving this health-aware decision layer largely untested. We introduce FAM-Bench, a multi-modal Food-as-Medicine benchmark with 2500 nutrition-expert-verified instances across 13 diet-related health conditions. The benchmark contains two complementary tasks: dish-level suitability assessment, where models judge whether a dish is suitable for a condition from its image and ingredient list, and comparative dish analysis, where models rank four candidate dishes by condition-specific suitability. Both tasks require integrating ingredient evidence, visual preparation cues, and clinical nutrition constraints, providing a standardized testbed for grounded health-aware reasoning in language and vision-language models.

AI.tldr: Introduces FAM-Bench, a multimodal benchmark to test whether AI models can judge food suitability for specific health conditions, going beyond basic recognition or nutrient estimation.
AI.motivation: Most current food AI benchmarks focus on recognizing dishes, parsing recipes, estimating nutrients, or answering generic nutrition questions. They do not evaluate the crucial “decision layer” of Food-as-Medicine: determining if a specific food is appropriate for a given diet-related health condition. This work aims to fill that gap so models can be assessed on clinically relevant, health-aware food reasoning, not just perception and description.
AI.method: The authors design FAM-Bench, a benchmark with 2500 expert-verified cases spanning 13 diet-related health conditions. It is multimodal, combining dish images and ingredient lists. The benchmark defines two tasks: (1) dish-level suitability assessment, where a model judges whether a single dish is suitable for a particular condition using both visual and ingredient information; and (2) comparative dish analysis, where a model must rank four candidate dishes for a condition by how suitable they are. Both tasks force models to integrate ingredient-level evidence, visual preparation cues (e.g., frying vs. steaming), and clinical nutrition constraints.
AI.result: They construct and release FAM-Bench with 2500 instances and show that it offers a standardized way to test grounded, health-aware reasoning in language-only and vision-language models. While detailed performance numbers are not given in the abstract, the benchmark itself is the central result: a curated, expert-validated evaluation set targeting Food-as-Medicine reasoning across multiple conditions and modalities.
AI.conclusion: FAM-Bench provides a targeted benchmark for evaluating whether language and vision-language models can perform clinically relevant Food-as-Medicine reasoning, i.e., assess and compare dish suitability for specific diet-related health conditions using both visual and ingredient cues under clinical nutrition constraints. This fills a gap in existing food AI benchmarks that do not test this health-aware decision-making layer.

title: Efficient Diffusion LLMs via Temporal-Spatial Parallel Decoding and Confidence Extrapolation
summary: Diffusion-based large language models (dLLMs) support parallel text generation via iterative denoising, yet inference remains latency-heavy because many steps are spent on redundant refinement and repeated remasking of tokens whose final values are already determined. Prior acceleration methods mainly depend on step-local confidence heuristics or fixed schedules, which are sensitive to prompt and task variation and ignore strong positional effects within a sequence. We cast diffusion decoding as a dynamic control problem and show that token-wise denoising trajectories provide the key signal for reliable control. We propose a trace-aware decoding framework with two components. First, Temporal-Spatial Parallel Decoding (TSPD) uses a lightweight temporalspatial controller that consumes per-token trajectory features, including confidence, entropy, and momentum, together with token position, to decide when a token has converged and can be safely fixed. Second, we introduce Confidence Extrapolation (CE), a training-free state-space module that forecasts future logit trends with uncertainty to support proactive decisions, including safe look-ahead and targeted stabilization when trajectories are oscillatory or underconfident. Together, TSPD and CE reduce unnecessary denoising iterations while preserving output quality, and they compose cleanly with system optimizations such as KV caching.

AI.tldr: They speed up diffusion-based language model decoding by dynamically stopping denoising for tokens that have effectively converged, using information from the whole denoising trajectory rather than only the current step.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based LLMs allow parallel text generation but require many denoising steps, leading to high latency. Existing acceleration relies on simple, step-local confidence thresholds or fixed schedules, which don’t adapt well across prompts, tasks, or token positions and often fail to exploit the rich temporal information in each token’s denoising trajectory.
AI.method: They formulate diffusion decoding as a dynamic control problem and build a trace-aware decoding framework. (1) Temporal-Spatial Parallel Decoding (TSPD) uses a small controller that ingests per-token temporal trajectory features—such as confidence, entropy, and momentum—plus token position to decide when each token has converged and can be frozen. (2) Confidence Extrapolation (CE) is a training-free state-space forecasting module that predicts future logit evolution and its uncertainty, enabling safe look-ahead and stabilizing tokens whose trajectories are oscillatory or not yet confident.
AI.result: Combining TSPD and CE substantially cuts down the number of denoising iterations spent on already-converged tokens, leading to lower inference latency. They preserve output quality while being compatible with typical system optimizations like key–value caching.
AI.conclusion: Using trajectory-aware, token-wise control and simple forecasting over logits allows diffusion-based LLMs to decode faster without sacrificing generation quality, and the proposed TSPD+CE framework slots cleanly into existing inference stacks as a practical speedup method.

title: Answer-Set-Programming-based Abstractions for Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted for publication at the 42nd International Conference on Logic Programming (ICLP 2026). To appear in Theory and Practice of Logic Programming (TPLP)
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) enables autonomous agents to learn policies from experience, but realistic problems often involve enormous state spaces, making learning and generalisation challenging. Abstraction and approximation are therefore essential. Relational Reinforcement Learning (RRL) offers a way to reason about objects and their relations, and the CARCASS framework by Martijn van Otterlo demonstrates how logical representations can model Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) in first-order domains. Originally implemented in Prolog, CARCASS leverages domain knowledge to create powerful abstractions. We explore Answer-Set Programming (ASP), which is a rich and, contrary to Prolog, fully declarative modelling language, to realise CARCASS abstractions. We evaluate our ASP-based implementation in case studies of two domains, viz. Blocks World and Minigrid. Our results indicate that CARCASS with ASP provides a promising approach to constructing abstractions for RL, especially when domain knowledge is available.

AI.tldr: The paper uses Answer-Set Programming to reimplement the CARCASS relational reinforcement learning abstraction framework, showing it works well in first-order domains like Blocks World and Minigrid when domain knowledge is provided.
AI.motivation: Traditional reinforcement learning struggles with very large state spaces, making it hard to learn and generalise. Relational Reinforcement Learning and the CARCASS framework address this by using logical, first-order representations to build abstractions, but CARCASS was originally implemented in Prolog, which is not fully declarative. The authors are motivated to see whether Answer-Set Programming, a richer and fully declarative language, can more effectively realise CARCASS-style abstractions for RL, especially in domains where structured, relational knowledge is natural.
AI.method: The authors take the CARCASS abstraction framework, originally implemented in Prolog, and reimplement its abstractions using Answer-Set Programming. They encode Markov Decision Processes in first-order logical form via ASP, leveraging domain knowledge about objects and relations. They then run case studies in two benchmark domains—Blocks World and Minigrid—to empirically evaluate how well the ASP-based abstractions support RL, comparing behaviour and feasibility rather than raw numerical benchmarks (as suggested by the abstract).
AI.result: Experiments in Blocks World and Minigrid show that CARCASS implemented with ASP can successfully construct useful abstractions for RL in relational domains. The ASP-based approach can exploit available domain knowledge to represent objects, relations, and transition structure in a concise and powerful way. The results indicate that the ASP realisation is practically viable and yields effective abstractions that support learning and generalisation.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that using Answer-Set Programming to realise CARCASS-style relational abstractions is a promising direction for reinforcement learning in large, structured state spaces. ASP’s fully declarative nature and expressive power make it well-suited for encoding domain knowledge and first-order MDPs. When such domain knowledge is available, CARCASS with ASP can provide strong abstractions that improve the tractability and effectiveness of RL, warranting further exploration and development of this approach.

title: Pairwise Reference Alignment as a Model-Level Ordinal Observable
summary:
Pairwise preference data is widely used in language-model evaluation and alignment, often for model ranking, reward modeling, or preference optimization. This note formulates a more basic measurement question: given a reference distribution of pairwise preferences, what model-level quantity is estimated when we test whether a model ranks preferred responses above rejected responses?
  We define pairwise reference alignment as an ordinal observable induced by a model scoring function. Given a reference pair distribution $P_{\mathrm{pair}}$ over triples $(x,y^+,y^-)$, and a scalar model score $S_M(x,y)$, we define the alignment observable as the probability that the model-induced ordering agrees with the reference preference ordering. We further define a centered order-parameter-like statistic and discuss a margin-based extension. The resulting quantities admit simple finite-sample estimators and concentration bounds under independent sampling assumptions.
  This note does not introduce a new benchmark. It provides a conceptual and statistical formulation for pairwise reference alignment, clarifies the role of the reference pair distribution, and distinguishes the general ordinal observable from scoring choices such as normalized log-probability or energy-based scores. We also provide an initial empirical study on Qwen2.5 models and RewardBench, where the proposed statistics increase with model size and instruction tuning and vary across reference-pair subsets as predicted by the formulation.

AI.tldr: Defines a formal statistic for how well a model’s scores agree with human pairwise preferences and studies its properties.
AI.motivation: Pairwise preference data is heavily used to evaluate and align language models, but it is unclear what exact model-level quantity is being estimated when we check whether a model prefers human-preferred responses. The paper aims to formally define this quantity, connect it to the underlying reference distribution of pairs, and clarify how different scoring functions affect the measurement.
AI.method: Formally define “pairwise reference alignment” as an ordinal observable: the probability that a model’s scoring function orders two responses in the same way as a reference preference label, given a distribution over prompt–response pairs. Introduce a centered, order-parameter-like statistic (alignment minus 1/2) and a margin-based variant that also incorporates how strongly one response is preferred. Derive simple finite-sample estimators and concentration bounds under independence assumptions. Empirically evaluate the statistics on Qwen2.5 models using RewardBench and different subsets of preference pairs.
AI.result: The proposed pairwise reference alignment statistics are easy to estimate from finite samples and admit concentration guarantees. Empirically, on Qwen2.5 and RewardBench, the statistics increase with model size and with instruction tuning, and they differ across subsets of preference pairs in a manner consistent with the theoretical formulation, demonstrating that they capture meaningful aspects of alignment with the reference distribution.
AI.conclusion: Pairwise reference alignment provides a clear, formally grounded quantity that standard pairwise preference tests are estimating: the probability that a model’s score-induced ordering agrees with a reference preference ordering. This perspective separates the underlying ordinal observable from arbitrary choices of scoring function, highlights the central role of the reference pair distribution, and offers practical, statistically controlled estimators for analyzing model alignment in pairwise preference settings.

title: AutoSci: A Memory-Centric Agentic System for the Full Scientific Research Lifecycle
summary: Scientific research has traditionally been human-intensive, requiring researchers to coordinate literature, ideas, experiments, manuscripts, and review responses across long project cycles. The rise of LLM-based scientific agents creates an opportunity to automate this process. Such a system must support the full research lifecycle, maintain structured persistent memory across projects, and improve its own research procedures over time. However, existing systems either partially satisfy or fail to satisfy these requirements, leaving a gap for a unified automated scientific research system. As a result, we present AutoSci, a memory-centric agentic system for the full scientific research lifecycle. AutoSci is organized around four modules. SciMem provides schema-governed research memory, separating Long-Term Knowledge Memory for reusable scientific knowledge from Active Research Memory for project-level artifacts such as ideas, experiments, manuscripts, and reviews. SciFlow executes a five-stage lifecycle from literature understanding to rebuttal through a harness that controls state, context, verification, feedback, and orchestration. SciDAG augments difficult skills with DAG-shaped multi-agent operators and reusable stage-specific templates. SciEvolve converts feedback signals from users, experiments, reviews, and external environments into versioned updates to SciMem organization, SciFlow skills, and SciDAG templates. Together, these modules make AutoSci a persistent research environment that can execute, remember, and evolve across research projects. The code repository is available at https://github.com/skyllwt/AutoSci.

AI.tldr: AutoSci is a memory-centric, multi-module LLM agent system that automates and continually improves the full scientific research lifecycle.
AI.motivation: Scientific research is labor-intensive and spans long, complex project cycles, requiring coordination of literature, ideas, experiments, manuscripts, and review responses. With the emergence of LLM-based agents, there is an opportunity to automate and scale this end-to-end process, but current systems either cover only parts of the lifecycle or lack persistent, structured memory and self-improvement capabilities. This leaves a gap for a unified, automated system that can support full projects, remember across them, and evolve over time.
AI.method: The authors design AutoSci, a modular, agentic framework centered on persistent, structured memory. It has four key components: (1) SciMem, a schema-governed memory layer that separates reusable Long-Term Knowledge Memory from project-level Active Research Memory (ideas, experiments, drafts, reviews). (2) SciFlow, a controller that runs a five-stage research lifecycle—from literature understanding through rebuttal—managing state, context, verification, feedback, and orchestration. (3) SciDAG, which encapsulates complex skills as DAG-shaped multi-agent operators with reusable templates specific to each lifecycle stage. (4) SciEvolve, which turns feedback from users, experiments, reviews, and the environment into versioned updates of the memory schemas, skills, and templates, enabling continual improvement.
AI.result: AutoSci is implemented as a persistent research environment capable of executing end-to-end research workflows, maintaining structured memories across projects, and updating its procedures over time. The abstract emphasizes the architectural contributions and availability of the code (GitHub) rather than quantitative benchmarks, implying that the primary result is a working, open-source system that unifies memory, workflow, skill orchestration, and self-improvement for scientific research.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that AutoSci fills the gap for a unified automated scientific research system by providing a memory-centric, agentic environment that spans the full research lifecycle, preserves structured knowledge across projects, and evolves its own workflows and capabilities via feedback. The system architecture—SciMem, SciFlow, SciDAG, and SciEvolve—collectively enables LLM-based agents to more reliably assist with or automate complex scientific research processes, and the open-source release invites further development and adoption.

title: Eywa: Provenance-Grounded Long-Term Memory for AI Agents
comment: 29 pages, 3 figures, 16 tables. Benchmark artifacts available at https://eywa.to/research
summary: AI agents that persist across sessions need memory they can retrieve, audit, update, and erase. Existing memory systems often collapse source evidence, extracted facts, retrieved context, and answer policy into one opaque prompt path, making failures difficult to diagnose: a wrong answer may come from missing evidence, unsupported extraction, stale state, retrieval loss, or answer-model behavior. We present Eywa, a provenance-grounded memory architecture built around evidence before belief. Eywa stores immutable source evidence before deriving canonical facts, validates extracted memories against typed signals and source support, and retrieves bounded memory context through a deterministic multi-route read path with zero LLM calls inside retrieval. Retrieved context is returned separately from answer instructions, allowing the same memory substrate to be evaluated across frontier, budget, and local answer models. Under a frozen, artifact-recorded retrieval configuration, Eywa reaches 90.19% judge accuracy on the LoCoMo C1-C4 split with Claude Sonnet 4.6 write and QA roles. On LongMemEval-S, it reaches 88.2% retrieval-sufficiency accuracy. On BEAM, a 700-question technical-memory stress benchmark, it reaches 81.45% mean nugget score and 85.29% pass@score >= 0.5. Full per-question artifacts, including questions, gold answers, model answers, retrieved context, and labels, are published at https://eywa.to/research.

AI.tldr: Eywa is a provenance-grounded memory architecture for AI agents that separates evidence, extracted facts, retrieval, and answer generation to enable transparent, auditable long-term memory with strong benchmark performance.
AI.motivation: Persistent AI agents need reliable, editable, and debuggable memory. Existing systems entangle raw evidence, derived facts, retrieval mechanisms, and answer policies in a single opaque prompt, making it hard to diagnose whether errors stem from missing or stale evidence, incorrect extraction, retrieval issues, or the answer model itself. The authors aim to design a memory system that is transparent, provenance-aware, and easily evaluable across different models and configurations.
AI.method: Eywa organizes memory around immutable source evidence. It first stores raw evidence, then derives canonical facts (memories) from it, enforcing validation against typed signals and explicit source support. The architecture separates memory retrieval from answer generation via a deterministic, multi-route read path that uses no LLM calls during retrieval. Retrieved context is output independently from answer instructions so that different answer models (frontier, budget, or local) can be plugged in without changing the memory substrate. The system also logs full artifacts (questions, answers, retrieved context, labels) for reproducibility and auditing.
AI.result: With a fixed, fully recorded retrieval setup, Eywa attains 90.19% judge accuracy on LoCoMo C1–C4 using Claude Sonnet 4.6 for writing and QA. On LongMemEval-S, it achieves 88.2% retrieval-sufficiency accuracy. On BEAM, a 700-question technical memory benchmark, it reaches an 81.45% mean nugget score and 85.29% pass rate at score ≥ 0.5. The authors release per-question artifacts to support analysis and benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: Eywa demonstrates that a provenance-grounded, evidence-first memory architecture can deliver strong long-term memory performance while remaining transparent, auditable, and model-agnostic. By cleanly separating evidence storage, fact extraction, retrieval, and answer generation—and eliminating LLM calls from the retrieval path—it becomes easier to diagnose failures, swap answer models, and rigorously evaluate memory behavior using shared artifacts and benchmarks.

title: LinTree: Improving LLM Reasoning with Explicitly Structured Search Histories
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often solve reasoning problems by generating intermediate traces that explore and revise partial solutions. From a search perspective, these traces can be viewed as linearized search trees, where the model extends a partial solution, abandons it when it fails, and backtracks to try alternatives. Compared with traditional heuristic-guided search, such a policy has a potential advantage: it conditions on the whole search trace rather than only on the current local state. We first test whether LLMs utilize this advantage by comparing trace-conditioned reasoning policies against best-first search equipped with an LLM heuristic that only observes the current local state. Across three controlled reasoning environments, Blocks World, grid Navigation, and Sokoban, we find that raw access to search history alone is not enough to reliably outperform heuristic search. We then study one possible reason: in LLM reasoning traces, the underlying search tree is only implicitly represented, and when the model backtracks or switches branches, the trace does not explicitly identify which earlier search state is being revisited. We show that adding simple parent pointers to explicitly represent the linearized tree (LinTree) structure improves both task performance and search efficiency relative to implicit reasoning models and LLM-heuristic-guided search. These results suggest that search history becomes most useful when its tree structure is made explicit, motivating more structure-aware representations for LLM reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how large language models (LLMs) use search history when solving reasoning tasks and finds that making the underlying search tree structure explicit with simple parent pointers (LinTree) significantly improves performance and efficiency over both standard LLM traces and heuristic-guided search.
AI.motivation: LLMs often solve reasoning and planning problems by producing chain-of-thought style traces that implicitly correspond to a search process over partial solutions. Traditional search algorithms (e.g., best-first search) use heuristics that only look at the current state, while LLMs can in principle condition on the entire search history encoded in the trace. The authors want to know: (1) Do LLMs actually exploit this richer history in a way that beats standard heuristic search? (2) If not, what limits their effectiveness? (3) Can we design a representation that better exposes the search-tree structure so that LLMs can use search history more effectively?
AI.method: The authors cast LLM reasoning as search over a tree of partial solutions where the usual chain-of-thought is a linearized traversal of this tree. They compare: (a) a trace-conditioned LLM policy that sees the full textual search history; and (b) a best-first search that uses an LLM only as a heuristic function on the current state. Experiments are performed in three controlled reasoning domains: Blocks World, grid Navigation, and Sokoban. They then propose an explicit linearized tree representation called LinTree, which augments traces with simple parent pointers indicating which prior state a new step extends or revisits. They evaluate how LinTree affects success rates and search efficiency versus the baseline implicit traces and heuristic search.
AI.result: Across all three environments, merely giving the LLM raw access to full search history in natural-language traces does not reliably outperform best-first search driven by an LLM heuristic that only observes the current state. However, when the traces are augmented with explicit parent pointers (LinTree) that clarify the underlying tree structure and which state is being revisited on backtracking, both task success and search efficiency improve notably. LinTree-based models outperform both implicit-trace policies and LLM-heuristic-guided best-first search.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that the main benefit of search history for LLM-based reasoning arises when the tree structure of that history is made explicit. Implicit chain-of-thought traces underutilize history because they do not clearly identify branches or backtracking targets. By adding minimal structural annotations in the form of parent pointers, LLMs can better navigate and exploit search trees, achieving higher success and more efficient search. This supports the broader idea that future LLM reasoning systems should use structure-aware representations of search rather than relying solely on unstructured natural-language traces.

title: XLGoBench: Detecting cross-lingual skill gaps with algorithmic tasks
summary: We introduce a set of synthetic algorithmic tasks to detect cross-lingual gaps in the abilities of large language models. Our benchmark is commensurate across languages, since it requires models to perform the same underlying task in different languages; scalable, since each task can be generated at varying levels of complexity allowing it to be adapted to models with different capabilities; quantifiable, since every task admits an objective notion of correctness; and transparent, since tasks are generated from simple templates that can be readily audited for translation errors. Because our benchmark focuses on algorithmic tasks, differential performance is a sufficient -- but not necessary -- indicator of cross-lingual gaps. Nevertheless, we show through extensive experiments that our benchmark exposes persistent cross-lingual gaps in multiple state-of-the-art models.

AI.tldr: They build a synthetic, algorithmic, and fully auto-gradable benchmark to reveal cross-lingual performance gaps in large language models (LLMs).
AI.motivation: Existing cross-lingual evaluations often mix linguistic understanding with world knowledge, lack strict comparability across languages, are hard to scale, and can be noisy due to translation or annotation errors. The authors want a clean, controlled way to test whether LLMs truly perform equally well across languages, using tasks where difficulty and correctness can be precisely defined.
AI.method: They design a suite of algorithmic tasks (e.g., synthetic reasoning or computation problems) that can be instantiated in many natural languages using simple, auditable templates. Each task is (1) commensurate across languages because it is the same underlying algorithmic problem expressed in different languages; (2) scalable by varying complexity parameters; (3) quantifiable since each instance has an objective correct answer; and (4) transparent because all prompts derive from simple templates that can be checked for translation errors. They then run multiple state-of-the-art LLMs on these tasks across languages and compare performance.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, the benchmark reveals consistent performance differences for the same models across different languages, i.e., models perform better in some languages than others on the exact same underlying algorithmic tasks.
AI.conclusion: Algorithmic, template-based synthetic tasks offer a clean, scalable, and auditable way to measure cross-lingual gaps in LLMs. Persistent gaps found in state-of-the-art models indicate that equal performance across languages is far from solved, and the proposed benchmark can serve as a diagnostic tool for future model development and evaluation.

title: Choosing the Lens: Strategic Perspective Activation in Context-Dependent Argumentation
comment: Accepted to LAMAS&SR workshop at FLoC 2026
summary: The same arguments often need to be evaluated under different external regimes. An agent with influence over the regime has a strategic lever that standard formalisms do not directly capture. We introduce context-dependent argumentation frameworks (CDAFs), an extension of Dung's theory in which a defeat function determines, per context, which attacks succeed. A perspective-labeled specialisation derives the defeat function from a relevance set $ρ$ and a priority $π$. The relevance set is the agent's action space. In a small worked example, the agent's target argument is rejected under every full-relevance injective priority, yet accepted under partial activations, one of which no VAF audience can mirror. We define the corresponding decision problem, ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION, and record baseline complexity bounds. Tight bounds and multi-agent variants are left open.

AI.tldr: Introduces context-dependent argumentation frameworks (CDAFs) where which attacks succeed depends on the external context, enabling analysis of strategic manipulation by agents who can influence context.
AI.motivation: Standard abstract argumentation frameworks (Dung’s theory and its extensions) assume a fixed attack relation and do not directly model situations where the same argumentation structure must be evaluated under multiple external regimes (contexts) that can be strategically influenced by an agent. The authors want to capture how an agent can manipulate which attacks are active or successful by choosing or influencing the context, something that is not well-represented in existing value-based or priority-based formalisms.
AI.method: They define context-dependent argumentation frameworks (CDAFs), extending Dung’s framework by adding a context-sensitive defeat function that specifies, for each context, which attacks actually succeed. They then propose a perspective-labeled specialization, where the defeat function is derived from (i) a relevance set ρ representing the agent’s possible actions or context activations, and (ii) a priority ordering π over elements. They illustrate the framework with a worked example where an agent tries to get a target argument accepted, comparing full-relevance injective priorities with partial activations. They then formally define a decision problem called ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION associated with the agent’s ability to achieve acceptance of the target argument via context activation.
AI.result: In the worked example, they show that the target argument cannot be accepted under any full-relevance injective priority, but it can be made accepted under certain partial activations, including one that cannot be mimicked by any audience in a value-based argumentation framework (VAF). They also establish baseline computational complexity bounds for the ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION decision problem, although these bounds are not yet tight.
AI.conclusion: CDAFs offer a more expressive way to model strategic control over which attacks succeed by making success context-dependent and tied to an agent’s action space. This captures manipulation phenomena that value-based frameworks cannot emulate. The work opens new questions on tight complexity characterizations of ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION and on extending the setting to multi-agent scenarios, which are left for future research.

title: Anchoring LLM Gender Bias to Human Baselines: A Cross-Lingual Audit
summary: We audit six large language models (LLMs) for gender stereotyping across English, Korean, Chinese, and Japanese. Three were developed primarily for English-language use (Claude, GPT, Gemini) and three for East Asian use (DeepSeek, Syn-Pro, HyperCLOVA X). We adopt the HEXACO-100 personality inventory and anchor each model against a cross-cultural human dataset spanning 48 countries to ask not whether LLMs are biased, but how far their gender attributions drift from the populations they are deployed among. Our findings show that their stereotyping spans a range roughly 2.5 times wider than the entire cross-country range found in humans, and the effect can compound across languages. One English-centric model, prompted in Korean, reached 5 times the local baseline, even when the prompt stated the candidate had already been hired, which often dampens human stereotyping. To characterize such behaviors without ranking them, we introduce a four-pattern framework -- concordance, suppression, reorganization, and amplification -- across 24 (model x language) cells. Item-level analysis reveals that translation does not just rescale stereotypes, but changes the attributes tied to it, hiding significant rearrangement under the surface while appearing well-calibrated. Our results ultimately suggest that no single debiasing pipeline is likely to address bias evenly across linguistic boundaries.

AI.tldr: The paper audits six large language models for gender stereotyping across four languages and compares them to human cross-cultural baselines, finding that LLM stereotypes are often much more extreme and vary unpredictably with language and translation.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed globally and interact with users in many languages, there is a need to understand not just whether they are biased, but how their gender stereotyping compares to real human populations across cultures and languages. Existing work often focuses on English-only settings and does not quantify bias relative to local human norms, nor how translation and multilingual usage can distort or compound stereotypes.
AI.method: The authors evaluate six LLMs—three primarily English-focused (Claude, GPT, Gemini) and three focused on East Asian languages (DeepSeek, Syn-Pro, HyperCLOVA X)—across English, Korean, Chinese, and Japanese. They use the HEXACO-100 personality inventory to elicit gender attributions from models and compare these attributions to a large human dataset from 48 countries. They analyze how far each model’s male–female differences deviate from local human baselines and categorize behaviors into four patterns (concordance, suppression, reorganization, amplification) across 24 model–language combinations, including item-level analysis of how specific traits shift under translation.
AI.result: LLMs’ gender stereotyping is substantially stronger and more variable than that observed among humans cross-culturally. The range of model gender differences is about 2.5 times wider than the entire range observed across human populations, and language can exacerbate these effects. One English-centric model, when prompted in Korean, exhibited gender stereotyping roughly five times stronger than the local human baseline, even under conditions (e.g., candidate already hired) that typically reduce stereotyping in humans. Item-level analysis indicates that translation doesn’t just scale stereotypes up or down; it alters which specific attributes are tied to gender, leading to hidden reorganization beneath seemingly well-calibrated averages.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLM gender stereotyping is not only stronger than human baselines but also structurally different and highly sensitive to language and translation. Because the manifestation of bias shifts qualitatively across languages—sometimes amplifying, suppressing, or reorganizing stereotypes—there is unlikely to be a single, uniform debiasing pipeline that works consistently across linguistic contexts. Responsible deployment will require language- and culture-aware auditing and mitigation strategies rather than one-size-fits-all solutions.

title: Incremental BPE Tokenization
summary: We propose a novel algorithm for incremental Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) tokenization. The algorithm processes each input byte in worst-case $\mathcal{O}(\log^2 t)$ time, leading to an overall complexity of $\mathcal{O}(n \log^2 t)$, where $n$ is the input length and $t$ is the maximum token length. The algorithm incrementally maintains BPE tokenization results for every prefix of the input text, implementing the standard BPE merge procedure defined by a fixed set of merge rules. This enables efficient partial tokenization in streaming settings. Functioning as a drop-in replacement for standard BPE, our approach achieves a speedup of up to ${\sim}3\times$ over Hugging Face's tokenizers, and demonstrates significant latency reductions over OpenAI's tiktoken on pathological inputs. We further introduce an eager output algorithm that enables streaming output, emitting tokens as soon as token boundaries are determined during incremental tokenization. Overall, our results demonstrate that BPE tokenization can be performed incrementally with strong worst-case guarantees, while providing practical latency benefits in modern large language model pipelines. Code: https://github.com/ModelTC/mtc-inc-bpe

AI.tldr: The paper presents an incremental BPE tokenization algorithm with strong worst-case time guarantees and practical speedups over existing tokenizers, supporting streaming and eager token output.
AI.motivation: Standard BPE tokenizers recompute tokenization for an entire string, which is inefficient for streaming or interactive settings and can suffer from poor worst-case behavior and latency. The authors want a method that incrementally maintains BPE tokenization for prefixes, with provable complexity bounds and practical performance benefits, fitting modern LLM pipelines.
AI.method: They design an incremental algorithm that maintains the BPE merge structure as new bytes arrive. For each incoming byte, the algorithm updates the tokenization in worst-case O(log^2 t) time, where t is maximum token length, leading to O(n log^2 t) overall time for input length n. The algorithm adheres to standard BPE merge rules so it can serve as a drop-in replacement. They also propose an eager output variant that emits tokens as soon as their boundaries become fixed during the incremental process, enabling streaming output.
AI.result: The implementation shows up to ~3x speedup compared to Hugging Face tokenizers and significantly lower latency than OpenAI's tiktoken on pathological inputs. The algorithm successfully maintains correct BPE tokenizations incrementally with the stated asymptotic bounds, and the eager output variant provides earlier token availability in streaming usage.
AI.conclusion: Incremental BPE tokenization with strong worst-case complexity is feasible and practically beneficial. Their algorithm can replace standard BPE tokenizers in LLM pipelines, particularly in streaming scenarios, offering both theoretical guarantees and lower latency, especially when using the eager output mechanism.

title: Beyond Agreement: Scoring Panel-Surfaced Biomedical Entity Candidates for Curator Triage
summary: Biomedical NER is deceptively simple for modern LLMs: plausible biomedical mentions are easy to surface, but corpus-convention correctness depends on annotation conventions, span boundaries, entity granularity, and type schemas. Multi-LLM agreement is a salience signal, not corpus-convention correctness. We introduce a candidate-level panel-output benchmark for panel-surfaced candidate verification, where the unit is an aligned candidate surfaced by an explicitly defined multi-model panel rather than a standalone extractor output. The benchmark aligns eight LLMs' predictions over five public biomedical NER datasets into a candidate master table. BioConCal is an in-domain supervised scorer that instantiates this layer with inference-time gold-free agreement, mention, surface-availability, and document features for a fixed candidate stream. In domain, BioConCal improves AUROC from 0.753 for raw agreement to 0.910. At a validation-selected 0.95 precision target it selects 1,340 candidates at empirical test precision 0.939, compared with 293 for raw agreement. This corresponds to candidate-level recall 0.592 and corpus-level recall 0.523 against a within-panel row-label ceiling of 0.883. The main benefit is not recovering entities missed by every panel member, but reshaping a noisy panel stream into a higher-yield review queue. Under entity-type shift, thresholds require target-domain validation, and exact character localization remains a separate deterministic post-processing step.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BioConCal, a supervised scoring model that filters and prioritizes candidate biomedical entities surfaced by multiple LLMs, turning noisy multi-model agreement into a high-yield review queue.
AI.motivation: Biomedical named entity recognition with large language models appears easy because models readily propose plausible entities, but correctness per corpus conventions is subtle: it depends on annotation guidelines, exact span boundaries, entity granularity, and type schemas. Simple multi-model agreement only signals that an entity is salient to LLMs, not that it matches the gold corpus. There is a need for a principled way to verify and rank entity candidates produced by a panel of LLMs, leveraging their outputs without requiring gold labels at inference time.
AI.method: The authors construct a candidate-level benchmark where the basic unit is an aligned entity candidate coming from a fixed panel of eight LLMs applied to five public biomedical NER datasets. They align all panel predictions into a master candidate table and define a verification problem over this fixed candidate stream. They then propose BioConCal, an in-domain supervised scoring model that takes, as features, panel agreement signals, mention-specific information, surface-form availability, and document-level features, all computed without needing gold labels at inference time. BioConCal outputs a score used to filter and prioritize candidates, with thresholds selected via validation to trade precision and recall. Exact character-level localization is handled separately via deterministic post-processing.
AI.result: On the benchmark, raw multi-LLM agreement achieves an AUROC of 0.753 for distinguishing correct from incorrect candidates. BioConCal improves this AUROC to 0.910. When tuning for high precision, a validation-selected threshold targeting 0.95 precision leads BioConCal to select 1,340 candidate entities with an empirical test precision of 0.939, compared to only 293 candidates selected by raw agreement at a similar precision. This yields candidate-level recall of 0.592 and corpus-level recall of 0.523, under a within-panel row-label upper bound recall of 0.883, demonstrating substantially better yield from the same candidate stream.
AI.conclusion: The work shows that instead of relying on naive multi-LLM agreement for biomedical NER, a supervised candidate-level scorer like BioConCal can substantially improve the precision–recall trade-off when ranking panel-generated entity candidates. The main gain is not in discovering entirely new entities absent from all panel members, but in reshaping the noisy panel output into a more efficient, higher-yield review queue for downstream use. However, under shifts in entity-type distributions, thresholds must be revalidated in the target domain, and accurate character-level span localization still needs separate deterministic post-processing.

title: Your Teacher Can't Help You Here: Combating Supervision Fidelity Decay in On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation transfers reasoning capabilities by training a student model on its own generated trajectories using token-level feedback from a teacher. However, we identify a critical bottleneck, \textbf{Supervision Fidelity Decay (SFD)}: as student-generated prefixes lengthen, the teacher's next-token distribution becomes less confident and less discriminative. Consequently, the teacher-dependent corrective signal in reverse-KL distillation weakens, causing student drift to compound across long reasoning chains. To mitigate SFD, we introduce \textbf{Lookahead Group Reward (\ours{})}. Building on the insight that next-step teacher confidence reflects the discriminative strength of future reverse-KL supervision, \ours{} evaluates the student's top-K candidate tokens by the teacher confidence they induce at the subsequent step and assigns a group-normalized reward. To maintain computational efficiency, we further design an entropy-triggered tree-attention mechanism. Across six math and code benchmarks, \ours{} improves mean@8 by \textbf{2.57} points over OPD for a 7B student, with gains increasing in longer-generation and reaching +\textbf{4.92} points on AIME-26 at 39k tokens.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a key failure mode in on-policy distillation for reasoning models—Supervision Fidelity Decay (SFD)—and proposes Lookahead Group Reward to retain strong teacher guidance on long reasoning chains, yielding substantial gains on math and code benchmarks.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation (training a student on its own sampled trajectories with teacher feedback) is attractive for transferring reasoning ability, but it suffers on long reasoning chains. As the student’s sampled prefix deviates from the teacher’s trajectories, the teacher’s next-token distribution becomes less confident and less discriminative, weakening the reverse-KL corrective signal. This Supervision Fidelity Decay leads to compounding student drift and degraded reasoning performance, especially on long generations, which many benchmarks require. The paper aims to understand and directly address this bottleneck so that smaller student models can inherit strong reasoning capabilities from larger teachers more reliably and efficiently.
AI.method: The authors first empirically identify and characterize Supervision Fidelity Decay (SFD): for longer, student-generated prefixes, the teacher’s next-token distribution becomes higher-entropy and less peaked, reducing the strength of reverse-KL supervision. To mitigate this, they propose Lookahead Group Reward. For each decoding step, the student proposes a top-K set of candidate tokens. For each candidate, they perform a one-step lookahead by hypothetically appending the token to the prefix and querying the teacher’s next-token distribution at the subsequent step. They then compute a measure of teacher confidence at that next step (e.g., entropy or concentration of the distribution) as a proxy for how discriminative the future reverse-KL signal would be. Candidate tokens are given a group-normalized reward based on the induced teacher confidence, which is used to train the student (e.g., in a policy-gradient or reward-weighted regression style). To control computational overhead from many teacher queries, they introduce an entropy-triggered tree-attention mechanism that selectively expands the lookahead only when the teacher’s distribution is sufficiently uncertain, reducing the number of expensive teacher evaluations while preserving the benefits of lookahead rewards.
AI.result: Using six math and code reasoning benchmarks and a 7B-parameter student model, their Lookahead Group Reward method consistently outperforms standard on-policy distillation (OPD). On mean@8 across all benchmarks, they report an average improvement of 2.57 percentage points relative to OPD. The gains are larger for tasks and settings requiring longer generations; on AIME-26 with up to 39k-token outputs, the method achieves a +4.92 point improvement in mean@8 over OPD. These results indicate that mitigating Supervision Fidelity Decay via lookahead-based rewards significantly improves the transfer of long-chain reasoning ability from teacher to student models.
AI.conclusion: Supervision Fidelity Decay is a core limitation of existing on-policy distillation approaches for reasoning models, because teacher guidance becomes weak and uninformative as student-generated prefixes diverge from the teacher’s typical behavior. The proposed Lookahead Group Reward addresses this by evaluating candidate next tokens according to the teacher’s future confidence and rewarding those that preserve strong, discriminative supervision, while an entropy-triggered tree-attention scheme keeps the method computationally feasible. Empirical results across multiple math and code benchmarks demonstrate that this approach significantly enhances student reasoning performance, particularly on long-context problems, suggesting that controlling supervision quality along the trajectory is crucial for effective reasoning distillation.

title: Fine-Tuning Improves Information Conveyance in Language Models
summary: Fine-tuning is often believed to reduce uncertainty and diversity in large language models, but existing analyses overlook output length, a key confounder, and therefore fail to capture how uncertainty is distributed across an entire generation rollout. To address this, we propose Canopy Entropy ($\mathrm{CE}^\star$), a measure that views language generation from a tree perspective, where ``canopy'' represents the space of all possible rollouts, making $\mathrm{CE}^\star$ naturally quantify the effective size of the generation space. $\mathrm{CE}^\star$ jointly captures uncertainty in both the output length $N$ and the generated sequence $Y_{1:N}$ -- indeed, we show that it equals to total Shannon entropy $H(N, Y_{1:N}\mid X)$, where $X$ denotes the prompt. This formulation yields interpretable metrics, including a length-entropy correlation term $ρ(N, r_N)$, where $r_N$ is the entropy rate, quantifying information conveyance efficiency by indicating whether longer outputs are more or less informative per token. Empirically, across tasks and model families, we find that fine-tuned models consistently exhibit stronger positive correlation $ρ(N, r_N)$, even when total entropy decreases. Furthermore, after controlling for model family, task, prompt, and output-length effects, we find that fine-tuning nearly triples the correlation strength between entropy rate and semantic diversity, suggesting that aligned models convert token uncertainty into semantic diversity more efficiently. Overall, these results demonstrate that fine-tuning does not simply reduce uncertainty, but fundamentally reorganizes it into more informative and semantically meaningful generations. Our code is available at https://github.com/WeiyiTian/canopy-entropy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Canopy Entropy, a tree-based measure of total uncertainty in language model outputs that jointly accounts for output length and content, and uses it to show that fine-tuning redistributes rather than just reduces uncertainty, making it more semantically informative.
AI.motivation: Existing analyses of how fine-tuning affects language model uncertainty usually ignore output length. Because output length significantly affects entropy measurements, these analyses miss how uncertainty is distributed across the entire generation process. The authors want a principled, interpretable way to quantify the effective size of the generation space, including both how long outputs are and what they say, and then use this to understand how fine-tuning changes model behavior.
AI.method: They conceptualize language generation as a tree of possible rollouts and define Canopy Entropy (CE*), which measures the total Shannon entropy of both output length N and token sequence Y_{1:N} given a prompt X, i.e., H(N, Y_{1:N} | X). From this formulation, they derive interpretable components, including a correlation term ρ(N, r_N) between length and entropy rate r_N, indicating whether longer outputs carry more or less information per token. They then empirically estimate CE* and related metrics across multiple model families, tasks, and prompts, comparing base and fine-tuned models while controlling for model family, task, prompt, and length effects. They also analyze the relationship between entropy rate and semantic diversity in generations.
AI.result: They find that fine-tuned models consistently show a stronger positive correlation between output length and entropy rate ρ(N, r_N), even when overall entropy decreases. After controlling for confounding factors (model family, task, prompt, length), fine-tuning almost triples the correlation between entropy rate and semantic diversity. This indicates that in aligned models, token-level uncertainty is more efficiently converted into variation in meaning rather than just surface-level randomness or verbosity.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning does not merely reduce language model uncertainty; it reorganizes it. Canopy Entropy shows that aligned models concentrate uncertainty into more informative, semantically diverse generations: longer outputs tend to be more information-dense per token, and semantic diversity is more closely tied to entropy rate. CE* thus offers a principled, length-aware framework to quantify and interpret how fine-tuning reshapes the generation space and the informational structure of model outputs.

title: Speculative Pipeline Decoding: Higher-Accruacy and Zero-Bubble Speculation via Pipeline Parallelism
summary: Speculative Decoding (SD) accelerates low-concurrency LLM inference by employing a draft-then-verify paradigm. However, mainstream methods typically rely on multi-token prediction, which introduces escalating prediction difficulty and serial drafting latency. To address these, we propose Speculative Pipeline Decoding (SPD), a groundbreaking framework that unlocks the true potential of pipeline parallelism. By partitioning the target LLM into $n$ pipeline stages, SPD allows LLM to process $n$ tokens in parallel to accelerate decoding. To continuous fill the pipeline in single sequence decoding, a speculation module aggregates intermediate features across different pipeline depths to predict the next token, executing strictly in parallel with the target model's pipeline step, to realize bounded difficulty, higher acceptance rates, and zero latency bubbles. Our experiments demonstrate that SPD achieves a significantly higher theoretical speedup compared to mainstream baselines, offering a highly scalable solution for LLM decoding acceleration. Our code is available at https://github.com/yuyijiong/speculative_pipeline_decoding

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Speculative Pipeline Decoding (SPD), a new speculative decoding framework that fully exploits pipeline parallelism to accelerate LLM inference while avoiding increased prediction difficulty and latency bubbles.
AI.motivation: Existing speculative decoding methods for LLMs speed up inference but mainly rely on multi-token prediction from draft models, which makes prediction increasingly hard as more tokens are predicted at once and introduces serial drafting latency. Moreover, they do not fully utilize pipeline parallelism in low-concurrency settings where a single or few sequences are decoded. The authors are motivated to design a method that (1) scales better with model size and depth, (2) keeps token prediction difficulty bounded, (3) achieves high acceptance rates, and (4) removes idle bubbles in pipeline-parallel execution for single-sequence decoding.
AI.method: They introduce Speculative Pipeline Decoding (SPD), which partitions the target LLM into n pipeline stages so that n tokens can be processed in parallel. Instead of a separate draft model producing long token segments, a speculation module is attached that aggregates intermediate features from different pipeline depths to predict only the next token. This module runs strictly in parallel with the main model’s pipeline step so that the pipeline stays continuously filled. The design enforces bounded per-step prediction difficulty, aims for higher acceptance in speculative verification, and eliminates pipeline bubbles. SPD is presented as a general framework that can be integrated with pipeline-parallel LLM inference.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation shows that SPD yields a substantially higher theoretical speedup compared with mainstream speculative decoding baselines. The results suggest improved scalability with respect to pipeline depth and better utilization of hardware for low-concurrency LLM decoding, while maintaining high acceptance rates. Implementation details and code are released in a public GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: SPD unlocks the performance potential of pipeline parallelism for speculative decoding by enabling parallel processing of multiple tokens across pipeline stages while keeping prediction difficulty bounded. By coupling a feature-aggregating speculation module with pipeline-parallel execution, SPD eliminates latency bubbles and delivers higher theoretical speedups than existing methods. The framework offers a scalable, practical approach for accelerating LLM decoding in low-concurrency scenarios.

title: MADS: Model-Aware Diverse Core Set Selection for Instruction Tuning
summary: Instruction fine-tuning is employed to enhance the instruction-following ability of large language models (LLMs). As the amount of instruction fine-tuning data increases, selecting the optimal core set becomes particularly important. However, ensuring the diversity of the core set remains a significant challenge. Existing methods predominantly distinguish different training data based on the text features themselves, decoupled from LLMs' own understanding and representation of the data. To address this issue, we propose a Model-Aware Diverse Core Set Selection method, which distinguishes data features based on the neural activation states during LLM inference. This approach serves as an efficient instantiation of coverage-based selection using model-intrinsic activation features to ensure the diversity in the core set. We extensively evaluate our method on six benchmarks that cover five distinct tasks. In our method, the core set selected by the 3B-parameter LLM performs effectively when utilized to fine-tune larger models with 7B, 8B, and 13B parameters. Experimental results on the Alpaca-GPT4 dataset, which comprises 52K instruction-response pairs, show that the core set, sized at 15\% of the original dataset and selected by Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, achieves an average improvement of 2.5\% when fine-tuning four larger base models compared with training on the full dataset. The experimental results demonstrate that our method enhances model performance on multiple downstream tasks while reducing data requirements.

AI.tldr: They propose a model-aware way to pick a small but diverse subset of instruction-tuning data using internal activation patterns of an LLM instead of just text features, and show it can outperform using the full dataset while using only 15% of the data.
AI.motivation: Instruction fine-tuning quality strongly depends on which training examples are chosen, especially when scaling up data. Existing core-set selection methods rely mostly on surface text similarity or static features and ignore how the LLM internally represents different instructions, which can limit diversity and coverage of behaviors. The authors want a selection method that is aligned with the model’s own feature space, improving diversity and efficiency of instruction-tuning data, and enabling small models to select data that works well for larger models.
AI.method: They introduce a Model-Aware Diverse Core Set Selection method. During LLM inference on candidate instruction-response pairs, they record neural activation states (internal representations). These activation vectors are then used as model-intrinsic features for coverage-based core-set selection, aiming to pick a subset of examples that maximally covers the activation space (i.e., is diverse with respect to the model’s internal understanding), rather than being chosen purely from text-level features. A 3B-parameter LLM (e.g., Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) is used to compute activations and select the core set, which is then used to fine-tune larger models.
AI.result: On six benchmarks spanning five tasks, core sets chosen by the 3B model transfer effectively to larger models of 7B, 8B, and 13B parameters. On the Alpaca-GPT4 instruction dataset (52K pairs), using only a 15% core set selected via their method to fine-tune four larger base models yields, on average, a 2.5% performance improvement over fine-tuning on the full dataset. This shows that their activation-based, coverage-oriented selection can both reduce data and improve downstream performance.
AI.conclusion: Model-aware core-set selection using neural activation features can better capture diversity and task coverage than text-only selection methods. A small model can effectively select a compact, diverse training subset that generalizes well to larger models, enabling improved instruction-following performance on multiple downstream tasks while reducing the size of the required fine-tuning data.

title: dMoE: dLLMs with Learnable Block Experts
comment: Working in progress. Code is available at: \url{https://github.com/fscdc/dMoE}
summary: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive models, offering competitive performance while naturally supporting parallel decoding. However, as dLLMs are increasingly integrated with Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures to scale model capacity, a fundamental mismatch arises between block parallel decoding and token-level expert selection. Specifically, each dLLM forward pass processes multiple tokens with bidirectional dependencies, whereas conventional MoE layers route each token independently. This mismatch substantially increases the number of uniquely activated experts, making inference increasingly memory-bound. To address this, we propose dMoE, a simple yet effective block-level MoE framework. The central idea of dMoE is to aggregate token-level expert distributions within each block into a unified block-level expert distribution, which is then used to guide expert routing in a more coherent manner. In this way, dMoE substantially reduces the number of uniquely activated experts during inference without sacrificing performance, thereby mitigating the memory-bound bottleneck. Extensive experiments across a variety of benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of dMoE. On average, dMoE reduces the number of uniquely activated experts from 69.5 to 14.6 while retaining 99.11% of the original performance. Meanwhile, it reduces memory usage by 76.64% to 79.84% and achieves 1.14$\times$ to 1.66$\times$ end-to-end latency speedup. Code is available at: https://github.com/fscdc/dMoE

AI.tldr: The paper introduces dMoE, a block-level Mixture-of-Experts framework tailored for diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs), which significantly cuts expert activations and memory usage while keeping model performance nearly unchanged.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs support parallel (block) decoding and can rival autoregressive models, but when combined with standard token-level Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers, there is a mismatch: dLLM layers process blocks of tokens jointly, while MoE routes each token independently. This causes many distinct experts to be activated per block, making inference memory-bound and limiting scalability and speed. The authors want a routing mechanism that aligns with block-wise computation to reduce expert activations and memory without hurting accuracy.
AI.method: They propose dMoE, a block-level MoE routing scheme for dLLMs. Instead of choosing experts independently for each token, dMoE first aggregates all tokens' expert probability distributions within a diffusion block to form a single block-level distribution over experts. This unified distribution is then used to decide which experts to activate for the entire block. In practice, this means coherent expert routing across tokens in the block, greatly reducing the count of unique experts loaded/used during a forward pass, while leveraging the shared structure captured by the diffusion block.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks, dMoE substantially lowers the number of uniquely activated experts during inference—from an average of 69.5 to 14.6—while preserving 99.11% of the original model performance. This routing change yields large memory savings (about 76.64% to 79.84% reduction) and improves end-to-end latency by 1.14× to 1.66×, demonstrating practical efficiency gains with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: Aligning MoE routing with the block-level computation pattern of diffusion LLMs effectively addresses the memory-bound nature of expert activation. dMoE’s simple block-level aggregation strategy reduces expert diversity per block, significantly cutting memory and speeding up inference, while maintaining nearly all the original performance. This suggests that structurally consistent routing (block-aware rather than token-wise) is a key design principle for scalable, efficient MoE integration in diffusion-based language models.

title: PatchWorld: Gradient-Free Optimization of Executable World Models
summary: Text-agent environments are typically modeled as partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs), assuming that the simulator's latent state and transition dynamics are hidden from the agent. Yet little work has examined whether executable code can be induced to serve as a world model for prediction and planning under partial observability. We introduce PatchWorld, a gradient-free framework that turns offline trajectories into executable Python world models through counterexample-guided code repair. Instead of predicting the next observation with a black-box model, PatchWorld induces symbolic belief-state programs whose action updates can be inspected, replayed, and locally patched. Across seven AgentGym environments, PatchWorld-Simple achieves the highest code-based planning score among evaluated methods, reaching 76.4\% macro success in live one-step lookahead while invoking no LLM calls inside the world-model prediction module itself. We further find that a human-specified residual-memory bias improves surface observation fidelity but weakens decision utility. This exposes a tradeoff in executable world models, since improving observation fidelity can come at the expense of action-discriminative dynamics, and vice versa. Code is available at https://github.com/HKBU-KnowComp/PatchWorld.

AI.tldr: PatchWorld learns executable Python world-models from offline text-agent trajectories using gradient-free, counterexample-guided code repair for planning under partial observability.
AI.motivation: Most text-agent environments are modeled as POMDPs and use black-box predictors for next-observation modeling, which hides the dynamics and makes them hard to inspect, edit, and reason about, especially under partial observability. The authors want to see whether explicit executable code can be induced as a transparent world model that supports prediction and planning while being interpretable and patchable.
AI.method: They introduce PatchWorld, which converts offline trajectories from text-agent environments into symbolic belief-state programs written in Python. Using a gradient-free, counterexample-guided code repair loop, the system repeatedly executes the current program, compares its predictions to logged trajectories, and patches the code to reduce mismatches. The resulting programs update belief states and implement action-conditioned dynamics, and can be inspected, replayed, and locally edited. They also explore adding a human-specified residual-memory bias to improve fidelity to surface observations.
AI.result: On seven AgentGym environments, the PatchWorld-Simple variant attains the best performance among code-based planning methods, achieving 76.4% macro success in live one-step lookahead planning without calling an LLM inside the world-model prediction component. When they add a residual-memory bias, the programs better match surface observations but degrade in decision-making quality, revealing a tradeoff in the learned dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Executable Python world models induced via counterexample-guided repair can support effective planning for text agents under partial observability, sometimes outperforming other code-based planners without requiring online LLM calls. However, efforts to maximize observation fidelity can reduce the action-discriminative structure of the dynamics, suggesting an inherent tradeoff between accurately mirroring observations and preserving decision-relevant abstractions in executable world models.

title: The Flip Side of RLHF: On-Policy Feedback for Reward Model Self-Supervised Improvement
