A simple linear probe trained on one language can exploit this shared subspace to provide effective, zero-shot confidence estimation in other languages, offering a strong, retraining-free baseline. This suggests that cross-lingual confidence estimation can be handled efficiently with lightweight probes instead of complex or language-specific CE methods.

title: GRKV: Global Regression for Training-Free KV Cache Compression in Long-Context LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) with extended context lengths rely on the key-value (KV) cache to support attention over prior tokens. However, maintaining the KV cache incurs substantial memory overhead, motivating KV-cache compression methods that enforce a fixed budget through eviction and merging. Modern eviction methods increasingly adopt span-based retention because preserving contiguous spans is empirically effective and better preserves semantic coherence. Yet, when combined with post-eviction merging, span-based retention concentrates merges onto a small set of span-boundary carrier tokens, producing a highly imbalanced merge pattern that exacerbates over-merging and increases information loss. To address this imbalance, we propose GRKV (Global Regression for KV Cache), a training-free KV-cache merging method that directly minimizes the discrepancy between compressed-cache and full-cache attention outputs. GRKV uses ridge-regression-based merge steps to distribute information from evicted tokens across retained tokens, while regularizing the updates to prevent over-smoothing. Across the LongBench and RULER long-context benchmarks, GRKV is the only merging method that improves overall performance with minimal overhead.

AI.tldr: Proposes GRKV, a training-free KV-cache merging method for long-context LLMs that reduces memory while improving performance by globally regressing compressed attention outputs toward full-cache outputs and avoiding over-merged span-boundary tokens.
AI.motivation: Extended-context LLMs need to store large key-value (KV) caches for attention over many prior tokens, which leads to high memory usage. Existing KV-cache compression methods with fixed budgets rely on eviction and merging. Span-based eviction preserves contiguous spans and semantic coherence, but when combined with merging it causes most merges to concentrate on span-boundary tokens, creating an imbalanced merge pattern that over-merges a few carrier tokens and increases information loss. The paper aims to reduce KV-cache memory without this degradation in attention quality.
AI.method: Introduce GRKV (Global Regression for KV Cache), a training-free KV-cache merging scheme. Instead of naïvely merging evicted tokens into nearest retained tokens or span boundaries, GRKV formulates merging as a ridge regression problem: choose updates to retained tokens such that the attention outputs from the compressed cache approximate those from the full cache, while using L2 regularization to limit the magnitude of updates and avoid over-smoothing. GRKV distributes information from evicted tokens across multiple retained tokens according to this regression solution, producing a more balanced merge pattern. It operates as a drop-in, low-overhead modification to standard KV-cache handling and works with span-based eviction policies.
AI.result: On long-context benchmarks LongBench and RULER, GRKV outperforms prior KV-cache merging methods. It is reported as the only merging method that yields overall performance improvements (relative to no merging or to other compression baselines) while adding minimal computational and memory overhead, demonstrating better retention of model accuracy under a fixed KV-cache budget.
AI.conclusion: By recasting KV-cache merging as a global ridge regression problem, GRKV mitigates the imbalance and over-merging introduced by span-based retention strategies. It more evenly distributes information from evicted tokens and closely matches the attention behavior of a full cache, enabling long-context LLMs to operate under tighter memory budgets without sacrificing—and in fact improving—performance on standard long-context benchmarks.

title: FBHM: Functional Benchmarking and Steering of VLMs for Hateful Meme Detection
summary: Hateful meme detection remains a formidable challenge for vision-language models, as existing benchmarks are structurally observational - confounding rhetorical hate mechanisms with target community features and preventing causal evaluation of model vulnerabilities. To address this, we introduce FBHM, a systematically curated benchmark of Functionality Based Hateful Memes constructed along two orthogonal axes: 25 distinct rhetorical functionalities and 10 target communities (5,000 memes total). Benchmarking state-of-the-art VLMs reveals a severe generalization gap: models highly accurate on standard datasets catastrophically drop to near-random performance on FBHM, proving they exploit dataset-specific heuristics rather than robust multimodal reasoning. To efficiently close this gap, we propose LSV (learnable steering vectors), an ultra-low data regime strategy that applies a causal intervention objective on as few as 500 steering samples (50 unique base memes), boosting FBHM performance by ~30 Macro-F1 points while outperforming in-context learning and PEFT without degrading source-domain performance.

AI.tldr: They build a new, causally-structured hateful meme benchmark that exposes vision-language models’ reliance on shortcuts, then introduce a lightweight steering-vector method that dramatically improves generalization with very little data.
AI.motivation: Existing hateful meme benchmarks mix up (confound) hate rhetoric with the identity of the targeted group, so models can perform well by memorizing superficial correlations instead of understanding how hate is expressed. This prevents causal analysis of where models fail and how to robustly improve them. The authors want a benchmark that disentangles rhetorical mechanisms from target communities and a method that can fix model vulnerabilities with minimal additional data.
AI.method:
1) Construct FBHM, a benchmark of 5,000 memes arranged along two orthogonal axes: 25 rhetorical functionalities (ways hate is expressed) and 10 different target communities. This systematic design allows controlled testing of whether models generalize across functionalities and targets.
2) Evaluate state-of-the-art vision-language models on FBHM versus standard hateful meme datasets to diagnose generalization behavior.
3) Propose Learnable Steering Vectors (LSV): in an ultra-low data regime, learn direction vectors in model representation space using a causal intervention objective, trained on as few as 500 labeled samples derived from 50 base memes. These vectors are then applied at inference to steer model predictions without full fine-tuning.
4) Compare LSV against in-context learning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) baselines, and assess its impact on both FBHM and the original source datasets.
AI.result: State-of-the-art vision-language models that are highly accurate on existing hateful meme datasets perform close to random guessing on FBHM, revealing a strong reliance on dataset-specific heuristics and poor causal generalization to new combinations of rhetoric and targets. Applying LSV with only 500 steering samples yields about a 30-point increase in Macro-F1 on FBHM, and this improvement surpasses that obtained by in-context learning and PEFT methods. Critically, the steering does not harm performance on the original source-domain benchmarks, indicating targeted robustness gains rather than overfitting.
AI.conclusion: FBHM exposes that current hateful meme detectors lack robust, causally grounded multimodal reasoning and instead exploit superficial correlations in existing benchmarks. A functionality- and target-structured benchmark is essential for diagnosing these weaknesses. Learnable Steering Vectors offer an efficient, low-data way to causally intervene in model behavior and significantly improve generalization to systematically varied hateful memes while preserving performance on prior datasets. This suggests that lightweight causal steering can be a practical path to safer, more reliable vision-language systems in sensitive applications like hate detection.

title: Target-Side Paraphrase Augmentation for Sign Language Translation with Large Language Models
comment: Accepted at GenSign (https://genai4sl.github.io/) at CVPR 2026. Non proceedings track
summary:
Sign language translation (SLT) remains constrained by limited paired sign-video/text corpora and heavy-tailed target vocabularies. We study target-side augmentation in which GPT-4o generates controlled paraphrase variants of reference sentences while the sign input remains unchanged. A Signformer-style pose-based Transformer is trained under a two-stage schedule: pre-training on the augmented corpus followed by fine-tuning on the original references.
  We evaluate on three datasets spanning complementary challenges: PHOENIX14T (German Sign Language), with moderate lexical diversity; GSL (Greek Sign Language), with highly ontrolled, repetitive recordings; and LSA-T (Argentinian Sign Language), with severe long-tail sparsity. On PHOENIX14T, augmentation improves BLEU-4 from 9.56 to 10.33. The near-saturated GSL baseline and extremely sparse LSA-T setting reveal the limits of the approach. To our knowledge, this is the first study to apply LLM-generated target-side araphrases and LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation to SLT. The semantic evaluation reveals gains in fidelity that lexical overlap metrics understate.

AI.tldr: The paper explores using GPT-4o to generate paraphrased target sentences for sign language translation training, improving performance modestly on one dataset and analyzing limits on others.
AI.motivation: Sign language translation suffers from scarce paired sign-video/text data and heavy-tailed vocabularies, making it hard for models to learn robust, generalizable mappings from signs to text. The authors seek a data-efficient way to enrich target-side linguistic variation without collecting new sign recordings.
AI.method: They perform target-side data augmentation: for each original text translation, GPT-4o generates controlled paraphrases while keeping the sign input fixed. A pose-based Transformer (Signformer-style) is then trained in two stages: first pre-trained on the expanded, paraphrased corpus, then fine-tuned on the original human references. Evaluation includes both traditional lexical metrics (e.g., BLEU-4) and LLM-as-a-Judge semantic evaluation.
AI.result: On the PHOENIX14T German Sign Language dataset, target-side augmentation increases BLEU-4 from 9.56 to 10.33, indicating modest but measurable gains. On GSL, where recordings are highly controlled and repetitive, the baseline is nearly saturated and augmentation yields limited improvement. On LSA-T, where the target vocabulary has extreme long-tail sparsity, augmentation also shows limited benefit, exposing the approach’s boundaries.
AI.conclusion: GPT-4o-generated target-side paraphrases can enhance sign language translation quality in settings with moderate lexical diversity but offer less benefit for highly controlled or extremely sparse datasets. The study is the first to apply both LLM-based paraphrase augmentation and LLM-as-a-Judge semantic evaluation to SLT, revealing that semantic gains may be underrepresented by surface lexical metrics like BLEU.

title: The Sword, Shield, and Achilles' Heel: Characterizing the Linguistic Inductive Bias of Large Language Models for Spatial Reasoning in Navigation Planning
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based navigation systems commonly construct explicit spatial representations (e.g., topological graphs, semantic raster maps) and translate them into textual descriptions as LLMs' inputs. However, the linguistic structures of such text-based spatial representations and the choices of contextual features (e.g., topology, geometry) they contain are often treated as neutral engineering decisions rather than key factors that shape LLMs' behavior. To fill the gap, we propose a dual-interventional framework that disentangles linguistic structures from different contextual cues to evaluate the linguistic inductive bias of LLMs for navigation planning. In the framework, representation intervention varies the linguistic format and the degree of linguistic compression, clarifying when linguistic representations support or inhibit navigation planning. Context intervention, combined with contextual feature combination and conflict probing, explicitly clarifies the preferences and weaknesses of LLMs when processing different contextual cues. Experiments across diverse spatial reasoning tasks and multiple model scales reveal a consistent pattern: topological information is a sturdy shield and the backbone of robust planning; linguistic format is a double-edged sword whose effect depends on model size, task demands, and the compression level; and semantic information is a fatal Achilles' heel -- incorrect semantic cues can systematically derail the planning process. Overall, our study shows that effective text-based spatial representations in LLM-based navigation should preserve topological integrity, calibrate representational compression to model capacity, and ensure semantic correctness, rather than simply adopting a single representation. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/jonesdong150/LLM-Navigation-Inductive-Bias.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how the linguistic form and spatial/contextual information in text-based maps affect LLMs’ navigation planning, proposing a dual-intervention framework to probe LLM inductive biases.
AI.motivation: Most LLM-based navigation systems convert spatial structures into text, but they treat the wording, format, and chosen spatial cues as neutral engineering details. The authors argue these are not neutral: they systematically shape how LLMs reason and plan. There is a lack of principled analysis of how different linguistic structures and combinations of topology, geometry, and semantics in text affect LLM performance and failure modes in navigation tasks.
AI.method: They design a dual-interventional framework with two main components: (1) Representation intervention, which manipulates the linguistic format (e.g., narrative vs. structured) and the level of linguistic compression of spatial descriptions to see when they help or hurt navigation planning. (2) Context intervention, which systematically varies and combines contextual cues (topology, geometry, semantics) and introduces conflicts between them to reveal LLMs’ preferences, biases, and weaknesses. They apply these interventions to a range of spatial reasoning tasks and LLM scales.
AI.result: Across tasks and model sizes, they find consistent patterns: topological information is the most reliable and forms the backbone of robust navigation planning; the effect of linguistic format is highly contingent on model size, task difficulty, and compression level, sometimes helping and sometimes harming; semantic cues are fragile—wrong semantic information can strongly and systematically mislead planning even when other cues are correct.
AI.conclusion: Effective textual spatial representations for LLM-based navigation should prioritize preserving topological structure, tune the level of linguistic compression to the model’s capacity and the task, and carefully maintain semantic accuracy. Designers should not rely on a single fixed text representation but instead treat representation choice as an integral, principled part of system design. The published code enables further study of these inductive biases.

title: Multilingual and Cross-Lingual Citation Needed Detection on Wikipedia for Lower-Resource Languages
summary: In automated fact-checking (AFC), check-worthiness detection identifies claims requiring verification based on domain-specific criteria. On Wikipedia, this task instantiates as Citation Needed Detection (CND), which flags claims lacking supporting citations. However, existing research has largely overlooked lower-resource languages, and recent AFC pipelines rely on large language models (LLMs), which are inaccessible to low-resource organizations. We introduce MCN, a multilingual CND corpus spanning 18 languages across three resource levels, on which we conduct an extensive study of small decoder-based language models (SLMs). Our experiments show that SLMs fine-tuned with an encoder-style objective substantially outperform prompted LLMs across languages. We further present one of the first studies on cross-lingual CND, demonstrating that SLMs fine-tuned solely on English claims surpass LLMs, even with little to no target-language adaptation. Our findings have important implications for lower-resource Wikipedia communities and suggest that compact, task-specific models are preferable to LLMs for CND. We release all data and code at https://github.com/gerritq/mcn

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MCN, a multilingual citation-needed detection corpus for 18 languages, and shows that small fine-tuned decoder models outperform prompted LLMs, especially in low-resource settings.
AI.motivation: Automated fact-checking relies on identifying which claims are worth checking. On Wikipedia this appears as detecting uncited statements that need references. Existing work focuses mainly on high-resource languages and often assumes access to large language models, which low-resource communities and organizations may not have due to cost and infrastructure limitations. There is a need for multilingual resources and efficient models that work well for lower-resource languages and can be practically deployed.
AI.method: The authors construct MCN, a multilingual Citation Needed Detection corpus covering 18 languages categorized into three resource levels. They then perform extensive experiments using relatively small decoder-based language models (SLMs), training them with an encoder-style fine-tuning objective tailored to the CND task. They compare these fine-tuned SLMs against prompted large language models across all languages, and also study cross-lingual transfer by training only on English claims and evaluating on other languages with minimal or no adaptation.
AI.result: Fine-tuned small decoder models with an encoder-style objective consistently outperform prompted LLMs on citation-needed detection across the 18 languages. In cross-lingual scenarios, SLMs trained exclusively on English still surpass LLM baselines on other languages, even when those target languages receive little or no task-specific adaptation.
AI.conclusion: For citation-needed detection on Wikipedia, particularly in lower-resource language editions, compact task-specific models trained on an appropriate objective are more effective and practical than relying on general-purpose prompted LLMs. The released MCN corpus and code provide a foundation for further research on multilingual and cross-lingual citation-needed detection and support more equitable AFC tools for low-resource communities.

title: Skill Availability and Presentation Granularity in Large-Language-Model Agents: A Controlled SkillsBench Study
summary: Skill documents provide procedural knowledge to large-language-model agents at inference time. This article studies whether the presentation granularity of controlled skill knowledge changes downstream task success. The experiment uses a pinned SkillsBench version, a 30-task domain-balanced subset validated by official oracle runs, two reasoning-enabled model configurations, six skill conditions, and five trials per task-condition-model cell. Skill availability is the clearest empirical signal. Relative to no skill, skill conditions increase task-mean pass rate by 26.7 to 36.0 percentage points for GPT-5.5 and by 18.0 to 26.0 percentage points for DeepSeek V4-Flash. The final data contain 1,800 rows, with 900 rows for each model. The task is the inference unit. Five trials are aggregated within each task-condition-model cell before paired contrasts are estimated over 30 tasks. The primary presentation contrasts are smaller and uncertain. Low-abstraction guidance differs from high-abstraction guidance by +0.7 percentage points for GPT-5.5 and -6.7 percentage points for DeepSeek V4-Flash, with both 95% bootstrap confidence intervals crossing zero. Adding one worked example to medium-abstraction guidance differs from the no-example variant by +0.7 and +1.3 percentage points. Mean-reward robustness checks preserve the same substantive conclusion. In this controlled subset, skill availability is associated with higher success than no skill, while the tested presentation-granularity changes yield small, uncertain, and model-dependent effects.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how different granularities of skill-document presentation affect the performance of LLM-based agents and finds that having any skill documentation helps a lot, while changing granularity or adding examples has only small, uncertain effects.
AI.motivation: Large-language-model agents can be given procedural “skill documents” at inference time, but it is unclear whether the way these skills are presented—at different levels of abstraction or with/without worked examples—meaningfully changes task success beyond merely having skills at all.
AI.method: Using a fixed SkillsBench subset of 30 validated tasks, the authors test two reasoning-enabled LLM configurations (GPT-5.5 and DeepSeek V4-Flash) under six different skill-document conditions, including no-skill and varying abstraction levels and examples, with five trials per task-condition-model cell. They aggregate the five trials per cell, then estimate paired contrasts over the 30 tasks, using bootstrap confidence intervals and reward-based robustness checks.
AI.result: Across 1,800 runs (900 per model), providing any skill documentation markedly boosts task-mean pass rates relative to no-skill: +26.7 to +36.0 percentage points for GPT-5.5 and +18.0 to +26.0 points for DeepSeek V4-Flash. In contrast, differences between low- vs high-abstraction guidance and between medium-abstraction with vs without a worked example are small (roughly −6.7 to +1.3 percentage points) and statistically uncertain, with confidence intervals overlapping zero.
AI.conclusion: For this controlled SkillsBench subset, the dominant factor is whether skill documents are available at all; their presence significantly improves LLM agent success. Finer-grained changes in presentation granularity or the inclusion of a single worked example produce small, noisy, and model-dependent effects, suggesting limited additional benefit from such tuning under the tested conditions.

title: Neuro-symbolic Syntactic Parsing: Shaping a Neural Network with the CYK Algorithm
summary: In this paper, we show the possibility of a direct injection of algorithms into neural network architecture. We focus on a complex algorithm, that is, Cocke-Youger-Kasami (CYK) for parsing context-free grammars in Chomsky Normal Form and we propose CYKNN, a simple recurrent neural network architecture for encoding the CYK algorithm in trainable matrix-vector multiplications.We experimented with a very simple grammar with 4 variations showing that our approach outperforms existing LLMs with more than 20B parameters with an in-context learning setting and smaller LLMs of the Qwen family fine-tuned with LoRA. Our attempt paves the way to a different approach to neuro-symbolic methodologies.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CYKNN, a recurrent neural network that directly encodes the CYK parsing algorithm as trainable matrix–vector operations, demonstrating strong performance on a small grammar compared to much larger LLMs.
AI.motivation: To explore whether classical symbolic algorithms—specifically the CYK parser for context-free grammars—can be directly injected into neural architectures, potentially overcoming limitations of standard LLMs on precise algorithmic reasoning and paving a new path for neuro-symbolic integration.
AI.method: Design a simple recurrent neural network, CYKNN, whose architecture and data flow explicitly mirror the dynamic programming steps of the CYK algorithm using matrix–vector multiplications. Train and evaluate CYKNN on parsing tasks defined by a very simple context-free grammar instantiated in four variations, and compare against large LLMs (>20B parameters) under in-context learning and smaller Qwen models fine-tuned with LoRA.
AI.result: On parsing tasks involving a simple grammar with four variants, CYKNN outperforms larger LLMs with more than 20B parameters used in an in-context setting, as well as smaller Qwen-family LLMs fine-tuned via LoRA, indicating that the algorithm-structured network is more accurate and/or efficient for this task.
AI.conclusion: Directly encoding classical algorithms into neural network architectures, as done with CYKNN for CYK parsing, is feasible and can yield superior performance to much larger, more general-purpose LLMs on targeted algorithmic tasks, suggesting a promising alternative direction for neuro-symbolic methods.

title: DOA: Training-Free Decoder-Only Attention Policy for Long-Form Simultaneous Translation with SpeechLLMs
summary: Simultaneous speech-to-text translation (SimulST) generates translations while speech is still unfolding, requiring a streaming policy that decides when to read and when to write. State-of-the-art approaches rely on attention-based encoder-decoder models where cross-attention provides explicit alignment signals. In contrast, Speech Large Language Models (SpeechLLMs) are decoder-only architectures relying solely on self-attention. This raises a central question: whether decoder self-attention contains sufficiently stable alignment signals to guide the streaming policy. Moreover, existing approaches typically rely on training-based adaptations or heuristic wait-$k$ policies and have not been validated in long-form settings. To fill these gaps, we propose Decoder-Only Attention (DOA), a training-free policy that enables long-form simultaneous translation with off-the-shelf SpeechLLMs by deriving a proxy alignment from self-attention. Experiments on Phi4-Multimodal and Qwen3-Omni show that DOA provides an effective alignment signal for supporting streaming decisions, enabling low-latency long-form SimulST with quality close to offline decoding without retraining.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a training-free streaming policy (DOA) that extracts alignment signals from decoder self-attention in SpeechLLMs to enable low-latency, long-form simultaneous speech translation with quality close to offline decoding.
AI.motivation: Simultaneous speech-to-text translation requires deciding when to read more speech and when to output translations. Existing state-of-the-art methods depend on encoder-decoder models whose cross-attention provides explicit alignment, and they often use heuristic or training-intensive policies that have not been tested for long-form inputs. With the rise of decoder-only SpeechLLMs that lack cross-attention, it is unclear whether their self-attention can offer stable alignment signals for streaming, and there is a need for methods that can exploit these models without retraining.
AI.method: The authors propose Decoder-Only Attention (DOA), a training-free streaming policy for SpeechLLMs. DOA derives a proxy alignment between source speech and target text directly from the model’s decoder self-attention patterns, and uses this inferred alignment to drive read/write decisions in simultaneous translation. It is applied on top of off-the-shelf SpeechLLMs such as Phi4-Multimodal and Qwen3-Omni, targeting long-form speech scenarios.
AI.result: On experiments with SpeechLLMs like Phi4-Multimodal and Qwen3-Omni, DOA yields a reliable alignment signal that supports effective streaming decisions. The method achieves low-latency, long-form simultaneous speech translation with translation quality that is close to that of offline decoding, without any retraining or task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Decoder self-attention in SpeechLLMs can be exploited as a practical alignment signal for simultaneous speech translation. The proposed DOA policy enables training-free, low-latency, long-form SimulST using off-the-shelf decoder-only models, narrowing the performance gap with offline translation and demonstrating that explicit cross-attention is not strictly necessary for effective streaming alignment.

title: Fine-grained Verification via Diagnostic Reasoning Supervision for Aspect Sentiment Triplet Extraction
summary: Aspect Sentiment Triplet Extraction (ASTE) aims to identify aspect terms, opinion terms, and sentiment polarities as structured triplets, providing essential inputs for downstream information system applications such as opinion mining, explainable recommendations, and review summarization. Prior work mainly focuses on end-to-end extraction, while post hoc verification of extracted triplets remains comparatively underexplored. This gap limits the reliability of ASTE systems, since predicted triplets may be locally plausible while being globally invalid. Moreover, candidate invalidity is multi-faceted and candidate usability is inherently graded, motivating a fine-grained verification mechanism that can filter or re-rank outputs from diverse extractors. In this paper, we propose FiVeD, a framework for Fine-grained Verification with Diagnostic reasoning supervision. Specifically, the verifier is trained with multiple complementary objectives, including validity classification and quality score estimation as primary tasks, with error type classification and rationale generation as auxiliary tasks. We define hierarchical error categories and construct plausible incorrect triplets under semantic and syntactic constraints, and leverage an off-the-shelf LLM with task-specific rubrics to produce quality scores and diagnostic rationales. During inference, the resulting quality scores are used to filter candidate outputs, supporting adjustable precision-recall tradeoffs. Experiments across multiple ASTE baselines demonstrate that FiVeD consistently improves extraction performance by up to 3.53 F1 points as a plug-and-play verification module.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes FiVeD, a fine-grained verification framework that post-processes Aspect Sentiment Triplet Extraction (ASTE) outputs to filter and re-rank triplets, improving overall extraction performance.
AI.motivation: Existing ASTE research mainly emphasizes end-to-end triplet extraction and pays little attention to verifying whether the extracted triplets are globally valid and reliable. Extracted triplets can be locally plausible but collectively inconsistent or incorrect. In addition, invalidity can arise from multiple types of errors and the quality of candidate triplets is better viewed on a graded scale than a binary one. This motivates a dedicated verification module that can assess, diagnose, and score ASTE outputs in a nuanced way, and can be easily attached to diverse base extractors.
AI.method: The authors introduce FiVeD, a Fine-grained Verification framework with Diagnostic reasoning supervision. They design a verifier model trained with multiple complementary objectives: (1) validity classification of candidate triplets, (2) quality score estimation, and two auxiliary tasks: (3) error type classification and (4) rationale (diagnostic explanation) generation. They define a hierarchical taxonomy of error categories and synthesize plausible but incorrect triplets by applying semantic and syntactic constraints. An off-the-shelf large language model is prompted with task-specific rubrics to assign quality scores and generate diagnostic rationales, which supervise the verifier. At inference time, FiVeD takes candidate triplets from various ASTE baselines, scores them, and then filters or re-ranks them according to adjustable thresholds to control the precision-recall tradeoff.
AI.result: Across multiple ASTE baseline extractors, plugging in FiVeD as a post hoc verification module consistently boosts extraction performance, achieving up to 3.53 F1 score improvement over the baselines. The framework demonstrates strong generality, working as a plug-and-play component regardless of the underlying ASTE architecture, and shows that fine-grained verification and diagnostic supervision can meaningfully enhance triplet quality.
AI.conclusion: FiVeD establishes that explicit, fine-grained verification with diagnostic supervision is an effective way to improve Aspect Sentiment Triplet Extraction. By jointly learning to classify validity, estimate quality, identify error types, and generate rationales—supported by LLM-derived rubrics—the framework can reliably filter and re-rank candidate triplets from different extractors. This leads to more accurate and trustworthy ASTE outputs, while allowing flexible control over precision-recall tradeoffs, and suggests a general strategy for adding verification layers to other structured prediction tasks.

title: Towards Efficient LLMs Annealing with Principled Sample Selection
summary: The annealing phase is a pivotal convergence stage in LLM pre-training that ultimately determines final model quality. However, effectively selecting training data during this phase remains a key challenge. Current strategies rely on empirical heuristics, such as domain filtering or context extension, which lack a principled grounding in optimization theory. In this work, we characterize the annealing phase through the lens of the loss landscape's spectral geometry. We argue that optimal convergence requires gradient updates to satisfy heterogeneous constraints across different eigen-directions. Building on this insight, we formulate data selection as a problem of satisfying these directional constraints. To this end, we propose DiReCT (Directionally-Restrained Constrained Training), a novel framework that reformulates sample selection in the annealing stage as a constrained optimization problem. By imposing explicit directional constraints on per-sample gradients based on the spectral properties of the Hessian, DiReCT identifies samples that align with the optimal curvature-aware descent path. Extensive experiments across various model scales demonstrate that DiReCT consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance. For future research, code is available at https://github.com/xuyj233/Direct.

AI.tldr: They propose DiReCT, a Hessian-spectrum-aware data selection framework for the annealing phase of LLM pre-training, improving convergence and final performance by enforcing directional gradient constraints.
AI.motivation: In the annealing phase of LLM pre-training, model quality is highly sensitive to which training samples are seen, yet current data selection strategies are largely heuristic (e.g., domain filters, longer contexts) and lack a theoretical foundation. The authors want a principled, optimization-theoretic way to select data that directly targets better convergence in this late training stage.
AI.method: The authors analyze the loss landscape of pre-training through its spectral geometry, focusing on Hessian eigen-directions. They argue that optimal convergence requires gradient updates to respect heterogeneous constraints along different eigen-directions. They then cast data selection during annealing as a constrained optimization problem, where each candidate sample’s gradient is evaluated against explicit directional constraints derived from the Hessian spectrum. Their framework, DiReCT (Directionally-Restrained Constrained Training), selects samples whose per-sample gradients align with a curvature-aware descent path implied by these constraints.
AI.result: Using DiReCT for data selection in the annealing phase yields consistent improvements over heuristic baselines across multiple LLM scales. In extensive experiments, models trained with DiReCT achieve state-of-the-art results versus prior data selection or curriculum-style strategies, indicating better convergence and final performance.
AI.conclusion: The annealing phase can be better controlled by viewing it through the Hessian’s spectral geometry and enforcing direction-specific gradient constraints. DiReCT operationalizes this as a constrained optimization-based data selection procedure, leading to superior pre-training outcomes. This shows that principled, curvature-aware data selection during annealing is both feasible and beneficial for large language model training.

title: Scaling Conversational Hungarian ASR: The BEA-Dialogue+ Corpus
summary: Conversational automatic speech recognition in Hungarian is constrained by the limited amount of publicly available dialogue-style training data. The BEA-Dialogue corpus addresses this need, but its strictly speaker-disjoint train/dev/eval split reduces the usable material to only 85 hours. In this paper, we introduce BEA-Dialogue+, an expanded version of the corpus that relaxes the split criterion for experimenters and dialogue partners while preserving complete separation of the primary speakers. This results in 200 hours of transcribed natural conversations and enables a controlled study of the trade-off between additional training data and speaker overlap across the splits. We evaluate several Whisper- and FastConformer-based models on both corpus versions, including Serialized Output Training (SOT)-based fine-tuning for dialogue transcription. Our results show that the larger corpus is more challenging for models without fine-tuning, whereas SOT-based adaptation yields consistent improvements in WER, CER, cpWER, and cpCER. Overall, BEA-Dialogue+ provides a substantially larger yet still demanding benchmark for Hungarian dialogue ASR, and a practical resource for training and evaluating dialogue transcription systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents BEA-Dialogue+, an expanded 200-hour Hungarian conversational speech corpus that relaxes some speaker-disjointness constraints, enabling better training and controlled evaluation of dialogue ASR systems, and shows that SOT-based fine-tuning improves recognition performance on this more challenging benchmark.
AI.motivation: Hungarian conversational ASR suffers from a shortage of publicly available dialogue-style training data. The existing BEA-Dialogue corpus further limits usable data to 85 hours due to a strictly speaker-disjoint train/dev/test split, constraining model training and making it hard to study how relaxing speaker separation might improve performance. There is a need for a larger, well-structured corpus that both increases training material and allows systematic investigation of the trade-off between data size and speaker overlap.
AI.method: The authors construct BEA-Dialogue+, an extended version of the BEA-Dialogue corpus that relaxes the speaker-disjoint splitting rules for experimenters and dialogue partners while maintaining full separation of primary speakers between train/dev/eval. This yields 200 hours of transcribed natural Hungarian conversations. They then train and evaluate several ASR models based on Whisper and FastConformer architectures on both the original and expanded corpora. Additionally, they apply Serialized Output Training (SOT) for dialogue transcription fine-tuning and compare performance in terms of WER, CER, cpWER, and cpCER across the two corpus variants and training regimes.
AI.result: Without fine-tuning, models find the larger BEA-Dialogue+ corpus more challenging, exhibiting degraded performance relative to the original BEA-Dialogue benchmark. However, when SOT-based adaptation is applied, all evaluated models show consistent performance gains across word error rate (WER), character error rate (CER), and conversation-participant-normalized metrics (cpWER, cpCER). This demonstrates that the expanded corpus, combined with appropriate fine-tuning, can be leveraged effectively despite its increased difficulty.
AI.conclusion: BEA-Dialogue+ offers a substantially larger (200-hour) and still challenging benchmark for Hungarian dialogue ASR, addressing data scarcity while preserving primary speaker separation across splits. The corpus enables controlled study of how additional training data and speaker overlap affect performance, and the experiments indicate that SOT-based fine-tuning is an effective strategy for improving recognition quality on this resource. Consequently, BEA-Dialogue+ represents a practical and valuable dataset for training and evaluating Hungarian dialogue transcription systems.

title: If LLMs Have Human-Like Attributes, Then So Does Age of Empires II
summary: Much research has been carried out on large language models (LLMs) and LLM-powered agentic workflows. However, many works within the field state emergence of, ascribe to, or assume, generalised anthropomorphic attributes to them (e.g., morality or understanding of natural language). Our goal is not to argue in favour or against the existence of these attributes, but to point out that these conclusions could be incorrect. For this we build and train a simple neural network on the videogame Age of Empires II, and note that any entity in a sufficiently-powerful substrate, such as LEGO or the Greater Boston Area, could also present such attributes. Hence, the purported anthropomorphic attributes of LLMs are empirically non-unique: although some properties (e.g., responses to prompts) could remain constant, others, such as the interpretation of their perceived behaviour, might change with the substrate. Thus, any empirically-grounded discussion requires explicit measurement criteria; otherwise the interpretation is left to the representation. We then show that assuming that these attributes exist or not in a system, independent of the substrate and in a generalised way, leads to either circular or uninformative conclusions, regardless of the experimenter's viewpoint on the subject. Finally we propose a 'null' assumption, where one assumes LLM non-uniqueness instead of assuming anthropomorphic attributes to set up an experiment, along with examples of it. We also discuss potential objections to our work, briefly survey the field, and prove that \textit{Age of Empires II} is functionally- and Turing-complete.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that anthropomorphic attributes ascribed to LLMs (like morality or understanding) are not empirically unique to them, and that such claims become circular or uninformative unless grounded in explicit, substrate‑aware measurement criteria.
AI.motivation: Many LLM and agent papers talk about LLMs as if they possess human‑like properties (morality, understanding, intentions) and often treat these as special or emergent features of LLMs. The authors are concerned that these claims may be mistaken or at least under‑specified, because similar attributions could be made to other systems. They want to clarify how such attributions should be evaluated empirically and to avoid misleading or unfalsifiable claims.
AI.method: They construct and train a simple neural network on the game Age of Empires II and use it as a concrete case study to show that systems implemented in very different substrates (e.g., a video game environment, LEGO, or even a whole city) can be ascribed similar anthropomorphic attributes. They analyze how behaviors, responses, and interpretations vary with the substrate, and they provide a formal argument that Age of Empires II is functionally and Turing‑complete. Conceptually, they examine how different assumptions about the existence of anthropomorphic properties (assuming they exist vs. assuming they do not) lead to circular or empirically empty conclusions.
AI.result: They demonstrate that purported anthropomorphic attributes of LLMs are empirically non‑unique: comparable attributes can be ascribed to entities implemented in other sufficiently powerful substrates. They show that, without clearly defined measurement criteria, interpretations of behavior depend heavily on how the system is represented, not on unique properties of LLMs. They also show that both pro‑ and anti‑anthropomorphic stances about such attributes lead to circular or uninformative reasoning when phrased in a substrate‑independent, generalized way. They provide examples of experimental setups under a proposed alternative assumption.
AI.conclusion: LLMs should not be treated as uniquely bearing anthropomorphic attributes based solely on their observed behaviors; similar attributions can arise in many other substrates, including systems built in Age of Empires II, which they prove is Turing‑complete. Any serious empirical discussion about properties like “morality” or “understanding” in LLMs requires explicit, operational measurement criteria and must be sensitive to the substrate in which the system is realized. The authors recommend a null assumption of LLM non‑uniqueness—designing experiments without presupposing that LLMs either do or do not possess such anthropomorphic attributes—to avoid circularity and make conclusions more informative.

title: Learning Whom to Trust: Market-Feedback Adaptive Retrieval for Frozen LLMs in Event-Driven Financial RAG
summary: Financial retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems typically rank evidence by textual relevance, but in financial markets the useful evidence source depends on event type, forecast horizon, and market context. We study news-triggered event-impact prediction as a point-in-time financial RAG problem. For each company-news anchor, the system retrieves related financial news and SEC filing passages, appends a pre-decision market-context card, and predicts multi-horizon residual-return signals. Our method keeps the large language model (LLM) reader frozen and adapts the retrieval layer through an external Bayesian source memory updated from matured residual-return feedback. On a fixed 89-stock Nasdaq-oriented universe derived from the FinRL-DeepSeek/FNSPID task, using original FNSPID news and point-in-time EDGAR filing passages, Frozen Reader with Source Memory improves held-out macro-F1 from 0.438 to 0.471 and downstream portfolio Sharpe from 0.52 to 0.84 relative to Frozen Reader with No Memory. A supervised LoRA reader improves static RAG modestly, but does not improve over the frozen source-memory reader. These results suggest that, for financial RAG, learning where to retrieve from can be as important as learning how to read, offering a simple, modular route to market-feedback adaptation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a financial RAG framework that keeps the LLM reader frozen but learns an adaptive retrieval layer (“source memory”) using market feedback, improving both predictive F1 and portfolio Sharpe on a Nasdaq stock universe.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG in finance usually retrieves evidence purely by text relevance, ignoring that the most useful information source (news vs. filings, etc.) depends on event type, time horizon, and market conditions. The authors want a system that can adapt, via realized market outcomes, to which sources and contexts are most informative for predicting the impact of news on stock returns.
AI.method: They frame news-triggered event-impact prediction as a point-in-time financial RAG problem. For each company-news event, the system retrieves associated news articles and SEC filing snippets, appends a pre-decision market-context card, and asks an LLM to predict multi-horizon residual-return signals. The LLM reader is kept frozen; instead, they add an external Bayesian “source memory” at the retrieval layer, which is updated over time using matured residual-return labels. They compare this frozen reader with and without source memory and also with a supervised LoRA-tuned reader using static RAG.
AI.result: On a fixed 89-stock Nasdaq-focused universe from the FinRL-DeepSeek/FNSPID setup, using FNSPID news plus point-in-time EDGAR filings, the Frozen Reader with Source Memory increases held-out macro-F1 from 0.438 to 0.471 and boosts portfolio Sharpe from 0.52 to 0.84 versus the frozen reader without memory. A supervised LoRA reader improves static RAG somewhat but still underperforms the frozen source-memory configuration.
AI.conclusion: Adapting the retrieval layer to learn which sources to query, driven by market-feedback signals, can be as or more beneficial than fine-tuning the LLM reader itself. The proposed frozen-reader-plus-source-memory design is simple and modular yet yields meaningful gains in both prediction accuracy and trading performance, highlighting the value of market-aware retrieval in financial RAG systems.

title: What Gets Unmasked First? Trajectory Analysis of Diffusion Models for Graph-to-Text Generation
summary: We present the first systematic study of masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) for graph-to-text generation. We analyze MDLM generation trajectories -- the order in which tokens are unmasked during iterative decoding -- and find that, unlike autoregressive LLMs which generate text linearly, MDLMs naturally prioritize entities first, followed by relational and function words, with structural tokens resolved last. We further identify a previously undocumented failure mode of supervised fine-tuning: SFT disrupts this strategy by prematurely anchoring structural sentence-ending tokens early in the decoding trajectory, effectively fixing the output length which can lead to omitted or hallucinated information. To address this, we propose lambda-scaled structural decoding, a training-free inference-time modification that downweights structural token confidence and recovers +9.4 BLEU-4. Finally, we introduce Graph-LLaDA, which integrates a Graph Transformer encoder into LLaDA's decoding process to explicitly incorporate relational graph structure. Cross-dataset evaluation on LAGRANGE reveals that previous baselines overfit to dataset-specific patterns, while LLM- and MDLM-based approaches generalize significantly better.

AI.tldr: Systematic study of masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) for graph-to-text, revealing their inherent decoding order and proposing methods to correct SFT-induced failures and better leverage graph structure.
AI.motivation: Graph-to-text generation often relies on autoregressive LLMs or models that overfit to dataset artifacts. Masked diffusion language models are promising but poorly understood, especially regarding their decoding behavior and robustness across datasets. There is also an observed degradation from supervised fine-tuning and a lack of methods that explicitly integrate graph structure into powerful LLM-style decoders.
AI.method: 1) Empirically analyze MDLM decoding trajectories to determine the order in which token categories (entities, relational words, structural tokens) are predicted. 2) Identify how supervised fine-tuning alters these trajectories and causes a new failure mode involving early commitment to structural sentence-ending tokens. 3) Propose lambda-scaled structural decoding, an inference-time adjustment that downweights structural token probabilities without retraining. 4) Design Graph-LLaDA by inserting a Graph Transformer encoder into the LLaDA MDLM decoding framework to directly encode relational graph structure. 5) Evaluate all methods on the LAGRANGE benchmark and perform cross-dataset generalization tests.
AI.result: The analysis shows MDLMs naturally generate entities first, then relational/function words, and only later determine structural tokens, unlike linear autoregressive generation. Supervised fine-tuning distorts this behavior by making structural tokens appear too early, effectively fixing output length and causing omissions or hallucinations. Lambda-scaled structural decoding mitigates this and yields a +9.4 BLEU-4 improvement. Graph-LLaDA, which injects a Graph Transformer encoder into LLaDA, exhibits strong cross-dataset generalization on LAGRANGE. Baselines that do not leverage MDLMs or LLMs tend to overfit to dataset-specific patterns and perform worse when evaluated out-of-domain.
AI.conclusion: Masked diffusion language models possess a beneficial, non-linear decoding strategy that prioritizes semantic content (entities and relations) before structure. Supervised fine-tuning can inadvertently damage this property by forcing early structural commitments, but a simple inference-time correction restores and even improves performance. Integrating explicit graph structure into MDLM decoders (Graph-LLaDA) further enhances cross-dataset generalization, suggesting MDLMs and LLM-based graph encoders are more robust and better-suited for graph-to-text generation than traditional baselines.

title: LongTraceRL: Learning Long-Context Reasoning from Search Agent Trajectories with Rubric Rewards
summary: Long-context reasoning remains a central challenge for large language models, which often fail to locate and integrate key information in extensive distracting content. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has shown promise for this task, yet existing methods are limited by low-confusability distractors and sparse, outcome-only reward signals that cannot supervise intermediate reasoning steps. To address these issues, we introduce \textsc{LongTraceRL}. For data construction, we generate multi-hop questions via knowledge graph random walks and leverage search agent trajectories to build \emph{tiered distractors}: documents the agent read but did not cite (high confusability) and documents that appeared in search results but were never opened (low confusability), producing training contexts that are far more challenging than those built by random sampling or one-shot search. For reward design, we propose a \emph{rubric reward} that uses the gold entities along each reasoning chain as fine-grained, entity-level process supervision. This rubric reward is applied only to responses with correct final answers (positive-only strategy), distinguishing the reasoning quality among correct responses and preventing reward hacking. Experiments on three reasoning LLMs (4B--30B) across five long-context benchmarks demonstrate that \textsc{LongTraceRL} consistently outperforms strong baselines and encourages comprehensive, evidence-grounded reasoning. Codes, datasets and models are available at \href{https://github.com/THU-KEG/LongTraceRL}{https://github.com/THU-KEG/LongTraceRL}.

AI.tldr: LongTraceRL improves long-context reasoning in LLMs using harder distractors and fine-grained reinforcement learning rewards based on reasoning chains, leading to better evidence-grounded performance on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing large language models struggle with long-context reasoning because they cannot reliably extract and integrate key information from large amounts of distracting text. Prior RL with verifiable rewards approaches help but are limited: their distractors are too easy (low confusability) and their rewards are sparse and only tied to final outcomes, providing no direct supervision for intermediate reasoning steps. The paper aims to build a more challenging training setup and a more informative reward signal that guides the entire reasoning process, not just the final answer.
AI.method: The authors propose LongTraceRL. For data, they create multi-hop questions using random walks on a knowledge graph, then run search agents whose trajectories produce tiered distractors: (1) high-confusability documents that were opened but not used; and (2) low-confusability documents that appeared in search results but were never opened. This yields harder, more realistic long-context scenarios than random sampling or single search queries. For rewards, they design a rubric reward that uses gold entities along each step of the correct reasoning chain as process-level supervision. This reward is only applied to responses with correct final answers (a positive-only strategy) to compare quality among correct chains and reduce reward hacking, rather than rewarding incorrect but superficially aligned reasoning.
AI.result: On three different reasoning LLMs ranging from 4B to 30B parameters and across five long-context benchmarks, LongTraceRL consistently outperforms strong baselines, including prior RLVR setups. The models trained with LongTraceRL show better performance on tasks requiring long-context integration and demonstrate more complete, evidence-grounded reasoning traces compared to alternatives. The constructed datasets and trained models are released publicly.
AI.conclusion: LongTraceRL effectively addresses two key weaknesses of prior RLVR approaches for long-context reasoning: simplistic distractor construction and coarse, outcome-only rewards. By introducing tiered, high-confusability distractors and a fine-grained rubric reward over reasoning entities, applied only to correct answers, the method improves both accuracy and the quality of reasoning traces in LLMs on challenging long-context benchmarks. The work suggests that carefully engineered training contexts and process-level rewards are crucial for advancing long-context reasoning capabilities in language models.

title: Scaling Multi-Hop Training Data via Graph-Constrained Path Selection
summary: Endowing large language models with compositional reasoning over specialized documents requires multi-hop training data at scale, where such data rarely exists outside of curated benchmarks built on structured sources. To construct it directly from plain, unannotated text, existing methods ask a single teacher model to jointly discover an evidence path through a document and verbalize it as a question-answer pair. However, these methods degrade sharply when documents are structured around repetitive templates and densely cross-referencing clauses, conditions that characterize most real-world specialized corpora. In this work, we decouple the two operations: reasoning paths are enumerated offline over a graph of contextual keyword centroids, and the teacher is invoked only to verbalize pre-validated paths. The graph enforces five geometric admissibility constraints, for which we provide Gram-matrix arguments establishing that local similarity bounds alone admit endpoint drift up to ${\sim}91^{\circ}$, and that an upper similarity bound is necessary to exit dense embedding cliques formed by boilerplate text. A matched-size ablation isolates the mechanism: at equal training scale, constrained and unconstrained chains yield indistinguishable downstream performance, and the gain at full scale comes from a 4.4$\times$ expansion of the usable corpus rather than from higher per-chain quality -- reframing the role of graph constraints, in this setting, as raising teacher synthesizability rather than improving chain content. Fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on 80K examples constructed from the CUAD legal contract corpus improves closed-book Token F1 from 21.66% to 38.58%. We have released our codes at https://github.com/hkgai-official/GCSCS.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a graph-constrained method to automatically generate multi-hop training data from unannotated specialized documents, greatly expanding usable data and improving LLM performance on legal contract QA.
AI.motivation: Large language models need multi-hop, compositional reasoning data for specialized domains (like legal or technical documents), but such data is scarce and existing automatic generation methods fail on real-world corpora that are highly templated and full of cross-references. Current approaches rely on a single teacher model to simultaneously find reasoning paths and turn them into QA pairs, which breaks down in these noisy, repetitive settings. The authors aim to create a scalable way to extract reliable multi-hop reasoning chains directly from plain, unannotated text in such specialized corpora.
AI.method: They decouple path discovery from question-answer verbalization. First, they build a graph whose nodes are contextual keyword centroids derived from the corpus. Over this graph, they enumerate reasoning paths offline, enforcing five geometric admissibility constraints in the embedding space to ensure paths are meaningful rather than trapped in boilerplate. Using Gram-matrix based arguments, they analyze how local similarity constraints can still allow large angular drift (~91°) between endpoints and show that an upper similarity bound is crucial to escape dense embedding cliques caused by repeated template text. Only after valid paths are found do they call a teacher model to turn each path into a QA example, thus constraining the teacher to synthesize from pre-validated chains. They then fine-tune Qwen3-32B on the generated examples from the CUAD legal contract dataset and run ablations comparing constrained vs unconstrained chains at matched data scales.
AI.result: The geometric analysis shows that relying solely on local similarity lower bounds is insufficient because paths can drift substantially in embedding space, and that an upper similarity cap is necessary to avoid staying within boilerplate-heavy cliques. Empirically, when the number of training chains is held constant, constrained (graph-filtered) and unconstrained chains produce nearly identical downstream performance, indicating that constraints do not significantly improve per-chain quality. However, with the full method, graph constraints enable a 4.4× increase in the portion of the corpus from which usable chains can be synthesized. This larger, synthesizable corpus is what drives the performance gain. Fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on 80K generated examples from CUAD improves closed-book Token F1 from 21.66% to 38.58%, a substantial boost in legal QA ability.
AI.conclusion: Separating path discovery from language synthesis and constraining path discovery via geometric conditions over a centroid graph makes it practical to harvest large-scale multi-hop training data from real-world specialized corpora. The core benefit of the graph constraints is not to make each reasoning chain inherently better, but to vastly expand the share of the corpus that a teacher model can reliably turn into QA examples. This reframes constraints as tools for improving teacher synthesizability rather than per-chain quality. The approach yields significant performance gains on legal contract question answering, demonstrating its value for enhancing compositional reasoning of large language models in specialized domains.

title: Language Models Learn Constructional Semantics, Not To Mention Syntax: Investigating LM Understanding of Paired-Focus Constructions
summary: Grasping the semantics of rare constructions (form-meaning pairings) has been shown to be a challenging problem that has currently only been solved by the largest LLMs. It remains an open question if open-source models have robust constructional understanding, and if so, what learning dynamics underlie the acquisition of this knowledge. Focusing on a set of rare Paired-Focus constructions in English (e.g. "let alone", "much less"), we construct a novel dataset to test their meanings using both scalar adjectival semantics and general world knowledge. Testing a wide range of models differing in parameter count, architecture, and pretraining dataset size, we find that several modestly sized models are sensitive to both the forms and the meanings of Paired-Focus constructions, though models trained on human-scale data fail at all meaning evaluations. Turning to training dynamics for a set of open-checkpoint models, we find that Paired-Focus understanding emerges later in training than Paired-Focus syntactic knowledge, and that learning of Paired-Focus semantics is correlated with gains in some domains of world knowledge. Overall, our empirical results support the conclusion that modestly sized open-source models can grasp the rare Paired-Focus constructions, and demonstrate a connection between knowledge of Paired-Focus constructions and other meaning domains.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether open-source language models can understand the meanings of rare English Paired-Focus constructions (like “let alone” and “much less”), showing that several modestly sized models do succeed, and that semantic understanding emerges later in training and is linked to gains in world knowledge.
AI.motivation: Previous work suggests that only very large proprietary LLMs robustly understand rare constructions, leaving open whether smaller, open-source models can do so and how such knowledge develops during training. The authors aim to fill this gap, both to assess the true linguistic competence of open models and to better understand the learning dynamics of constructional semantics, focusing on difficult, infrequent Paired-Focus constructions.
AI.method: The authors design a dedicated evaluation dataset targeting the semantics of rare English Paired-Focus constructions (e.g., “let alone”, “much less”) using tests that rely on scalar adjective meanings and general world knowledge. They then evaluate a wide array of language models that vary in size, architecture, and pretraining data scale on this dataset. For a subset of open-checkpoint models, they track performance across training checkpoints to study when syntactic vs. semantic knowledge of these constructions emerges and how this relates to broader world-knowledge learning.
AI.result: Several modestly sized open-source models show sensitivity not only to the surface forms of Paired-Focus constructions but also to their intended meanings, successfully passing the semantic evaluations. In contrast, models trained only on human-scale data volume fail on all meaning-focused tests. Analysis of training checkpoints reveals that syntactic competence with Paired-Focus constructions develops earlier, while full semantic understanding appears later and is positively correlated with improvements in certain world-knowledge domains.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that robust understanding of rare Paired-Focus constructions is not exclusive to the largest proprietary models: modestly sized open-source LMs can acquire both their form and meaning. Furthermore, the temporal gap between syntactic and semantic acquisition, and its correlation with world-knowledge gains, suggests that constructional semantics emerges as part of broader meaning and knowledge development during training.

title: Mellum2 Technical Report
summary: We present Mellum 2, an open-weight 12B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language model with 2.5B active parameters per token. Mellum 2 is a general-purpose language model specialized in software engineering, spanning code generation and editing, debugging, multi-step reasoning, tool use and function calling, agentic coding, and conversational programming assistance, and it is the successor to the completion-focused 4B dense Mellum model. The architecture builds on the Mixture-of-Experts (64 experts, 8 active) and combines Grouped-Query Attention with 4 KV heads, Sliding Window Attention on three of every four layers, and a single Multi-Token Prediction head that doubles as both an auxiliary pre-training objective and a built-in draft model for speculative decoding; each choice was validated by ablation with inference efficiency on commodity GPUs as a design constraint. Pre-training spans approximately 10.6 trillion tokens through a three-phase curriculum that progressively shifts the mixture from diverse web data toward curated code and mathematical content, optimized with Muon under FP8 hybrid precision and a Warmup-Hold-Decay schedule with linear decay to zero. The pre-trained base is extended to a 128K context window via a layer-selective YaRN and then post-trained in two stages (supervised fine-tuning followed by RLVR), yielding two released variants: an Instruct model that answers directly and a Thinking model that emits an explicit reasoning trace before its final answer. Across code generation, math and reasoning, tool use, knowledge, and safety benchmarks, Mellum 2 is competitive with open-weight baselines in the 4B-14B range while running at the per-token compute of a 2.5B dense model. We release the base, instruct, and thinking checkpoints, together with this report on the architecture decisions, data pipeline, and training recipe behind them, under the Apache 2.0 license.

AI.tldr: Mellum 2 is a 12B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts code-specialized LLM that uses only 2.5B active parameters per token, optimized for efficient inference on commodity GPUs and released as base, instruct, and thinking variants.
AI.motivation: To build an open-weight, highly efficient yet strong language model specialized for software engineering tasks (code generation, editing, debugging, agentic coding, etc.), achieving performance comparable to larger models while keeping per-token compute low enough for commodity hardware, and to provide a transparent report of the architecture, data, and training recipe.
AI.method: They design a 12B-parameter MoE architecture with 64 experts and 8 active per token, using Grouped-Query Attention with 4 KV heads and Sliding Window Attention in most layers to improve efficiency. A single Multi-Token Prediction head serves as both an auxiliary training objective and a speculative decoding draft model. The model is pretrained on ~10.6T tokens with a three-phase curriculum that gradually shifts from diverse web data to curated code and math, trained with the Muon optimizer in FP8 hybrid precision and a Warmup-Hold-Decay schedule. After pretraining, they extend context to 128K using layer-selective YaRN, then apply two-stage post-training (SFT and RLVR) to produce Instruct and Thinking variants that differ mainly in how they present reasoning.
AI.result: Mellum 2 achieves performance competitive with other open-weight language models in the 4B–14B parameter range on benchmarks for code generation, math and reasoning, tool use, knowledge, and safety, while matching the per-token compute cost of a 2.5B dense model. They successfully train and release multiple checkpoints (base, instruct, thinking) and validate each major architectural choice via ablations with inference efficiency constraints.
AI.conclusion: A carefully engineered MoE architecture plus large-scale, curriculum-based pretraining and targeted post-training can yield an efficient, open-weight, code-specialized LLM that rivals much larger models at a fraction of the per-token compute. Mellum 2 demonstrates that MoE, attention optimizations, and multi-token prediction can be combined to provide strong software engineering capabilities and long-context reasoning while remaining practical to deploy on commodity GPUs, and the full recipe is shared under Apache 2.0 to support community use and extension.

title: Wind Turbine Maintenance Log Labelling Framework: LLM-Driven Data Correction and Enrichment via Semantic Extraction of Reliability Intelligence
comment: An adjustable template containing the Python script architecture, applied dynamic prompts, and data schemas is hosted in an open-source GitHub repository: https://github.com/mvmalyi/llm-driven-wind-turbine-maintenance-log-labelling
summary: As wind turbine fleets age, data-driven reliability engineering is essential to optimise their operation and maintenance for service life extension and levelised cost of energy reduction. Failure event descriptions within historical maintenance logs are a source of valuable reliability intelligence. However, they typically appear as unstructured natural language entries, rendering them inaccessible for quantitative analysis. This paper presents a novel methodology leveraging a large language model (LLM) to systematically standardise and structure maintenance logs based on their free-text descriptors. Operating on a dataset of 16,316 maintenance logs from 280 turbines monitored over nine years, the developed model-agnostic framework autonomously corrected hierarchical system codes and extracted evidence-based taxonomies of maintenance actions and failure modes. The automated pipeline successfully structured over 70% of the dataset. It resolved pervasive misclassification issues, such as isolating previously unclassified pitch system faults and restoring missing system codes, and enriched the records by applying empirical taxonomies to label specific actions taken and failure modes addressed. By using system-based log batches to construct empirical dictionaries of failure modes, observable symptoms, dominant mechanisms, and candidate causes, this approach reduces the inherent subjectivity of manual failure modes and effects analysis (FMEA). Ultimately, the methodology provides a highly scalable, cost-effective blueprint for translating large sets of qualitative field observations into quantitative reliability metrics, laying the foundation for integrated root-cause analysis across the renewable energy sector, improved FMEA, and advanced predictive maintenance.

AI.tldr: The paper uses a large language model to turn free-text wind turbine maintenance logs into structured, standardised reliability data.
AI.motivation: Aging wind turbine fleets need better reliability engineering to extend service life and reduce levelised cost of energy. Historical maintenance logs contain rich failure information but are written as unstructured natural language, making them hard to analyse quantitatively. Existing coding of system hierarchies is often incomplete or incorrect, and manual FMEA is subjective and unscalable, motivating an automated, data-driven approach.
AI.method: The authors develop a model-agnostic framework based on a large language model that processes 16,316 maintenance logs from 280 turbines across nine years. The LLM automatically corrects and completes hierarchical system codes and extracts structured taxonomies of maintenance actions and failure modes from free-text descriptions. Using system-based batches of logs, the method builds empirical dictionaries of failure modes, observable symptoms, dominant mechanisms, and candidate causes, which are then applied to consistently label each record. The process is implemented as an automated pipeline for large-scale log standardisation.
AI.result: The automated LLM-based pipeline successfully structured more than 70% of the maintenance log dataset. It corrected widespread misclassification problems, identified previously unclassified pitch system faults, restored missing system codes, and enriched records with detailed labels of maintenance actions and failure modes based on empirical taxonomies. This led to higher-quality, more complete reliability data derived from originally unstructured text.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LLM-driven methodology offers a scalable, cost-effective way to convert qualitative wind turbine maintenance logs into quantitative reliability metrics. By building empirical, evidence-based taxonomies and dictionaries, it reduces the subjectivity of manual FMEA and supports integrated root-cause analysis, better FMEA quality, and advanced predictive maintenance across the renewable energy sector.

title: Divergence Decoding: Inference-Time Unlearning via Auxiliary Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently memorize sensitive training data thereby creating significant privacy and copyright risks. Addressing these risks, i.e., removing such knowledge from an existing model checkpoint, has proven challenging as many unlearning methods lead to catastrophic utility loss or are ineffective for complex queries. We introduce Divergence Decoding (DD), a mechanism that uses small auxiliary models to steer the logits of the LLM away from specific data during inference. Training these models is straight forward, i.e., we use standard pre-training and fine-tuning setups. We find the method decisively outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines on unlearning benchmarks across a variety of model and training dataset scales consistent with DD being an effective and inexpensive solution to unlearning. We then demonstrate that this steered distribution can be trivially distilled back into the base model. Since the method is generally applicable to any probabilistic model, we explore its efficacy outside of text generation and find evidence of generalization to the domain of images.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Divergence Decoding, a lightweight, training-time and inference-time method to unlearn specific data from large language models with minimal utility loss, using small auxiliary models to steer output distributions and enabling distillation of the steered behavior back into the base model, with preliminary evidence it generalizes beyond text to images.
AI.motivation: LLMs often memorize sensitive training data, exposing privacy and copyright risks when models regurgitate or exploit that information. Existing machine unlearning methods either substantially damage the model’s overall capabilities or fail on realistic, complex prompts. There is a need for a practical, scalable, and effective way to remove or neutralize knowledge of specific data from already-trained models, ideally without full retraining and with minimal performance degradation on unrelated tasks.
AI.method: The authors introduce Divergence Decoding (DD), which augments a frozen base probabilistic model (e.g., an LLM) with small auxiliary models that modify the output logits during inference. These auxiliary models are trained using standard pre-training and fine-tuning setups so that, at decoding time, they push the output distribution away from sequences associated with the targeted data to be unlearned. DD effectively reweights or steers token probabilities so that memorized sensitive examples become unlikely continuations. The paper also shows that the adjusted, steered output distribution can be distilled back into the base model parameters so that a standalone, updated model exhibits the unlearned behavior without needing the auxiliary models at inference. The authors further adapt the idea to non-text modalities by applying a similar steering mechanism to image models.
AI.result: On standard unlearning benchmarks and across various model sizes and training dataset scales, Divergence Decoding outperforms state-of-the-art unlearning baselines. It achieves stronger removal of targeted information while preserving general model utility, and is described as both effective and inexpensive compared with alternatives that require heavy retraining or cause severe performance drops. The authors show that distillation of the steered distribution back into the base model succeeds, suggesting DD can be used not just as an inference-time patch but as a way to permanently modify the model. Initial experiments in image generation or classification indicate that the approach is not limited to language models and can improve unlearning performance in vision settings as well.
AI.conclusion: Divergence Decoding is presented as a generally applicable, low-cost framework for machine unlearning that works by steering model outputs away from specific data via small auxiliary models. It consistently beats existing unlearning methods in benchmarks while avoiding catastrophic utility loss, and its behavior can be distilled into the base model to create a self-contained, unlearned model checkpoint. The technique’s applicability to other probabilistic models, including image models, suggests it may serve as a versatile tool for privacy- and copyright-preserving model deployment across modalities.

title: Reinforcement Learning Amplifies Emergent Misalignment from Harmless Rewards
summary: Emergent misalignment (EM) is the surprising tendency of language models to become broadly misaligned after fine-tuning on narrowly misaligned examples.
