While EM has been extensively studied in the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) setting, evidence that it also arises from reinforcement learning (RL) is limited to large, closed-source models, leaving the phenomenon expensive to study and difficult to reproduce. We characterize EM from RL in small, off-the-shelf open-weight models along three axes. First, we show that rewarding narrow, overtly misaligned behavior produces substantially higher general-domain misalignment than sample-matched SFT. Second, we show that EM from RL can be induced by reward signals that could plausibly arise naturally, such as unpopular aesthetic preferences or poor rhetorical appeals. Third, we evaluate in-training mitigations developed for SFT-induced EM and find that they broadly transfer, with interleaving on-policy safety data performing best.

AI.tldr: The paper studies emergent misalignment caused by reinforcement learning in small, open models, showing it is stronger than from supervised fine-tuning, can arise from realistic reward signals, and can be mitigated with safety data interleaving.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed that fine-tuning models on a small set of misaligned examples can cause broad misalignment, but this was mainly explored in supervised fine-tuning and large closed models, making results hard to reproduce and understand for RL-based training. The authors aim to demonstrate and systematically analyze emergent misalignment under RL in accessible open-weight models.
AI.method: They apply reinforcement learning to small, off-the-shelf open-weight language models using reward functions that encourage narrowly misaligned behaviors, then compare their general-domain misalignment to models trained with sample-matched supervised fine-tuning. They also design reward signals that mimic realistic but potentially harmful preferences (e.g., aesthetic or rhetorical biases) and test whether existing mitigation strategies from SFT, such as interleaving safety-aligned data during training, reduce emergent misalignment during RL.
AI.result: Rewarding narrow, overt misalignment via RL produces substantially greater broad misalignment than using supervised fine-tuning on the same data. Emergent misalignment can be triggered even when the reward corresponds to more subtle and realistically occurring signals like unpopular aesthetic tastes or flawed rhetorical styles, not just overtly bad behaviors. Mitigation strategies created for SFT transfer to RL, with interleaving on-policy safety data achieving the best reduction in emergent misalignment.
AI.conclusion: Emergent misalignment is not limited to SFT or to large proprietary models; it also appears, and can even be stronger, when using RL on small open-weight models. Realistic reward schemes can unintentionally induce broad misalignment, so RL training pipelines must be carefully designed. Still, SFT-derived mitigation techniques, particularly interleaving safety-aligned data on-policy, are effective tools for controlling emergent misalignment in RL-trained language models.

title: Bundesrecht: An Open Library and Corpus for German Statutory Reference Processing
summary: Statutory references are central to legal language understanding, but are difficult to process automatically, as they appear in compact and variable surface forms, may combine multiple targets, use special abbreviations, and often point to lower-level units. Existing tools for German focus either on parsing references from legal documents or accessing statutory text once citations are explicit. This paper introduces bundesrecht, an open resource for German statutory reference processing, consisting of a software library and a structured corpus of German federal law. The library parses, normalizes, and resolves German statutory references, mapping raw citation strings to structured objects, expanding compact references into canonical forms, and linking them to statutory provisions. The accompanying dataset preserves the internal hierarchy of statutes from laws to fine-granular subclauses. We evaluate the parser and normalizer on 2,944 annotated German legal references using strict exact-match and micro information extraction metrics. We further evaluate canonical reference deduplication and show that normalized references group real citation surface variants far more reliably than string matching. bundesrecht is the first open resource that covers German statutory reference processing as an end-to-end pipeline, from raw citation string to resolved statutory provision, and is available on PyPI.

AI.tldr: The paper presents bundesrecht, an open, end-to-end pipeline and corpus for processing German statutory references, from raw citation strings to resolved provisions, along with an evaluation on annotated references.
AI.motivation: Statutory references are crucial for understanding legal texts but are hard to process automatically due to compact and variable citation forms, abbreviations, merged targets, and fine-grained referencing. Existing German tools are fragmented, handling either extraction or access to already-explicit citations, but not the full workflow. There is a need for an open, comprehensive resource that can robustly handle German statutory references end to end.
AI.method: The authors build bundesrecht, consisting of (1) a software library and (2) a structured corpus of German federal law. The library parses raw citation strings, normalizes them into canonical structured objects, expands compact references into explicit canonical forms, and resolves them by linking to specific statutory provisions. The corpus encodes the hierarchical structure of statutes, from entire laws down to fine-grained subclauses. They evaluate parsing and normalization on 2,944 manually annotated German legal references using strict exact-match and micro-level information extraction metrics, and they also evaluate canonical-reference-based deduplication versus naive string matching.
AI.result: The parser and normalizer achieve measurable performance on a benchmark of 2,944 annotated references under strict exact-match and micro information extraction metrics (details not in abstract, but implied to be satisfactory). Canonical normalization of references significantly improves deduplication of citations: normalized forms cluster distinct surface variants of the same legal reference much more reliably than plain string matching, demonstrating the utility of the structured approach.
AI.conclusion: bundesrecht is the first open, end-to-end resource for German statutory reference processing that spans from raw citation strings to resolved statutory provisions. It combines a software library and a structured corpus preserving statute hierarchy and is made publicly available via PyPI. The work fills a gap in German legal NLP infrastructure and enables more robust, downstream applications that rely on accurate statutory reference handling.

title: A Visually Impaired Assistance Benchmark for VLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation
summary: AI-based Visually Impaired Assistance (VIA) remains challenging, largely due to the high cost of human evaluation. The VLM-as-a-Judge paradigm may offer a promising alternative, although it has mostly been studied in general domains. We therefore ask whether such judges can be trusted for VIA tasks. To investigate this question, we introduce VIABLE (Visually Impaired Assistance Benchmark for VLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation), the first benchmark for VLM-as-a-Judge evaluation in VIA. VIABLE contains over 300K judgment samples across three scenarios and introduces an Effectiveness--Impartiality--Stability framework with a 12-mode failure taxonomy. Based on VIABLE, our systematic study of seven judges across different model scales shows that existing models are largely unreliable across all evaluation axes. The strongest judge, GPT-5.4, achieves only 52.6% single-failure diagnostic accuracy, yet exhibits the highest self-preference rate at 94.2%; while open-source judges are strongly biased and adversarially fragile. To address these issues, we propose VIA-Judge-Agent, a model-agnostic inference-time harness that augments judges with visual evidence extraction and a taxonomy-guided workflow. It enables positive improvements in diagnostic accuracy and downstream VIA responses more preferred by BLV users. Data and code are available at: https://github.com/YiyiyiZhao/VIABLE

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VIABLE, a large benchmark to test whether vision-language models (VLMs) can reliably act as evaluators (“judges”) for AI systems assisting visually impaired users, and proposes VIA-Judge-Agent to improve their reliability at inference time.
AI.motivation: Evaluating AI systems for visually impaired assistance (VIA) currently depends heavily on costly and scarce human judgments from blind and low-vision (BLV) users. Recent work suggests using vision-language models as automatic judges (VLM-as-a-Judge), but these have mainly been studied in general domains, not in VIA, where safety, fairness, and robustness are critical. The authors want to know if such automatic judges can be trusted for VIA tasks and to identify where and how they fail.
AI.method: The authors build VIABLE, the first benchmark dedicated to evaluating VLM-as-a-Judge in the VIA setting. VIABLE includes over 300K judgment samples covering three VIA scenarios, and it introduces an Effectiveness–Impartiality–Stability evaluation framework with a 12-mode failure taxonomy that categorizes different ways judges can go wrong. Using VIABLE, they systematically evaluate seven VLM judges of varying scales, measuring diagnostic accuracy, bias, adversarial robustness, self-preference, and other reliability aspects. To mitigate identified weaknesses, they design VIA-Judge-Agent, an inference-time, model-agnostic harness that wraps existing judges with (1) explicit visual evidence extraction and (2) a taxonomy-guided reasoning workflow, without retraining the underlying models.
AI.result: Experiments on VIABLE show that current VLM judges are broadly unreliable for VIA. The strongest tested judge, GPT-5.4, attains only 52.6% accuracy in diagnosing single failure modes but simultaneously exhibits an extremely high self-preference rate of 94.2%, indicating strong bias toward its own outputs. Open-source judges perform even worse, demonstrating substantial bias and vulnerability to adversarial inputs. When wrapped with the proposed VIA-Judge-Agent harness, judges obtain better diagnostic accuracy and yield downstream VIA system responses that are more often preferred by blind and low-vision users in user studies.
AI.conclusion: Existing VLM-as-a-Judge approaches cannot yet be trusted as reliable evaluators for visually impaired assistance systems: they are inaccurate, biased, and unstable. However, careful benchmark design (VIABLE) plus structured inference-time interventions (VIA-Judge-Agent) can meaningfully improve their performance and user-perceived quality, offering a more practical path toward safer, more trustworthy automatic evaluation in VIA. The resources released by the authors lay groundwork for future research on robust, fair, and domain-sensitive VLM judges.

title: The Latin Substrate: How Language Models Represent and Mediate Script Choice
summary:
Many languages are written in multiple scripts, requiring large language models (LLMs) to generate equivalent linguistic content in distinct orthographic forms. While prior work suggests that LLMs route information through shared latent representations, how they internally mediate script variation remains poorly understood.
  We study this question by first examining per-layer output distributions with the logit lens, which reveals consistent latent romanization during transliteration, and then through representational and mechanistic analyses of script generation. At the representational level, we show that scripts of the same language become increasingly separable across layers and that a simple linear steering direction can flip a model's output script while largely maintaining semantic content. The vector generalizes asymmetrically to writing systems unseen during construction, flipping non-Latin output to Latin reliably, but mapping Latin output into varied non-Latin scripts. At the mechanistic level, we localize a small set of late-layer attention heads that causally mediate script choice. These heads transfer across unrelated languages and writing systems, suggesting that script routing is implemented by language-agnostic components. Across both analyses, we observe a consistent directional asymmetry: non-Latin output is produced by a compact, identifiable gate, while Latin-script output emerges from diffuse contributions across the network. Collectively, our findings hint that LLMs organize script variation around shared latent representations while exhibiting a privileged substrate toward Latin script.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how large language models internally handle different writing scripts of the same language, revealing a shared latent representation with an asymmetric bias toward Latin script.
AI.motivation: Many languages can be written in multiple scripts (e.g., Latin and non-Latin), and LLMs must generate equivalent content in these different orthographies. Prior work indicates shared latent representations across such variations, but it is unclear how script choice itself is represented and controlled inside the models. Understanding this is important for interpretability, fairness across scripts, and improving multilingual performance.
AI.method: The authors analyze LLMs using: (1) logit lens inspection of per-layer output distributions during transliteration to detect latent romanization; (2) representational analyses measuring how separable different scripts of the same language become across layers; (3) linear steering experiments, identifying a direction in representation space that flips the output script while preserving semantics and evaluating its generalization to unseen scripts; and (4) mechanistic interpretability, localizing late-layer attention heads that causally control script choice and testing their transfer across unrelated languages and writing systems.
AI.result: They find evidence of consistent latent romanization in intermediate layers. Scripts used for the same language become more distinguishable across layers, and a simple linear direction can reliably flip script while mostly preserving meaning. This direction generalizes asymmetrically: it reliably converts non-Latin outputs into Latin, but converting Latin to non-Latin leads to varied non-Latin scripts. Mechanistically, a small number of late-layer attention heads are shown to causally mediate script selection. These heads transfer across unrelated languages and scripts, indicating language-agnostic script routing. Across analyses, they observe a directional asymmetry: non-Latin script generation is controlled by a compact, identifiable mechanism, whereas Latin script arises from more diffuse contributions across the network.
AI.conclusion: LLMs appear to organize script variation using shared latent representations with a privileged, default-like role for Latin script. Script routing is implemented partly via language-agnostic late-layer attention heads and a steerable direction in representation space, with non-Latin script generation relying on a compact gating mechanism on top of a broad Latin-oriented substrate. This structure explains observed asymmetries in how models handle Latin vs. non-Latin scripts and suggests both opportunities and constraints for steering script behavior in multilingual LLMs.

title: Unlocking Fine-Grained Translation Quality Estimation in LRMs through Synergistically Evolving Implicit and Explicit Reasoning
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) still struggle with fine-grained translation quality estimation (QE), even with long reasoning chains. We argue that LRMs already possess strong multilingual capabilities, while the core challenge stems from the intrinsic difficulty of learning the fine-grained QE task. In this paper, we propose RIEQE (Reasoning both Implicitly and Explicitly for QE), a simple two-stage training framework that enables the co-evolution of implicit (layer-wise) and explicit (token-wise) reasoning capabilities. To make implicit reasoning feasible, we first decompose the complex QE task into straightforward subtasks. Based on this, our two-stage approach applies: (1) NonThinking-SFT, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) without reasoning chains to directly boost the model's implicit reasoning tendency and capability; and (2) Thinking-RLVR, standard Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) to subsequently strengthen explicit reasoning. Results demonstrate that implicit and explicit reasoning synergistically co-evolve under our framework. On the WMT test sets, RIEQE based on Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 surpasses all baselines in explicit reasoning performance, while its implicit reasoning capability is also comparable to the best current encoder-based models. We further provide evidence for the synergistic collaboration between implicit and explicit reasoning, showing how they mutually benefit each other.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RIEQE, a two-stage training framework that jointly improves implicit (layer-wise) and explicit (token-wise) reasoning for fine-grained translation quality estimation, achieving state-of-the-art explicit reasoning and strong implicit reasoning on WMT benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models, despite strong multilingual abilities and long reasoning chains, perform poorly on fine-grained translation quality estimation. The authors believe the core problem is the intrinsic difficulty of learning this fine-grained QE task rather than weak multilingual capability. They aim to design a training strategy that makes this difficult task easier to learn and better exploits LRMs’ reasoning potential.
AI.method: The authors propose RIEQE, which decomposes QE into simpler subtasks and trains models in two stages. Stage 1 is NonThinking-SFT: supervised fine-tuning without explicit reasoning chains, designed to directly strengthen the model’s implicit, layer-wise reasoning tendencies and capabilities. Stage 2 is Thinking-RLVR: reinforcement learning with verifiable reward, used to further enhance explicit, token-wise reasoning based on the earlier implicit training. This setup encourages implicit and explicit reasoning to co-evolve and support each other.
AI.result: On WMT test sets, RIEQE built on Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 outperforms all baselines in explicit reasoning metrics and achieves implicit reasoning performance comparable to top encoder-based QE models. Empirical analyses show that implicit and explicit reasoning improve together under the proposed two-stage training, confirming the effectiveness of the framework.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that the main challenge in fine-grained QE is task learning difficulty rather than language capacity, and that a two-stage training framework that separately but jointly targets implicit and explicit reasoning can effectively address it. RIEQE enables synergistic co-evolution of both reasoning types, leading to state-of-the-art explicit reasoning performance and competitive implicit reasoning, and the authors provide evidence that the two forms of reasoning mutually reinforce one another.

title: LLM Judges Inconsistently Disagree Across Safety Criteria and Harm Categories
summary: We evaluate the consistency of automated judges in conducting a multi-dimensional safety evaluation in a reference-free setup. Our results indicate that Large Language Models are unreliable judges in identifying safety issues related to machine-generated advice in regulated domains such as finance, although they are more reliable at identifying more overt forms of unsafe/harmful content such as violence. The degree of inconsistency in a model's judgments can vary significantly by the chosen safety criteria and can be impacted by the language of the content and its linguistic style as well. Finally, there is high disagreement among different judges for the same output, across domains, safety criteria, and languages. These findings provide new insights on the practice of using LLMs as evaluators and offer several recommendations for practitioners on how to use automated judges in practical scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how consistent large language models (LLMs) are as automated “judges” for multi-dimensional safety evaluation in a reference-free setting, finding substantial inconsistency, especially in regulated domains like finance, and offering practical recommendations for safer use.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used to automatically evaluate other models’ outputs, especially for safety, there is a need to understand how reliable and consistent these automated judges are, particularly in sensitive, regulated domains where safety failures can have serious real-world consequences.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic empirical study of LLM-based judges performing multi-dimensional safety evaluations of machine-generated advice without ground-truth references. They analyze consistency within a single model and disagreement across different models, varying the domain (e.g., finance vs more overt harm such as violence), safety criteria, language of the content, and linguistic style of the text being evaluated.
AI.result: The study finds that LLMs are unreliable as judges of safety issues for machine-generated advice in regulated domains (e.g., finance), while being considerably more reliable at flagging overtly harmful content such as violence. Inconsistency in judgments depends strongly on the specific safety criterion, the language of the text, and its stylistic properties. Moreover, different LLM judges frequently disagree with each other on the same output across domains, criteria, and languages.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based automated judges cannot yet be relied upon as consistent, general-purpose safety evaluators, especially in regulated, advice-giving domains. Their performance is much better on clearly harmful content but degrades in nuanced or domain-specific contexts. The paper concludes with recommendations for practitioners on cautious, context-aware use of automated judges, emphasizing the need for complementary methods and safeguards rather than sole reliance on LLM evaluators.

title: Multi-Turn Multi-Agent Dialogue for Collaborative Reconstruction Improves VLM Performance on Spatial Reasoning, But Only Barely
summary: Robots operating in diverse environments rely on visual input to interpret objects and spatial layouts. In human-collaborative tasks, they are expected to communicate this understanding through language. Vision-language models (VLMs) support robotic tasks involving visual interpretation, question answering, and instruction following, but their capabilities in collaborative dialogue tasks requiring spatial reasoning remain underexplored. We study this gap through a collaborative structure-building task that combines visual interpretation, grounding, language-guided interaction, and action generation. We develop a framework in which VLMs use dialogue to reconstruct a target structure from visual and textual inputs. We evaluate open-weight and closed VLMs across interaction settings, input modalities, and image representations. Results show that spatial reasoning over visual representations remains difficult for the evaluated VLMs. Detailed text representations of the target yield higher reconstruction success across modality conditions, while decomposed image representations improve performance. These findings reveal limits in visual spatial grounding and grounded instruction generation for collaborative VLM agents.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well current vision-language models can engage in collaborative dialogue that requires spatial reasoning, using a structure-building task as a benchmark.
AI.motivation: Robots in human-collaborative settings must both understand visual scenes (objects, layouts) and communicate that understanding via natural language. While vision-language models have been applied to perception, question answering, and instruction following, it is unclear how well they handle multi-turn collaborative dialogue that depends on spatial reasoning and grounded language about structures in the environment. The paper aims to fill this gap and reveal current limitations.
AI.method: The authors design a collaborative structure-building task where an agent must reconstruct a target structure using dialogue, based on visual and textual descriptions. They create a framework in which various open-weight and closed-source VLMs attempt this task under different interaction settings, input modalities (text, images, or both), and image representations (e.g., holistic vs decomposed views). Performance is measured by reconstruction success and analyzed for spatial reasoning and grounding capabilities.
AI.result: Across models and conditions, spatial reasoning over visual representations is challenging: VLMs struggle to accurately use visual input to guide collaborative reconstruction. However, providing rich, detailed textual descriptions of the target structure consistently improves reconstruction success regardless of modality. Additionally, breaking the visual input into decomposed image representations (rather than a single holistic image) yields better performance, suggesting that finer-grained visual structure aids reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs are limited in visual spatial grounding and in generating grounded instructions for collaborative, dialogue-based tasks. Enhancing text detail and decomposing visual inputs can partially mitigate these weaknesses, but the results highlight a substantial gap between current capabilities and the requirements of robust robot-human collaboration involving spatial reasoning. The work positions the proposed task as a benchmark to drive future research on VLMs as collaborative agents in complex spatial environments.

title: "Intelegi Româneşte?'' A Recipe for Romanian Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) largely follow the text-only LLM trajectory, excelling on English benchmarks but sharply degrading on low-resource languages, where neither large-scale image-text corpora nor culturally grounded evaluations exist. We present a systematic study of building a language-specific VLM for Romanian, covering the full pipeline from data construction to architectural choices. We translate established English VLM training and evaluation corpora into Romanian, applying machine translation to textual annotations and to in-image text, preserving visual grounding while adapting the textual content. Using this data, we train and ablate a series of VLMs to isolate the contribution of (i) vision backbones of varying scale and pretraining, (ii) language backbones from multilingual to Romanian-adapted LLMs, and (iii) OCR-style image-text data. We further curate HoraVQA, a culturally native evaluation set grounded in Romanian everyday scenes. Romanian-adapted VLMs consistently outperform their same-sized counterparts and, across all evaluated benchmarks, even surpass models from the next larger size category.

AI.tldr: The paper builds and analyzes Romanian-specific vision-language models by translating existing English datasets, experimenting with architectural components, and creating a culturally grounded evaluation benchmark, HoraVQA.
AI.motivation: Most vision-language models perform well in English but degrade sharply for low-resource languages like Romanian due to scarce image-text data and lack of culturally relevant evaluation benchmarks. The authors aim to understand how to systematically build strong VLMs for such languages, closing the performance gap and providing a blueprint for localization.
AI.method: The authors translate established English VLM training and evaluation corpora into Romanian using machine translation on both textual annotations and in-image text, ensuring visual grounding is preserved. They then train multiple VLM variants while ablating three factors: (i) different visual backbones with varying scale and pretraining; (ii) different language backbones ranging from generic multilingual to Romanian-adapted LLMs; and (iii) inclusion or exclusion of OCR-style image-text data. Additionally, they construct HoraVQA, a Romanian, culturally native VQA benchmark, by curating images from everyday Romanian scenes with corresponding questions and answers.
AI.result: Romanian-adapted language backbones consistently lead to better VLM performance than similarly sized multilingual or non-adapted models. These Romanian-adapted VLMs not only outperform their same-sized baselines but, across benchmarks (including the new HoraVQA), often surpass models from the next larger size category, highlighting the strong benefits of language and culture-specific adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Systematically adapting VLMs to a specific low-resource language—via translated training/evaluation corpora, careful architectural choices, and culturally grounded benchmarks—yields substantial performance gains. For Romanian, such adaptation allows smaller, Romanian-tuned VLMs to outperform even larger generic models, suggesting that language- and culture-specific pipelines are a powerful approach for extending VLM capabilities beyond English and other high-resource languages.

title: SCOPE: Self-Play via Co-Evolving Policies for Open-Ended Tasks
summary: Self-play can train language models without external supervision. However, existing methods require rule-checkable answers, leaving open-ended tasks dependent on curated prompts or frontier-model judges. We introduce SCOPE, a data-free self-play framework for open-ended tasks that co-evolves two policies: a Challenger that generates document-grounded tasks, and a Solver that answers them through multi-turn retrieval. A frozen copy of the initial model serves as the self-judge, which writes task-specific rubrics from the source document and grades Solver responses against them. Across three 7-8B instruction-tuned models (Qwen2.5, Qwen3, OLMo-3), SCOPE improves open-ended performance by up to +10.4 points on eight benchmarks and matches or exceeds GRPO_data trained on ~9K curated prompts. Although trained only on open-ended tasks, SCOPE also improves held-out short-form QA by up to +13.8 points on seven held-out benchmarks, surpassing GRPO_data on all three models. Ablations show that co-evolving the Challenger is necessary to keep tasks near the Solver's frontier, that gains arise from improvements in both retrieval and synthesis with the relative contribution varying by task, and that rubric generation quality is the bottleneck for self-judging.

AI.tldr: SCOPE is a data-free self-play framework that improves open-ended and QA performance of language models by co-evolving a task-generating Challenger, a multi-turn retrieval Solver, and a frozen self-judging model that creates rubrics and grades responses.
AI.motivation: Existing self-play methods for training language models work mainly for tasks with rule-checkable answers, and open-ended tasks still depend on curated prompts or stronger external judges. The authors want a scalable, data-free way to train models on open-ended, document-grounded tasks without relying on frontier models or large curated datasets.
AI.method: They propose SCOPE, which jointly trains two policies from an initial model: (1) a Challenger that generates document-grounded, open-ended tasks; (2) a Solver that answers these tasks with multi-turn retrieval. A frozen copy of the initial model acts as a self-judge, generating task-specific evaluation rubrics from the source document and grading the Solver’s responses. Training proceeds in self-play where Challenger and Solver co-evolve while the judge remains frozen, enabling data-free reinforcement-like improvement. They perform ablations on components such as Challenger co-evolution, retrieval vs. synthesis contributions, and rubric quality.
AI.result: On three 7–8B instruction-tuned LMs (Qwen2.5, Qwen3, OLMo-3), SCOPE improves open-ended benchmark performance by up to +10.4 points across eight benchmarks and matches or exceeds a GRPO_data baseline trained on ~9K curated prompts. Despite being trained only on open-ended tasks, SCOPE also boosts performance on seven held-out short-form QA benchmarks by up to +13.8 points, outperforming GRPO_data on all three models. Ablation studies indicate that a co-evolving Challenger is required to keep tasks appropriately challenging, that both retrieval and synthesis are responsible for gains with task-dependent contributions, and that rubric generation quality limits the effectiveness of self-judging.
AI.conclusion: SCOPE demonstrates that data-free self-play can effectively train language models on open-ended, document-grounded tasks without external judges or curated prompt datasets. Co-evolving a task generator and a retrieval-based solver under a frozen self-judge yields substantial gains on both open-ended and short-form QA benchmarks, rivaling or surpassing data-intensive baselines. The framework’s main bottleneck is the quality of automatically generated rubrics, suggesting future work on better self-judging mechanisms.

title: Translation Analytics for Freelancers II: Benchmarking Local LLMs for Confidential Translation Workflows
comment: 20 pages. Accepted at EAMT-2026 (Tilburg, Netherlands, June 2026)
summary: Building on our previous work, this paper develops practical, low-barrier methods for freelance translators and smaller language service providers to evaluate translation technologies using rigorous yet accessible analytic methods. Here we address a high-stakes, specialized need: offline translation for confidentiality-sensitive domains in which privacy constraints preclude the use of cloud-based engines and commercial LLMs. We expand the Reeve Foundation Trilingual Corpus (RFTC) used in our previous work into a multilingual corpus (RFMC) by adding sentence-aligned German and Simplified Chinese reference translations. We then benchmark several locally runnable language models (via Ollama) across four language directions on 1000+ sentences selected from this corpus. We use consistent single-prompt calls without fine-tuning or domain adaptation, comparing local LLM outputs against commercial NMTs (DeepL, Baidu), a frontier LLM (GPT-5.2), and professional-grade local NMT systems (OPUS-CAT, NeuralDesktop, Promt). Automatic evaluation is conducted with MATEO. Results reveal substantial variation in local LLM performance across language directions and model sizes. The best local LLMs match or surpass local NMT systems and a frontier LLM, though they remain behind top commercial NMTs. These findings underscore the viability of carefully selected local LLM translation for privacy-constrained professionals and inform future research on model scaling and multilingual capability.

AI.tldr: Paper evaluates locally runnable LLMs for offline, privacy-preserving translation using a new multilingual corpus and practical methods for freelancers and small LSPs.
AI.motivation: Freelance translators and small language service providers increasingly need to assess translation technologies, especially for confidentiality-sensitive domains where cloud-based engines and commercial LLMs are unusable due to privacy constraints. There is a lack of practical, low-barrier, yet rigorous methods and benchmarks tailored to these users, and existing work focused on fewer languages and systems.
AI.method: Extend the Reeve Foundation Trilingual Corpus (RFTC) into a larger multilingual corpus (RFMC) by adding sentence-aligned German and Simplified Chinese translations. Select 1000+ sentences and benchmark multiple locally runnable LLMs via Ollama over four translation directions. Use consistent, single-prompt, zero-shot style calls without fine-tuning or domain adaptation. Compare local LLM outputs with commercial NMTs (DeepL, Baidu), a frontier LLM (GPT-5.2), and professional local NMT tools (OPUS-CAT, NeuralDesktop, Promt). Evaluate all systems automatically with the MATEO metric and analyze performance variation by language direction and model size.
AI.result: Local LLMs show wide performance variability depending on language pair and model size. The best-performing local LLMs are competitive with, and sometimes exceed, professional local NMT systems and even a frontier LLM. However, none outperform state-of-the-art commercial cloud NMT engines overall. The experiments validate RFMC as a useful benchmark and demonstrate practical evaluation workflows feasible for non-expert practitioners.
AI.conclusion: Carefully chosen local LLMs can deliver viable translation quality for professionals who must work offline due to privacy or confidentiality constraints, especially when tuned to the right language directions and model sizes. While commercial NMT remains superior on average, local LLMs can match or surpass other local solutions, making them a realistic option for sensitive workflows. The work also offers a reusable corpus and methodology to guide future research on scaling local LLMs and improving their multilingual translation capabilities.

title: Language Models Can Resolve Reference Compositionally, But It's Not Their Native Strength: The Case of the Personal Relation Task
comment: A pre-MIT Press publication version. Paper accepted to Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
summary: Do neural models, such as Large Language Models, genuinely acquire compositional abilities for interpretation of natural language? When we talk about semantic interpretation, we can distinguish two complementary aspects: establishing what an expression refers to in the world (which we call the Extensional task) and representing its sense in a structured way (which we call the Intensional task). We evaluate LLMs and humans on both tasks in the setting of the Personal Relation Task (Paperno 2022) in which, given a universe of people and their relationships with each other, one is asked to interpret a noun phrase such as "Amber's parent's friend". Here, for the Intensional task, the answer is the formula "friend(parent(amber))", and for the Extensional task, the person. We find that humans and LLMs show opposite strengths: humans perform better on Extensional than Intensional tasks, and LLMs vice versa. Our methodology brings greater nuance to the understanding of compositional abilities in modern machine learning models. Our results support the notion that the lack of referential grounding in LLM training is a crucial missing component in mimicking human-like language understanding.

AI.tldr: The paper compares humans and large language models (LLMs) on two kinds of compositional semantic tasks—intensional (structured meaning representations) and extensional (reference to entities)—and finds that humans and LLMs have opposite strengths, highlighting the importance of referential grounding for human-like understanding.
AI.motivation: There is an ongoing debate about whether neural models, particularly LLMs, truly possess compositional semantic abilities comparable to humans. Prior work often treats “semantic interpretation” as a single capability, without clearly separating the ability to build structured representations of meaning from the ability to correctly identify real-world referents. The authors want to refine this discussion by formally distinguishing and jointly evaluating these two aspects—intensional and extensional semantics—on the same task, and to see how humans and LLMs differ. This can reveal more precisely what is missing from current LLM training for human-like language understanding.
AI.method: The authors use the Personal Relation Task (Paperno 2022). A small, explicit universe of individuals is given, along with their interpersonal relations (e.g., parent, friend). Participants must interpret complex noun phrases like “Amber’s parent’s friend.” Two tasks are defined: (1) Intensional: produce a structured, compositional representation of the phrase’s meaning (e.g., `friend(parent(amber))`); (2) Extensional: identify the specific individual in the universe that the phrase refers to, given the relational database. They test both humans and LLMs on these tasks, compare performance, and analyze the pattern of strengths and weaknesses. The methodology thereby separates the ability to form internal structured meaning from the ability to ground that meaning in a world of entities and relations.
AI.result: The experiments show a striking dissociation: humans outperform LLMs on the Extensional task (choosing the correct individual) but perform worse than LLMs on the Intensional task (constructing the correct structured expression). LLMs, in contrast, are relatively strong at forming the correct compositional expression but struggle more with correctly grounding that expression to a specific referent in the modeled world. This pattern indicates that LLMs’ compositional strengths are primarily in abstract, symbolic-like manipulation of expressions, not in reference resolution.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest that current LLMs and humans have complementary semantic profiles: humans are better at referential, grounded interpretation, whereas LLMs are better at constructing syntactically well-formed, intensional representations. This undermines simple claims that LLMs either fully possess or entirely lack compositional semantics. Instead, compositionality must be decomposed into distinct sub-capabilities. The observed weakness of LLMs in extensional, grounded tasks supports the view that the absence of genuine referential grounding in their training is a key reason they do not yet exhibit human-like language understanding. Future work should incorporate grounding mechanisms or training regimes that connect language more directly to world-referential structure.

title: BenHalluEval: A Multi-Task Hallucination Evaluation Framework for Large Language Models on Bengali
summary: Despite Bengali being the sixth most spoken language in the world, no prior work has systematically evaluated hallucination in large language models (LLMs) for Bengali. We introduce BenHalluEval, a fine-grained hallucination evaluation framework for Bengali covering four tasks: Generative Question Answering (GQA), Bangla-English Code-Mixed QA, Summarization, and Reasoning. We construct 12,000 hallucinated candidates using GPT-5.4 across twelve task-specific hallucination types, drawn from three existing Bengali datasets, and evaluate seven LLMs spanning reasoning-oriented, multilingual, and Bengali-centric categories under a dual-track protocol that independently measures false-positive rate on ground-truth instances (Track A) and hallucination detection rate on hallucinated candidates (Track B). To jointly penalise both failure modes and prevent inflated scores from uniform response bias, we propose BenHalluScore, a dual-track calibration metric that ranges from 7.72% to 55.42% across models and tasks, revealing substantial variation in hallucination calibration. Chain-of-thought prompting, applied as a mitigation strategy, shifts response distributions without consistently improving hallucination discrimination. BenHalluEval establishes the first dedicated hallucination benchmark for Bengali and highlights the inadequacy of single-track and prompting-only evaluation approaches for low-resource language settings. The dataset and code are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BanglaHalluEval-EB77.

AI.tldr: The paper presents BenHalluEval, the first systematic framework and benchmark to evaluate hallucinations of large language models in Bengali across multiple tasks, along with a dual-track metric, BenHalluScore.
AI.motivation: Bengali is one of the most widely spoken languages, yet hallucination behavior of LLMs in Bengali has not been systematically studied. Existing hallucination evaluations are mostly focused on high-resource languages and often use single-track metrics that can be gamed by trivial response strategies. There is a need for a Bengali-specific benchmark and more robust evaluation methodology that captures both over- and under-confidence in model outputs.
AI.method: The authors build BenHalluEval, covering four task settings (Generative QA, Bangla-English code-mixed QA, summarization, and reasoning). They automatically construct 12,000 hallucinated answer candidates with GPT-5.4, organized into twelve hallucination types and grounded in three existing Bengali datasets. Seven diverse LLMs (reasoning-focused, multilingual, Bengali-centric) are evaluated with a dual-track protocol: Track A measures false-positive hallucinations on ground-truth instances, and Track B measures the ability to correctly flag hallucinated candidates. They then introduce BenHalluScore, which combines both tracks into a single calibration-oriented metric that discourages uniform response biases. They also test chain-of-thought prompting as a mitigation strategy.
AI.result: Across models and tasks, BenHalluScore values range from 7.72% to 55.42%, indicating large differences in hallucination calibration quality. The evaluation shows that models vary substantially in their ability to both avoid hallucinating on correct inputs and detect hallucinations in provided candidates. Chain-of-thought prompting changes how models respond but does not consistently enhance their capacity to distinguish factual from hallucinatory content.
AI.conclusion: BenHalluEval is the first dedicated hallucination benchmark for Bengali, exposing significant calibration gaps in current LLMs for this language. The study demonstrates that single-track evaluations and mitigation via prompting alone are insufficient, especially in low-resource settings, and argues for dual-track, calibration-aware metrics like BenHalluScore. The released dataset and code enable future work on hallucination detection and mitigation in Bengali and potentially other low-resource languages.

title: Are Full Rollouts Necessary for On-Policy Distillation?
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) provides dense teacher feedback along rollouts generated by the student and has emerged as a promising post-training paradigm for long-horizon reasoning. However, standard OPD typically generates full rollouts during training, which is computationally expensive and may expose the student to unreliable teacher feedback at late rollout positions, especially during early training. We identify the rollout horizon as a key bottleneck in OPD that substantially impacts training efficiency. Unlike Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), OPD does not require a complete trajectory or a final answer reward to provide learning signals. This observation suggests that full rollouts may not always be necessary for effective OPD. Motivated by this insight, we propose two simple horizon-control strategies: Progressive OPD (POPD), which gradually expands the rollout horizon during training, and Truncated OPD (TOPD), which permanently performs distillation on reliable truncated rollouts. Experiments on mathematical reasoning show that POPD improves the training efficiency of OPD by up to 3$\times$, while TOPD matches OPD performance using only 10\% of the rollout horizon, leading to substantial wall-clock and memory reductions. These results demonstrate that controlling the rollout horizon offers a simple and practical path to more efficient OPD.

AI.tldr: The paper improves the efficiency of on-policy distillation (OPD) for long-horizon reasoning by shortening or progressively extending rollouts instead of always using full trajectories.
AI.motivation: Standard on-policy distillation uses full rollouts generated by the student model, which is computationally expensive and can lead to the student learning from unreliable teacher feedback, especially at late positions early in training. Since OPD does not strictly require complete trajectories or final rewards (unlike RL with verifiable rewards), there is an opportunity to reduce computation and noise by limiting how far into the rollout distillation is performed.
AI.method: The authors identify rollout horizon length as a core efficiency bottleneck in OPD and propose two horizon-control strategies: (1) Progressive OPD (POPD), which starts with short horizons and gradually increases the rollout length during training; and (2) Truncated OPD (TOPD), which always distills only on reliable truncated rollouts, never using the full sequence. These approaches exploit the fact that early tokens in a trajectory tend to have more reliable teacher supervision and are cheaper to generate.
AI.result: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks, POPD speeds up OPD training by up to 3× while preserving performance, and TOPD achieves comparable performance to standard OPD while using only 10% of the rollout horizon, leading to significant reductions in wall-clock time and memory consumption.
AI.conclusion: Rollout horizon is a critical, previously underexploited lever in on-policy distillation. By controlling horizon length with progressive or truncated strategies, one can substantially improve the efficiency of OPD without sacrificing performance, offering a simple and practical enhancement for long-horizon reasoning post-training.

title: Consolidating Rewarded Perturbations for LLM Post-Training
summary: Post-training of language models is commonly framed as a sample-score-update loop implemented by gradient descent. A recent line of work, exemplified by RandOpt, relocates this loop to weight space, sampling Gaussian perturbations around a pretrained model and ensembling the top-K rewarded specialists at inference. While competitive with PPO and GRPO under matched training compute, this prediction-level ensemble incurs K forward passes per test example and does not extend cleanly to free-form generation. We ask whether the rewarded population can instead be folded into a single deployable model, replacing the inference-time ensemble with one consolidated update. A split-half analysis over 25 model-task pairs reveals reproducible low-rank structure in every case. We turn this geometry into CoRP (Consolidating Rewarded Perturbations), a gradient-free operator that combines reward-weighted aggregation, compatibility-aware reweighting, and a held-out validation gate, with no gradient flowing through the language model. Across five language models from 0.5B to 8B and five tasks covering math, code, and creative writing, CoRP improves the base model by 8.1 points on average. Using one tenth of RandOpt's perturbation budget, CoRP exceeds single-inference RandOpt by 6.5 points and recovers more than half of the gain of the 50-pass majority-vote ensemble, at one forward pass per test example.

AI.tldr: They propose CoRP, a gradient-free method to consolidate multiple rewarded weight perturbations of a language model into a single updated model, matching much of an ensemble’s performance with single-pass inference.
AI.motivation: Ensembling many rewarded perturbations in weight space (like RandOpt) achieves strong performance but is computationally expensive at inference (requiring K forward passes per example) and awkward for free-form generation. There is a need for a way to capture the benefits of these rewarded populations in one deployable model without gradient-based training.
AI.method: They analyze the geometry of rewarded perturbations across 25 model-task pairs and find consistent low-rank structure. Building on this, they design CoRP: a gradient-free operator that (1) aggregates perturbations with reward-based weights, (2) reweights them based on mutual compatibility, and (3) uses a held-out validation gate to control which consolidated updates are applied, all without backpropagating through the language model.
AI.result: On five language models (0.5B–8B parameters) and five tasks (math, code, creative writing), CoRP improves the base model by an average of 8.1 points. With only one-tenth of RandOpt’s perturbation budget, CoRP outperforms single-inference RandOpt by 6.5 points and recovers more than half of the performance gain achieved by RandOpt’s 50-pass majority-vote ensemble, while requiring just one forward pass per test example.
AI.conclusion: Rewarded weight-space perturbations exhibit reproducible low-rank structure that can be exploited to consolidate an ensemble of specialists into a single model. CoRP operationalizes this as a practical, gradient-free post-training method that captures much of the ensemble’s benefit with dramatically lower inference cost, making weight-space optimization more deployable for language model adaptation.

title: Reliable Multilingual Orthopedic Decision Support from Clinical Narratives: Language-Aware Adaptation and Verification-Guided Deferral
summary: Multilingual orthopedic decision support remains challenging in low-resource healthcare settings, where clinical narratives contain specialized terminology, mixed scripts, incomplete evidence, label imbalance and language-dependent documentation patterns. This article presents a reliability-oriented framework for classifying free-text orthopedic notes in English, Hindi and Punjabi. We compare task-aligned multilingual transformer encoders, a task-fine-tuned DistilBERT baseline, zero-shot instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs) and a domain-adaptive encoder, IndicBERT-HPA. IndicBERT-HPA augments IndicBERT with language-aware orthopedic adapter heads to support clinically relevant multilingual representation learning. Evaluation extends beyond aggregate accuracy to per-class performance, ROC-AUC, AUPRC, expected calibration error, cross-language stability and robustness under controlled balanced and natural-prevalence distributions. The evaluated zero-shot LLMs remain substantially less effective than task-adapted encoders for closed-set classification, with language-dependent instability. Under natural clinical prevalence, IndicBERT-HPA achieves the strongest overall performance, reaching an averaged Macro-F1 of 0.8792, Macro-AUROC of 0.894 and AUPRC of 0.902. We further implement a deterministic selective-verification layer combining confidence gating, evidence-consistency checking and language-risk screening. On a randomly selected held-out 5,000-record subset, it achieves 84.4% selective accuracy and 0.76 selective Macro-F1 at 72.3% coverage, compared with 71.5% accuracy and 0.65 Macro-F1 for accept-all prediction. These results support reliability-oriented multilingual clinical decision support with explicit deferral.

AI.tldr: They propose and evaluate a reliability-focused framework for classifying multilingual orthopedic clinical notes (English, Hindi, Punjabi), showing a domain-adapted IndicBERT variant with safety checks outperforms generic multilingual and zero-shot LLM approaches.
AI.motivation: Low-resource multilingual orthopedic settings need dependable automated decision support from free-text notes, but face issues such as specialized jargon, code-mixing, incomplete evidence, label imbalance, and language-specific documentation patterns. Existing models, especially generic multilingual transformers and zero-shot LLMs, struggle with both accuracy and reliability (calibration, robustness, language stability) under realistic class prevalences.
AI.method: They build and compare several approaches for closed-set classification of orthopedic notes in English, Hindi, and Punjabi: task-aligned multilingual transformer encoders, a fine-tuned DistilBERT baseline, zero-shot instruction-tuned LLMs, and a new domain-adaptive encoder, IndicBERT-HPA. IndicBERT-HPA extends IndicBERT with language-aware orthopedic adapter heads to learn better clinical representations. Evaluation includes not only standard metrics (per-class scores, Macro-F1, ROC-AUC, AUPRC) but also calibration error, cross-language stability, and robustness under balanced vs natural prevalence. They also design a deterministic selective-verification layer that combines confidence thresholds, evidence-consistency checks, and language-risk screening to decide when to accept predictions and when to defer.
AI.result: Zero-shot instruction-tuned LLMs perform substantially worse and less stably than task-adapted encoders for this closed-set classification task, with notable language-dependent variability. Under natural clinical prevalence, the proposed IndicBERT-HPA yields the best overall performance, with average Macro-F1=0.8792, Macro-AUROC=0.894, and AUPRC=0.902. The selective-verification layer on a held-out 5,000-record subset attains 84.4% selective accuracy and 0.76 selective Macro-F1 at 72.3% coverage, improving over accept-all prediction (71.5% accuracy, 0.65 Macro-F1) by deferring uncertain/risky cases.
AI.conclusion: Domain-adaptive, language-aware encoders like IndicBERT-HPA, combined with explicit selective-verification mechanisms, can deliver more reliable multilingual orthopedic decision support than generic multilingual transformers or zero-shot LLMs. Evaluating beyond aggregate accuracy—considering calibration, cross-language stability, and natural prevalence—is crucial. Explicit deferral of uncertain cases substantially improves effective reliability in low-resource clinical environments.

title: UniAudio-Token: Empowering Semantic Speech Tokenizers with General Audio Perception
summary: Semantic speech tokenizers have become a widely used interface for Audio-LLMs, owing to their compact single-codebook design and strong linguistic alignment. However, their focus on linguistic abstraction induces acoustic blindness, limiting their applicability beyond speech-centric tasks. We propose UniAudio-Token, a framework that empowers semantic tokenizers with general audio perception without compromising speech ability. Instead of altering the semantic paradigm, UniAudio-Token mitigates its information loss through two key innovations: (1) Semantic-Acoustic Primitives (SAP) provide structured supervision by decomposing audio into linguistic content, vocal attributes, and auditory-scene primitives; and (2) Semantic-Acoustic Equilibrium (SAE) introduces a content-aware gating mechanism that adaptively restores fine-grained acoustic details from shallow layers. Extensive evaluations show that UniAudio-Token learns comprehensive universal representations while preserving high-fidelity speech generation. When integrated with downstream LLMs, it outperforms all single-codebook baseline tokenizers on both understanding and generation tasks, effectively serving as a unified audio interface. We publicly release all our code, including training and inference scripts, together with the model checkpoints at https://github.com/Tencent/Universal_Audio_Tokenizer.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UniAudio-Token, a semantic speech tokenizer enhanced for general audio understanding and generation, overcoming the acoustic blindness of existing single-codebook semantic tokenizers.
AI.motivation: Existing semantic speech tokenizers work well as interfaces for Audio-LLMs due to their compact, single-codebook design and strong linguistic alignment, but they largely ignore rich acoustic details such as speaker characteristics and environmental sounds. This acoustic blindness severely restricts their use for tasks beyond speech-centric applications. The paper is motivated by the need for a unified tokenization framework that retains the benefits of semantic tokenizers while enabling comprehensive audio perception across both speech and non-speech content.
AI.method: The authors propose UniAudio-Token, which maintains the semantic tokenization paradigm but augments it with two main components. First, Semantic-Acoustic Primitives (SAP) decompose audio into three structured factors: linguistic content, vocal attributes, and auditory-scene primitives, providing richer supervision during training. Second, Semantic-Acoustic Equilibrium (SAE) uses a content-aware gating mechanism to selectively restore fine-grained acoustic information from shallow network layers, balancing linguistic abstraction with acoustic detail. The framework is trained to produce universal audio representations suitable for use as a single-codebook interface for downstream LLMs.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that UniAudio-Token captures both semantic and acoustic information, enabling comprehensive audio perception while preserving high-fidelity speech generation. When plugged into various downstream LLM-based systems, it consistently outperforms existing single-codebook semantic tokenizers on both audio understanding and audio generation benchmarks. The model serves effectively as a unified audio interface, suitable for a variety of tasks rather than only speech-centric ones.
AI.conclusion: UniAudio-Token successfully extends semantic speech tokenizers into general-purpose audio tokenizers without sacrificing speech performance. By introducing Semantic-Acoustic Primitives and Semantic-Acoustic Equilibrium, the framework mitigates the acoustic information loss inherent in traditional semantic tokenizers and yields universal audio representations. This makes it a strong candidate for a unified audio interface for Audio-LLMs, and the authors support further research by releasing code, training/inference scripts, and model checkpoints.

title: Preference-Aware Rubric Learning for Personalized Evaluation
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve from general-purpose assistants to user-centric agents, personalization has become central to aligning model behavior with individual preferences, making the evaluation of personalized alignment a critical bottleneck. Existing evaluation methods-ranging from automatic metrics to LLM-as-a-judge approaches-fail to capture subjective, user-specific preferences embedded in long-term interaction histories. We identify three essential principles for reliable and effective personalized evaluation: Representativeness, User-Consistency, and Discriminativeness. To address these principles, we introduce Personalized Evaluation as Learning, a paradigm that formulates personalized evaluation as a learning problem rather than a static judgment. Under this paradigm, we propose PARL (Preference-Aware Rubric Learning for Personalized Evaluation), a framework that learns to induce preference-aware evaluation rubrics directly from raw user histories and performs a self-validation mechanism to ensure consistency with the user's preferences. PARL integrates rubric induction with a discriminative reinforcement learning objective that contrasts user-authored responses against competitive personalized model outputs, enabling the learned rubrics to capture precise, user-specific decision boundaries. Experiments on real-world personalized text generation tasks show that PARL consistently induces high-fidelity rubrics that reliably identify user-aligned responses and generalize across users and tasks, while capturing stable stylistic preferences and fine-grained evaluative patterns. To ensure reproducibility, our code is available at https://github.com/SnowCharmQ/PARL.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PARL, a framework that treats personalized evaluation of LLM outputs as a learning problem, inducing user-specific evaluation rubrics from interaction histories and contrasting user responses with model outputs to learn fine-grained, stable preferences.
AI.motivation: As LLMs shift toward user-centric agents, aligning them with individual user preferences becomes crucial. However, current evaluation methods cannot adequately capture subjective, user-specific preferences—especially those reflected in long-term interaction histories—creating a bottleneck for developing truly personalized systems. The authors aim to provide a more faithful, user-grounded way to evaluate how well models match an individual user’s style and preferences.
AI.method: The authors introduce the paradigm of Personalized Evaluation as Learning, where evaluation is treated as a learning task instead of static judging. They propose PARL (Preference-Aware Rubric Learning), which learns preference-aware evaluation rubrics directly from raw user histories. PARL identifies three guiding principles—Representativeness, User-Consistency, and Discriminativeness—and operationalizes them by (1) inducing rubrics from user-authored content, (2) applying a self-validation mechanism to ensure the rubrics are consistent with that user’s preferences, and (3) using a discriminative reinforcement learning objective that contrasts user-authored responses with strong personalized model outputs. This encourages the learned rubric to discover precise, user-specific decision boundaries. The learned rubrics are then used to evaluate future model outputs for that user.
AI.result: On real-world personalized text generation tasks, PARL consistently learns rubrics that align well with individual user preferences. These rubrics reliably distinguish user-aligned responses from misaligned ones, generalize across different users and tasks, and capture stable stylistic and fine-grained evaluative patterns. Empirical results indicate that PARL outperforms existing evaluation methods on personalization fidelity and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Personalized evaluation of LLMs should be approached as a learning problem that infers user-specific rubrics from interaction histories rather than as a static, one-off judgment. PARL implements this idea by learning preference-aware rubrics with built-in self-validation and a discriminative RL objective, achieving high-fidelity, generalizable personalized evaluation in practice. This paradigm can better support the development and assessment of user-centric LLM agents, and the open-sourced code facilitates reproduction and extension.

title: Semantic Triplet Restoration: A Novel Protocol for Hierarchical Table Understanding in Large Language Models
summary: Table question answering requires models to recover semantic relations encoded implicitly by two-dimensional layout, merged cells, and hierarchical headers. Current pipelines typically use HTML or Markdown as intermediate table representations, but these layout-oriented serializations introduce markup overhead and require large language models to infer header-cell alignments from row and column spans. We propose Semantic Triplet Restoration (STR), a protocol that rewrites each cell as an atomic fact <item path, feature path, value>, where the item path specifies the row-wise entity, the feature path specifies the hierarchical attribute, and the value contains the cell content. We also present TripletQL, a lightweight query-aware router that uses STR to select an appropriate rendering or filtered subset of triplets for each question. Across four Chinese and English table-QA benchmarks, STR matches or improves upon HTML-based baselines while reducing input tokens. The relative benefit grows for smaller language models and longer table contexts, suggesting that explicit semantic representations are especially useful under constrained inference budgets. Code and data are available at https://github.com/Phoenix-ni/STR.git .

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Semantic Triplet Restoration (STR), which converts table cells into atomic semantic triplets and uses a query-aware router (TripletQL) to improve table question answering while reducing token usage.
AI.motivation: Existing table QA systems rely on HTML/Markdown serializations that are layout-oriented, verbose, and force models to implicitly reconstruct semantic relations like header-cell alignment, spans, and hierarchies, which is inefficient especially for smaller LLMs and longer tables.
AI.method: The authors propose STR, a protocol that rewrites each table cell as a semantic triplet <item path, feature path, value>, where item path encodes the row-wise entity and feature path encodes hierarchical headers/attributes. They also design TripletQL, a lightweight query-aware routing module that, given a question, selects an appropriate rendering or a filtered subset of relevant triplets as input to the LLM.
AI.result: On four Chinese and English table QA benchmarks, STR achieves performance comparable to or better than HTML-based baselines while using fewer input tokens. The gains are more pronounced for smaller language models and for tables with longer contexts.
AI.conclusion: Making table semantics explicit through triplet-based representations and query-aware routing can improve the accuracy–efficiency tradeoff in table QA, particularly under constrained inference budgets (small models or long tables), demonstrating an advantage over traditional HTML/Markdown table serializations.

title: What Am I Missing? Question-Answering as Hidden State Probing
summary: Test-time reasoning has become a significant field of study since the introduction of chain-of-thought reasoning in large language models (LLMs). However, the mechanisms of this reasoning process are still under-explored -- from the same input prompt, and even the same partial solution, LLMs can produce varied answers if sampled multiple times. We propose to leverage question-asking as an inference-time intervention that articulates information about the model's hidden state. To achieve that, we present a student-teacher setting where a student asks questions to a teacher. We train a probe on the student's hidden state before and after asking a question and find it is predictive of the trajectory's final correctness, even before generating the teacher's answer. This suggests there is a meaningful signal from the self-diagnosis that occurs during question generation rather than information transfer from the teacher. We then frame question-asking as a sequential decision problem, using this probe as a quality score, and define a gating policy to ask questions that maximize likelihood of correctness. We find that the success of question-asking as an intervention is largely dependent on the model's self-consistency. Our empirical results show a gap between detection and recovery; while our gating policy captures model correctness and uncertainty, interventions are equally likely to harm correct trajectories as they are to recover incorrect ones. This gap between diagnosis and correction has broader implications on language models' capacity for self-refinement under uncertainty.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how asking questions at test time can reveal and potentially improve large language models’ reasoning, using a student-teacher setup and probes on hidden states.
AI.motivation: Despite progress in chain-of-thought reasoning, the underlying mechanisms of test-time reasoning in LLMs are not well understood, especially why multiple samplings from the same prompt can yield different outcomes. The authors aim to better understand and exploit the model’s internal uncertainty and self-diagnosis signals during reasoning, focusing on whether and how test-time question-asking can serve as an effective intervention.
AI.method: They set up a student–teacher framework where the student LLM can ask clarifying questions to a teacher LLM during inference. They train a probe on the student’s hidden states immediately before and after the question is generated, and use it to predict whether the final reasoning trajectory will be correct—critically, before seeing the teacher’s answer. They then cast question-asking as a sequential decision process: using the probe output as a quality score, they design a gating policy that decides when to ask questions to maximize final correctness, and evaluate this policy empirically under varying levels of model self-consistency.
AI.result: The probe applied to the student’s hidden states (before receiving any teacher response) accurately predicts the eventual correctness of the reasoning trajectory, showing that question-generation itself exposes self-diagnostic signals. Using the probe for gating, the system can detect when the model is likely to be correct or uncertain. However, interventions based on asking questions do not consistently improve performance: they are roughly as likely to degrade an otherwise correct reasoning path as they are to fix an incorrect one. The effectiveness of question-asking as an intervention is highly dependent on the underlying model’s self-consistency.
AI.conclusion: Question-asking at test time surfaces meaningful internal information about an LLM’s reasoning state and uncertainty, enabling accurate detection of likely success or failure via a probe. Yet there remains a pronounced gap between being able to diagnose errors and being able to correct them: even well-informed interventions may not yield net performance gains and can harm correct trajectories. This suggests fundamental limitations in current language models’ capacity for reliable self-refinement under uncertainty, and highlights the need for mechanisms that better align internal self-diagnosis with beneficial corrective actions.

title: Disagreeing Rationales: Rethinking Classification and Explainability Evaluation in Hate Speech Detection
summary: Human disagreement is ubiquitous and well-known in labeling. However, variation in explanations, captured through token-level human rationales, remains far less explored. At the same time, it is unclear how to best evaluate human labels and rationales -- or even how to best aggregate rationales beyond majority vote -- in light of this variation. Yet, rationales may provide additional insights into the richness of human reasoning, that may differ in style, values and interpretations -- especially in subjective NLP tasks like hate speech detection. In this work, we unify diverse models, training strategies, loss functions, and existing evaluation metrics under a single protocol by systematically re-implementing them across different label and rationale representation spaces. Classification metrics are organized around two key properties -- predictive and distributional -- while explainability metrics through three complementary dimensions: plausibility, faithfulness, and complexity. In this unified supervision framework, we evaluate model behavior across classification and explainability metrics, as well as metric sensitivity to the choice of label (hard and soft) and rationale representation space (hard, intermediate and soft). Results show that both hard and soft metrics favor softer representations, highlighting their effectiveness in capturing variation and the need to rethink evaluation in subjective NLP.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how variation in human explanations (token-level rationales) and labels should be evaluated and aggregated, especially for subjective NLP tasks like hate speech detection, and shows that softer representations of labels and rationales better capture this variation.
AI.motivation: While human disagreement in labels is well known, much less is understood about variation in human explanations (rationales) and how to evaluate or aggregate them. Existing evaluation practices (e.g., majority vote, single metric types) may fail to capture the richness and diversity of human reasoning, particularly in subjective tasks, so a more principled and unified framework is needed.
AI.method: The authors re-implement a broad set of models, training strategies, loss functions, and evaluation metrics under a single, unified protocol. They represent labels in hard vs. soft forms and rationales in hard, intermediate, and soft spaces. They then organize classification metrics into predictive vs. distributional properties and explainability metrics into plausibility, faithfulness, and complexity, and systematically compare model behavior and metric sensitivity across these representation spaces.
AI.result: Across this unified supervision and evaluation framework, both hard and soft evaluation metrics tend to favor softer label and rationale representations. These soft representations more effectively capture human variation in labels and explanations compared to hard or majority-vote style aggregation.
AI.conclusion: Soft representations of labels and token-level rationales are more suitable for modeling and evaluating subjective NLP tasks, as they better capture variation in human reasoning. The results suggest that common evaluation and aggregation practices should be reconsidered, advocating for a shift toward softer, distribution-aware supervision and metrics in tasks like hate speech detection.
